## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)** is a deep learning architecture, originally introduced by Long, Shelhamer, and Darrell (CVPR 2015), that transforms a standard classification Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) into a **pixel-wise dense predictor** by replacing the final fully-connected (dense) layers with convolutional layers. This enables the network to output a spatial segmentation map whose dimensions match the input image, allowing **end-to-end semantic segmentation** of medical images such as CT, MRI, X-ray, and histopathology slides.

Mathematically, an FCN is a parametric function $F_{\theta}: \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C_{in}} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C_{out}}$ that preserves spatial information from input to output, where $H$ and $W$ are the height and width of the image, $C_{in}$ is the number of input channels, and $C_{out}$ is the number of semantic classes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** FCNs are the foundational architecture for **semantic segmentation** in medical imaging. They are the prerequisite for understanding U-Net, DeepLab, and modern transformer-based segmentation networks.

## 1.2 Why FCNs Exist — The Problem They Solve

A standard classification CNN (e.g., VGG-16, ResNet) compresses an image into a **1D vector of class probabilities** through successive pooling operations, destroying spatial information. For medical tasks like tumor delineation, organ segmentation, or lesion quantification, we need a **2D label map** — one prediction per pixel — not a single global label. FCNs solve this problem by:

1. Removing the fully-connected layers.
2. Adding **upsampling layers** to restore the original spatial resolution.
3. Using **skip connections** to combine coarse, semantic features with fine, local features.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cartographer vs. Caption Writer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine two experts examining a brain MRI scan.
> - **Standard CNN (Caption Writer):** Looks at the whole image and writes a *single sentence* — "This is a glioma." It summarizes and forgets the geometry.
> - **Fully Convolutional Network (Cartographer):** Draws a *detailed map* of the brain, color-coding every pixel: blue for healthy tissue, red for tumor, green for edema. It preserves geometry.
> 
> The FCN is essentially a CNN that was retrained to be a **map-maker instead of a caption-writer**, producing a labeled map of the same size as the input image.

## 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU Examinations

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Dense Prediction** | Predicting a label for every pixel in the image, not just one global label. |
| **Encoder** | The downsampling path (VGG/ResNet backbone) that extracts semantic features. |
| **Decoder** | The upsampling path that restores spatial resolution. |
| **Skip Connection** | A pathway that merges feature maps from an earlier (high-resolution) layer with a later (low-resolution) layer. |
| **Deconvolution** | A learnable upsampling operation (also called transposed convolution). |
| **Heatmap / Score Map** | The per-pixel class probability output of the FCN. |
| **Strided Convolution** | Convolution with stride $> 1$ used to downsample feature maps. |
| **Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy** | The standard loss function for segmentation. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The FCN pipeline consists of the following sequential operations:

**Step 1 — Convolutional Backbone (Encoder)**
- An input image $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ is passed through stacked convolutions and pooling layers.
- Typical backbones: **VGG-16**, **ResNet-50**, or **DenseNet**.
- Pooling reduces spatial dimensions progressively. For a network with 5 pooling layers, the output feature map is $32\times$ smaller.

**Step 2 — Replacement of Fully-Connected Layers**
- The traditional FC layers (which flatten the feature map) are replaced with **$1 \times 1$ convolutions** that act as channel-wise projections.
- A $1 \times 1$ conv with $C_{out}$ filters produces a heatmap of shape $\frac{H}{32} \times \frac{W}{32} \times C_{out}$.

**Step 3 — Upsampling (Decoder)**
- The coarse heatmap is upsampled to the input resolution using either:
  - **Transposed convolution** (learnable, parametric upsampling)
  - **Bilinear interpolation** (fixed, non-parametric)
  - **Nearest-neighbor** upsampling

**Step 4 — Skip Connections (Refinement)**
- Feature maps from intermediate layers are fused with the upsampled output.
- Three standard FCN variants exist:
  - **FCN-32s** — single $32\times$ upsampling, no skip connections.
  - **FCN-16s** — combines the final layer with the `pool4` feature map.
  - **FCN-8s** — combines final, `pool4`, and `pool3` feature maps (most accurate).

**Step 5 — Pixel-wise Softmax + Loss Computation**
- A softmax is applied independently at every spatial location.
- Pixel-wise cross-entropy loss is computed against the ground-truth mask.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **exam-critical**. Memorize the dimensional formulas and their meaning.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_{out} = \lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \rfloor + 1$ | Standard convolution output size | Backbone feature map dimensions |
| 2 | $H_{out}^{up} = (H_{in} - 1) \cdot s - 2p + k$ | Transposed convolution output size | Decoder upsampling math |
| 3 | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$ | Softmax per pixel | Class probability map |
| 4 | $L_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$ | Pixel-wise cross-entropy | FCN training objective |
| 5 | $D(A,B) = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Dice coefficient | Medical segmentation metric |
| 6 | $IoU(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Intersection over Union (Jaccard) | Overlap metric |
| 7 | $L_{Dice} = 1 - \frac{2\sum p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum p_i + \sum g_i + \epsilon}$ | Dice loss (differentiable surrogate) | Class-imbalanced segmentation |
| 8 | $F_{skip} = W_{up} \cdot F_{coarse} + W_{fuse} \cdot F_{fine}$ | Skip-connection fusion | Combining multi-scale features |
| 9 | $\text{Receptive Field} = k + (k-1)(s-1)$ for sequential layers | Effective receptive field per layer | Context aggregation analysis |
| 10 | $\mathbf{Y}_{pred} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ | Output score map shape | Final network output definition |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radiology:** Automated organ and tumor segmentation in CT/MRI (kidney, liver, brain tumor).
- **Pathology:** Nuclei segmentation in H&E stained whole-slide images.
- **Ophthalmology:** Vessel segmentation in retinal fundus images for diabetic retinopathy screening.
- **Cardiology:** Myocardium segmentation in echocardiograms for ejection fraction estimation.
- **Surgical Planning:** 3D reconstruction of patient anatomy from segmented 2D slices.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Size of Transposed Convolution

The transposed convolution is the learnable upsampling operation that is the **inverse** of a strided convolution. Given an input feature map of size $H_{in}$, kernel size $k$, stride $s$, and padding $p$, the output dimension is derived as follows.

**Step 1:** Start with the forward convolution formula (the inverse operation we are reversing):

$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$

**Step 2:** We want to express $H_{in}$ in terms of $H_{out}$. Invert the relation by treating $H_{out}$ as the small map and $H_{in}$ as the upsampled output. In a transposed convolution, each input element contributes to a $k \times k$ window in the output, with stride $s$ between starting positions of consecutive elements.

**Step 3:** Place $H_{in}$ elements in the output, each producing $k$ new positions, separated by stride $s$. The total output size is:

$$H_{out}^{up} = (H_{in} - 1) \cdot s - 2p + k$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example:** Suppose $H_{in} = 7$, $k = 4$, $s = 2$, $p = 0$. Then:

$$H_{out}^{up} = (7 - 1) \cdot 2 - 2(0) + 4 = 12 + 4 = 16$$

**Step 5 — Verification:** A stride-2 transpose conv approximately doubles the input dimension. Indeed, $7 \times 2 = 14 \approx 16$ (the +2 comes from the kernel size exceeding the stride). This confirms the formula.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pixel-wise Softmax for Per-Class Probability

The output of the FCN is a 3D tensor $\mathbf{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$. We convert raw logits $z_{h,w,c}$ into probabilities using softmax, applied independently at each spatial pixel $(h, w)$.

**Step 1:** Define the unnormalized exponential for class $c$ at pixel $(h, w)$:

$$e^{z_{h,w,c}}$$

**Step 2:** Define the normalization constant (sum over all $C$ classes) at the same pixel:

$$S_{h,w} = \sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_{h,w,j}}$$

**Step 3:** Form the probability by division:

$$\hat{y}_{h,w,c} = \frac{e^{z_{h,w,c}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_{h,w,j}}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of Valid Probability Distribution:**
- Non-negativity: $\hat{y}_{h,w,c} > 0$ since exponentials are positive.
- Unit sum:

$$\sum_{c=1}^{C} \hat{y}_{h,w,c} = \frac{\sum_{c=1}^{C} e^{z_{h,w,c}}}{S_{h,w}} = \frac{S_{h,w}}{S_{h,w}} = 1$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy Loss

The loss is computed across all $N = H \times W$ pixels and all $C$ classes.

**Step 1:** For a single pixel $(h, w)$, the cross-entropy between predicted distribution $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ and one-hot ground truth $\mathbf{y}$ is:

$$\ell_{h,w} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{h,w,c} \log(\hat{y}_{h,w,c})$$

**Step 2:** Because the ground truth is one-hot encoded, only the term corresponding to the true class $c^{\star}$ survives:

$$\ell_{h,w} = -\log(\hat{y}_{h,w,c^{\star}})$$

**Step 3:** Average over all pixels:

$$L_{CE} = \frac{1}{H \cdot W} \sum_{h=1}^{H} \sum_{w=1}^{W} \ell_{h,w}$$

**Step 4 — Final compact form:**

$$L_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c}), \quad \text{where } N = H \cdot W$$

**Step 5 — Gradient (used in backpropagation):**

$$\frac{\partial L_{CE}}{\partial z_{i,c}} = \hat{y}_{i,c} - y_{i,c}$$

This elegant result tells us the gradient of the cross-entropy loss combined with softmax is simply the **prediction error** — a fact exploited by KTU examiners as a common question.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dice Coefficient as a Differentiable Loss

The Dice coefficient is preferred for medical segmentation due to severe class imbalance (e.g., a tumor occupies <5% of the image).

**Step 1:** Define the Dice similarity:

$$D = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

**Step 2:** Approximate set operations as pixel sums. Let $p_i \in [0,1]$ be the predicted probability and $g_i \in \{0,1\}$ be the ground truth at pixel $i$:

$$D = \frac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i g_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i}$$

**Step 3:** Convert into a loss (to be minimized):

$$L_{Dice} = 1 - D = 1 - \frac{2\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} g_i + \epsilon}$$

**Step 4:** The $\epsilon$ term (typically $10^{-6}$) prevents division by zero when both sets are empty. This smoothing term is **frequently asked** in KTU exams.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — FCN Variants Comparison

| Variant | Skip From | Upsampling Factor | mIoU (PASCAL VOC) | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FCN-32s | None | 32× | ~62.7% | Lowest |
| FCN-16s | pool4 | 32× → 2× → 16× | ~64.0% | Medium |
| FCN-8s | pool4 + pool3 | 32× → 4× → 8× | ~65.5% | Highest |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FCN-8s Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end data flow of an FCN-8s applied to a medical MRI scan, showing encoder stages, skip connections, and the decoder fusion path.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Medical Image HxWx3] --> B[Conv1 Stage Pool1 H/2xW/2]
    B --> C[Conv2 Stage Pool2 H/4xW/4]
    C --> D[Conv3 Stage Pool3 H/8xW/8]
    D --> E[Conv4 Stage Pool4 H/16xW/16]
    E --> F[Conv5 Stage Pool5 H/32xW/32]
    F --> G[1x1 Conv replaces FC layer H/32xW/32xC classes]
    G --> H[Transposed Conv 2x upsample H/16xW/16xC]
    E --> I[1x1 Conv on pool4 H/16xW/16xC]
    I --> J[Add Fusion at H/16xW/16xC]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Transposed Conv 2x upsample H/8xW/8xC]
    D --> L[1x1 Conv on pool3 H/8xW/8xC]
    L --> M[Add Fusion at H/8xW/8xC]
    K --> M
    M --> N[Transposed Conv 8x upsample HxWxC]
    N --> O[Pixelwise Softmax HxWxC]
    O --> P[Argmax Per Pixel Segmentation Map HxW]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style P fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#2ECC71
    style J fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F39C12
    style M fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F39C12
    style O fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#E74C3C
```

## 4.2 Skip Connection Mechanism — Conceptual Zoom-In

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Coarse Feature Map H32xW32xC] --> T1[Transposed Conv 2x]
    SK[Skip Feature Map H16xW16xC] --> C1[1x1 Conv Channel Match]
    T1 --> ADD[Elementwise Addition]
    C1 --> ADD
    ADD --> FUS[Fused Multi Scale Feature H16xW16xC]
    FUS --> T2[Transposed Conv 2x]
    T2 --> OUT[Refined Output H8xW8xC]

    style IN fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B
    style SK fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#2874A6
    style ADD fill:#F5B7B1,stroke:#922B21
    style FUS fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#239B56
    style OUT fill:#E8DAEF,stroke:#7D3C98
```

## 4.3 Detailed Transposed Convolution Operation (2× Upsampling)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Input Pixel Value x] --> I1[Place x at top left of 2x2 zero output]
    I1 --> M1[Multiply by 3x3 kernel and scatter to output]
    P2[Next Input Pixel x2 with stride 2] --> M1
    M1 --> O1[Overlapping outputs summed elementwise]
    O1 --> O2[Final Upsampled Feature Map 2x larger]

    style P1 fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#2874A6
    style P2 fill:#D6EAF8,stroke:#2874A6
    style M1 fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#B7950B
    style O2 fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#239B56
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a standard CNN and a Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) for medical image segmentation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Standard CNN | Fully Convolutional Network |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Single class label (1D vector) | Per-pixel class map (3D tensor $H \times W \times C$) |
| Final layers | Fully-connected / dense layers | $1 \times 1$ convolutional layers |
| Spatial info | Lost due to flattening | Preserved throughout |
| Use case | Image classification (e.g., pneumonia yes/no) | Semantic segmentation (e.g., tumor boundary) |
| Inference | One prediction per image | One prediction per pixel |

**(1 mark for CNN limitation, 1 mark for FCN solution, 1 mark for application context)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three upsampling techniques used in FCN decoders.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Nearest-Neighbor Interpolation (1 mark):** Replicates each pixel into a $k \times k$ block. Fast, parameter-free, but produces blocky outputs.
2. **Bilinear Interpolation (1 mark):** Computes each new pixel as a weighted average of the 4 nearest input pixels using linear weights in both directions. Smooth, non-parametric.
3. **Transposed Convolution / Deconvolution (1 mark):** A learnable convolution in reverse. Inserts zeros between input pixels, then applies a standard convolution with a learned kernel. Trainable, content-adaptive, but can produce checkerboard artifacts.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the architecture of a Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) for semantic segmentation. With a neat block diagram, describe FCN-32s, FCN-16s, and FCN-8s variants. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Fully Convolutional Network transforms a classification CNN into a pixel-wise predictor. The architecture consists of an **encoder** (downsampling), a **bottleneck** (replaced FC layers), and a **decoder** (upsampling) with optional skip connections.

- **FCN-32s:** The output of pool5 is $1/32$ the input size. A $1 \times 1$ conv converts channels to $C$ classes, and a single $32 \times$ transposed convolution restores the original size. No skip connections — suffers from coarse, blocky outputs. (2 marks)

- **FCN-16s:** The pool5 prediction is $2 \times$ upsampled (to $1/16$). Simultaneously, a $1 \times 1$ conv is applied to pool4 features. These two are **summed element-wise**, and the result is $16 \times$ upsampled. One skip connection — better boundary localization. (2 marks)

- **FCN-8s:** The FCN-16s output is $2 \times$ upsampled (to $1/8$) and added to a $1 \times 1$ conv of pool3 features. Final $8 \times$ upsampling produces the segmentation map. Two skip connections — finest detail recovery. (2 marks)

- **Diagram (1 mark):** Block diagram showing backbone → 1x1 conv → upsample → skip → fuse → final upsample → softmax.

**(b) With suitable equations, derive the pixel-wise cross-entropy loss used to train an FCN. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The pixel-wise cross-entropy is the negative log-likelihood of the true class under the predicted softmax distribution, averaged over all pixels.

**Step 1:** Apply softmax at each spatial location to get probabilities $\hat{y}_{h,w,c} \in [0,1]$ with $\sum_c \hat{y}_{h,w,c} = 1$. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Define the loss at a single pixel as:

$$\ell_{h,w} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{h,w,c} \log(\hat{y}_{h,w,c})$$

Since $y_{h,w,c}$ is one-hot, this reduces to $-\log(\hat{y}_{h,w,c^\star})$. (1 mark)

**Step 3:** Aggregate over all $N = H \cdot W$ pixels:

$$L_{CE} = \frac{1}{H \cdot W} \sum_{h=1}^{H} \sum_{w=1}^{W} \ell_{h,w}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4:** Compact form using index $i$ for pixel and $c$ for class:

$$L_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5:** Gradient with respect to logits $z_{i,c}$ (used in backpropagation):

$$\frac{\partial L_{CE}}{\partial z_{i,c}} = \hat{y}_{i,c} - y_{i,c}$$

Interpretation: gradient is the difference between prediction and truth. (1 mark)

**Step 6:** Discussion — when the predicted probability of the true class approaches 1, the loss approaches 0; if the model is confident and wrong, the loss explodes. This property makes CE sensitive to class imbalance, motivating the Dice loss in medical imaging. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the role of skip connections in Fully Convolutional Networks. How do they improve segmentation accuracy in medical images? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **skip connection** merges high-resolution features from an earlier encoder layer with the upsampled, semantically rich features from a later layer. This addresses two complementary needs:

- **Deep layers** capture *what* (semantic class) but have *lost where* (spatial precision) due to pooling.
- **Shallow layers** capture *where* (edges, textures) but lack *what* (semantic meaning).

**Step 1 — Bottleneck problem (2 marks):** After 5 pooling layers, the feature map is $32 \times$ smaller. Direct $32 \times$ upsampling produces coarse, pixelated boundaries unsuitable for clinical use (e.g., a 2 mm tumor boundary error is unacceptable).

**Step 2 — Skip fusion (2 marks):** By summing the upsampled prediction with a $1 \times 1$ conv projection of `pool4` (for FCN-16s) or `pool4 + pool3` (for FCN-8s), the network recovers fine spatial detail. Mathematically:

$$F_{fused} = \mathcal{U}(F_{coarse}) + W_{1 \times 1} \cdot F_{skip}$$

**Step 3 — Trade-off (1 mark):** More skip connections → finer detail but also more parameters and potential noise injection.

**Step 4 — Clinical relevance (2 marks):** In tumor segmentation, FCN-8s can delineate irregular boundaries; in retinal vessel segmentation, it captures thin 1-pixel-wide vessels that FCN-32s would miss.

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the transposed convolution operation used in FCN decoders. Derive the output dimension formula. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):** A transposed convolution is the *gradient* of a convolution. It inserts zeros between input pixels (stride factor) and applies a learned kernel to expand spatial size. It is also called *deconvolution* or *upconvolution*.

**Step 2 — Operation (2 marks):** Given input $H_{in}$, kernel $k$, stride $s$, padding $p$:
- Insert $s - 1$ zeros between each input element.
- Apply a normal convolution with stride 1, kernel size $k$, padding $p$.

**Step 3 — Diagram (1 mark):**

```
Input 3x3         Output 5x5
 1 2 3             1 0 2 0 3
 4 5 6    --->     0 0 0 0 0
 7 8 9             4 0 5 0 6
                   0 0 0 0 0
                   7 0 8 0 9
```

**Step 4 — Derivation (2 marks):** Starting from the standard convolution:

$$H_{out} = \left\lfloor \frac{H_{in} + 2p - k}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$$

Inverting with $H_{in} \to H_{in}^{up}$ and $H_{out} \to H_{in}$:

$$H_{out}^{up} = (H_{in} - 1) \cdot s - 2p + k$$

**Step 5 — Example:** $H_{in} = 8$, $k = 4$, $s = 2$, $p = 0$ gives $H_{out}^{up} = (8-1)\cdot 2 - 0 + 4 = 18$. The output is more than double due to kernel overlap. (Bonus 0 mark — included for clarity.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Confusing FCN with U-Net:** Students often write "FCN = U-Net." This is **wrong**. U-Net is a *specific improvement* over FCN with symmetric encoder-decoder and concatenation-based skip connections (not summation). KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for this.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the 1×1 convolution step:** A common error is to say "we just remove FC layers." The correct answer must explicitly mention the **$1 \times 1$ conv that replaces the FC layer** to act as a channel-wise classifier.
> 
> 3. **Transposed conv vs. inverse conv:** Students sometimes claim "transposed convolution is the exact inverse of convolution." It is **not** — it is the transpose of the convolution *matrix*, not a true inverse. This subtle point is asked in Part A.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the epsilon in Dice loss:** Omitting the $\epsilon$ smoothing term in Dice loss is a 1-mark deduction in derivation questions.
> 
> 5. **Wrong softmax axis:** Softmax in FCN is applied **across channels** at each spatial location, not across spatial locations. Conflating this is a frequent mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FCN Definition:** A CNN with FC layers replaced by $1 \times 1$ convolutions + upsampling → produces a per-pixel class map of the same size as the input.
- **Two Key Innovations:** (1) $1 \times 1$ conv replacing FC layers, (2) Transposed convolution for learnable upsampling.
- **Three Variants:** FCN-32s (no skip), FCN-16s (1 skip from pool4), FCN-8s (2 skips from pool4 and pool3) — finer variants give better boundary accuracy.
- **Skip Connections Fuse:** Coarse semantic features (deep) + fine spatial features (shallow) via element-wise addition.
- **Loss Function:** Pixel-wise cross-entropy (default) or Dice loss (preferred for class-imbalanced medical data).
- **Output Tensor Shape:** $H \times W \times C$, where $C$ is the number of tissue/organ/lesion classes.
- **Receptive Field:** FCNs have *large* receptive fields in deep layers, giving strong contextual understanding — critical for differentiating similar-looking pathologies.
- **Transposed Conv Math:** $H_{out}^{up} = (H_{in} - 1)\cdot s - 2p + k$.
- **Softmax + CE Gradient Trick:** $\frac{\partial L_{CE}}{\partial z} = \hat{y} - y$ — memorize for derivations.
- **Dice Coefficient:** $D = \frac{2\sum p_i g_i}{\sum p_i + \sum g_i}$ — range $[0,1]$, 1 = perfect overlap.
- **Limitations to Know:** (i) FCN-8s still has checkerboard artifacts from transposed convolutions, (ii) it is slow at inference, (iii) it struggles with 3D volumetric data — motivating 3D U-Net and V-Net.
- **Successor Architectures:** U-Net, SegNet, DeepLab (atrous convolution), and Mask R-CNN all build upon FCN principles.
- **Medical Use Cases:** Brain tumor segmentation (BraTS challenge), lung nodule detection (LIDC), cell nucleus segmentation (MoNuSAC), cardiac MRI (ACDC challenge).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: U-Net
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT865 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** U-Net

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**U-Net** is a *convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture* specifically engineered for **semantic segmentation** of biomedical images. Introduced by Ronneberger et al. (2015) at the University of Freiburg, it is characterised by a symmetric **"U" shaped topology** consisting of two main pathways:

- **Contracting Path (Encoder):** A traditional CNN stack that captures *context* through successive convolutions and pooling operations.
- **Expansive Path (Decoder):** A symmetric up-sampling pathway that enables *precise localisation* through transposed convolutions.
- **Skip Connections:** Concatenation layers that fuse high-resolution features from the encoder with the up-sampled features of the decoder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** U-Net is the foundational architecture for **pixel-wise classification** in medical imaging tasks such as tumour boundary delineation, cell segmentation, organ segmentation, and lesion detection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Microscopic Surgeon**
> Imagine a pathologist examining a tissue slide under a microscope:
> 1. She first **zooms out** to grasp the *overall tissue context* (analogous to the **Encoder** capturing global features).
> 2. Then she **zooms in** to *precisely trace the boundary* of a cancer cell (analogous to the **Decoder** reconstructing spatial detail).
> 3. Crucially, she keeps referring back to the *original high-resolution view* to ensure the boundary is accurate (analogous to **Skip Connections** carrying fine-grained detail forward).

This "see-broadly, then zoom-in-with-context" workflow is the essence of U-Net.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Input Tile Size:** Standard **572 × 572 × 1** (grayscale) or **572 × 572 × 3** (RGB) image patches.
- **Output Segmentation Map:** **388 × 388 × C**, where **C** is the number of classes.
- **Overlap-Tile Strategy:** Borders are predicted by mirroring the input context — crucial when the image is larger than the network's input tile.
- **Loss Function:** **Weighted Pixel-wise Cross-Entropy** (used to handle class imbalance between cells and background).
- **Training Epochs (original paper):** ~**140,000 iterations**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown

The U-Net architecture has **23 convolutional layers** in its original formulation, and the structure follows a strict symmetry rule: *every pooling operation in the encoder is mirrored by an up-sampling operation in the decoder.*

#### 2.1.1 Contracting Path (Left Arm)
- Each block contains **two 3 × 3 unpadded convolutions**, each followed by a **ReLU activation** and a **2 × 2 max-pooling** with stride 2 for downsampling.
- The number of feature channels **doubles** at every downsampling step: 64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 1024.
- Mathematical intent: increase the **receptive field** to capture broader contextual information.

#### 2.1.2 Bottleneck
- The deepest layer contains **1024 channels** at the lowest spatial resolution.
- It is the most *semantically rich* but *spatially coarsest* representation.

#### 2.1.3 Expansive Path (Right Arm)
- Each block begins with a **2 × 2 transposed convolution (up-convolution)** that halves the number of feature channels while doubling spatial resolution: 1024 → 512 → 256 → 128 → 64.
- Followed by concatenation with the correspondingly cropped encoder feature map, then **two 3 × 3 convolutions** with ReLU.

#### 2.1.4 Skip Connections
- Concatenation (not addition) along the channel dimension fuses encoder features with decoder features.
- This compensates for spatial information lost during pooling.
- Cropping is required because every convolution is *unpadded* (valid convolution), causing feature maps to shrink by 2 pixels per layer.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why U-Net Works So Well for Medical Imaging:**
> 1. **Limited Data:** Medical datasets are small; U-Net's symmetric design means the decoder up-samples from the bottleneck, allowing accurate segmentation even with few training samples.
> 2. **Class Imbalance:** Lesions often occupy < 5% of pixels; the weighted loss function compensates.
> 3. **Precise Boundaries:** Skip connections preserve edge information that pure encoder-decoder structures (like SegNet without skip links) lose.
> 4. **Any-Size Input:** With the overlap-tile strategy, arbitrarily large histopathology slides can be processed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the essential formulas a student **must memorise** for KTU University Examinations on U-Net.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Convolution Output Size | $\displaystyle W_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{W_{\text{in}} - F + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | Output dimension after convolution | pixels |
| Transposed Conv. Output | $\displaystyle W_{\text{out}} = (W_{\text{in}} - 1) \cdot S - 2P + F$ | Upsampled output size | pixels |
| Receptive Field Growth | $\displaystyle r_{\ell} = r_{\ell-1} + (F - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{\ell-1} S_i$ | Effective receptive field at layer $\ell$ | pixels |
| Receptive Field (2-pool) | $\displaystyle r_n = 2^{n+1} - 1$ | After $n$ pooling ops with $F=3$ | pixels |
| Pixel-wise Softmax | $\displaystyle p_k(x) = \frac{\exp(a_k(x))}{\sum_{k'=1}^{K} \exp(a_{k'}(x))}$ | Class probability at pixel $x$ | dimensionless |
| Weighted Cross-Entropy | $\displaystyle E = \sum_{x \in \Omega} w(x) \cdot \log \left( p_{\ell(x)}(x) \right)$ | Total loss over image domain | nats |
| Weight Map | $\displaystyle w(x) = w_c(x) + w_0 \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{(d_1(x) + d_2(x))^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)$ | Class-balancing + boundary emphasis | dimensionless |
| Dice Coefficient | $\displaystyle \text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Segmentation overlap metric | dimensionless [0,1] |
| Intersection over Union | $\displaystyle \text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Standard segmentation metric | dimensionless [0,1] |
| Number of Parameters (Conv) | $\displaystyle N_p = F^2 \cdot C_{\text{in}} \cdot C_{\text{out}} + C_{\text{out}}$ | Weights + biases of a conv layer | scalars |

Where:
- $W_{\text{in}}, W_{\text{out}}$ = input/output spatial size, $F$ = filter size, $P$ = padding, $S$ = stride.
- $K$ = number of classes, $a_k(x)$ = activation for class $k$ at pixel $x$.
- $d_1(x), d_2(x)$ = distances to nearest and second-nearest cell boundaries.
- $A, B$ = predicted and ground-truth segmentation masks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Receptive Field in U-Net (Analytical Proof)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Derivation:** Show that after $n$ downsampling blocks, the receptive field grows as $r_n = 2^{n+1} - 1$.

**Step 1 — Base case.**
For a single $3 \times 3$ convolution with stride 1, the receptive field is simply:
$$r_0 = 3$$
There is **one** convolution, so $n = 0$ conv-pool units, giving $r_0 = 2^{0+1} - 1 = 3 - 1 = 1$? Let us define carefully. The U-Net block has *two* convolutions *before* a $2 \times 2$ pool. After the first block ($n=1$):

**Step 2 — After block 1.**
Two $3 \times 3$ convs followed by a $2 \times 2$ pool with stride 2:
$$r_1 = 2(3) - 1 = 5$$
Check: $2^{1+1} - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3$ (using compact formula from the paper's block definition). Using the standard U-Net convention of *2 convs per block before pooling*:
$$r_n = (2^{n+1} - 1) \cdot \text{(expansion factor per block)}$$

**Step 3 — Inductive step.**
Assume after block $n$, the receptive field is $r_n$. Block $n+1$ consists of two more $3 \times 3$ convolutions (each adds 2 to the receptive field, since stride 1) followed by a $2 \times 2$ pool (which doubles the stride contribution of all prior layers):

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{n+1} &= 2 \cdot r_n + 2 \cdot (3 - 1) \\
&= 2 \cdot r_n + 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the recurrence.**
The recurrence $r_{n+1} = 2 r_n + 4$ with $r_0 = 3$ is a first-order linear recurrence. The homogeneous solution is $r_n^{(h)} = A \cdot 2^n$. The particular solution is a constant $r^* = -4$ (substituting: $-4 = -8 + 4 \Rightarrow 0 = 0$ ✓):

$$\begin{aligned}
r_n &= A \cdot 2^n - 4 \\
r_0 = 3 \implies A &= 7
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$r_n = 7 \cdot 2^n - 4$$

**Step 5 — Verification.**
- $n = 0$: $r_0 = 7 - 4 = 3$ ✓
- $n = 1$: $r_1 = 14 - 4 = 10$ (after block 1: two 3×3 convs and one 2×2 pool → receptive field = 10) ✓
- $n = 4$ (bottleneck of standard U-Net): $r_4 = 7 \cdot 16 - 4 = 108$ pixels.

This exponential growth is the **mathematical reason** the encoder captures *global context* — at the bottleneck, a single activation "sees" ~108 pixels of the original image.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Weighted Cross-Entropy Loss for Class Imbalance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Derivation:** Show the derivation of the per-pixel weight $w(x)$ that compensates for class imbalance and emphasises inter-cell boundaries.

**Step 1 — Standard cross-entropy.**
For a multi-class segmentation with $K$ classes, the cross-entropy at pixel $x$ with true label $\ell(x)$ is:
$$E(x) = - \log \left( p_{\ell(x)}(x) \right) = - a_{\ell(x)}(x) + \log \sum_{k=1}^{K} \exp(a_k(x))$$

**Step 2 — Introduce per-pixel weight $w(x)$.**
The total energy becomes:
$$E = \sum_{x \in \Omega} w(x) \cdot E(x) = - \sum_{x \in \Omega} w(x) \cdot \log \left( p_{\ell(x)}(x) \right)$$

**Step 3 — Decompose $w(x)$ into two parts.**
The weight is the sum of a *class-frequency* term and a *boundary-distance* term:
$$w(x) = w_c(x) + w_0 \cdot \exp \left( -\frac{(d_1(x) + d_2(x))^2}{2 \sigma^2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Justify the boundary term.**
Consider a pixel $x$ *very close* to a cell boundary, i.e. $d_1(x) \to 0$ and $d_2(x)$ small. The exponential term approaches:
$$\exp\left( -\frac{d_1^2}{2\sigma^2} \right) \approx 1 - \frac{d_1^2}{2\sigma^2} \approx 1 \quad (\text{when } d_1 \ll \sigma)$$
Hence the weight is large, **penalising misclassifications near boundaries heavily**. Conversely, for a pixel far from any boundary ($d_1 \to \infty$):
$$\exp\left( -\frac{d_1^2}{2\sigma^2} \right) \to 0$$
The weight reduces to the class-frequency term $w_c(x)$.

**Step 5 — Final form.**
$$\boxed{\;E = \sum_{x \in \Omega} \left[ w_c(x) + w_0 \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{(d_1(x) + d_2(x))^2}{2 \sigma^2} \right) \right] \cdot \log \left( p_{\ell(x)}(x) \right)\;}$$

This is the **exact loss function** used in the original U-Net paper for the ISBI 2015 cell-tracking challenge.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Feature-Map Size Through the Network

Given a **572 × 572 × 1** input, trace the encoder feature map dimensions.

| **Stage** | **Operation** | **Output Size (W × H × C)** | **Receptive Field** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input | — | $572 \times 572 \times 1$ | 1 |
| Conv 1a | $3 \times 3$, no pad | $570 \times 570 \times 64$ | 3 |
| Conv 1b | $3 \times 3$, no pad | $568 \times 568 \times 64$ | 5 |
| Pool 1 | $2 \times 2$, stride 2 | $284 \times 284 \times 64$ | 6 |
| Conv 2a/b | Two $3 \times 3$ | $280 \times 280 \times 128$ | 14 |
| Pool 2 | $2 \times 2$ | $140 \times 140 \times 128$ | 16 |
| Conv 3a/b | Two $3 \times 3$ | $136 \times 136 \times 256$ | 40 |
| Pool 3 | $2 \times 2$ | $68 \times 68 \times 256$ | 44 |
| Conv 4a/b | Two $3 \times 3$ | $64 \times 64 \times 512$ | 104 |
| Pool 4 | $2 \times 2$ | $32 \times 32 \times 512$ | 112 |
| Bottleneck | Two $3 \times 3$ | $28 \times 28 \times 1024$ | 280 |

This shows the **spatial shrinkage** ($572 \to 28$) compensated by **channel growth** ($1 \to 1024$), and is a standard KTU exam question type.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 U-Net Block-Level Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INPUT 572x572x1"] --> B["CONV 3x3 + ReLU x2, 64 ch"]
    B --> C["MAXPOOL 2x2 stride 2"]
    C --> D["CONV 3x3 + ReLU x2, 128 ch"]
    D --> E["MAXPOOL 2x2 stride 2"]
    E --> F["CONV 3x3 + ReLU x2, 256 ch"]
    F --> G["MAXPOOL 2x2 stride 2"]
    G --> H["CONV 3x3 + ReLU x2, 512 ch"]
    H --> I["MAXPOOL 2x2 stride 2"]
    I --> J["BOTTLENECK CONV 3x3 + ReLU x2, 1024 ch"]
    J --> K["UPSAMPLE 2x2 transposed conv, 512 ch"]
    K --> L["CONCAT with encoder h568x568x64"]
    L --> M["CONV 3x3 + ReLU x2, 512 ch"]
    M --> N["UPSAMPLE 2x2 transposed conv, 256 ch"]
    N --> O["CONCAT with encoder 136x136x256"]
    O --> P["CONV 3x3 + ReLU x2, 256 ch"]
    P --> Q["UPSAMPLE 2x2 transposed conv, 128 ch"]
    Q --> R["CONCAT with encoder 280x280x128"]
    R --> S["CONV 3x3 + ReLU x2, 128 ch"]
    S --> T["UPSAMPLE 2x2 transposed conv, 64 ch"]
    T --> U["CONCAT with encoder 568x568x64"]
    U --> V["CONV 3x3 + ReLU x2, 64 ch"]
    V --> W["1x1 CONV, K classes"]
    W --> X["SOFTMAX pixelwise"]
    X --> Y["SEGMENTATION MAP 388x388xK"]
```

### 4.2 Skip Connection Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["Encoder Feature Map"] --> S1{"CROP to decoder size"}
    S1 --> CAT["CONCATENATE channel-wise"]
    D1["Decoder Up-sampled Map"] --> CAT
    CAT --> CB["Two 3x3 Convs + ReLU"]
    CB --> OUT["Output of decoder block"]
```

### 4.3 Loss Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IMG["Ground Truth Mask"] --> DIST1["Compute distance transform d1"]
    IMG --> DIST2["Compute distance transform d2"]
    DIST1 --> WMAP["Weight map w of x"]
    DIST2 --> WMAP
    PRED["Predicted softmax p k of x"] --> CE["Pixel cross-entropy"]
    WMAP --> WCE["Weighted cross-entropy E"]
    CE --> WCE
    WCE --> BACK["Backpropagation"]
    BACK --> UPD["Update weights theta"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain the three main components of the U-Net architecture. Why is the architecture called "U-Net"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Contracting Path (Encoder)** — Captures context via successive $3\times3$ convolutions and $2\times2$ max-pooling, doubling channels at each step. *(1 mark)*
2. **Expansive Path (Decoder)** — Recovers spatial resolution via $2\times2$ transposed convolutions, halving channels at each step. *(1 mark)*
3. **Skip Connections** — Concatenate cropped encoder feature maps with corresponding decoder feature maps to fuse high-resolution detail with deep semantic features. *(1 mark)*

The name **"U-Net"** comes from the symmetric U-shape formed when the contracting path is plotted on the left and the expansive path on the right, with skip connections forming the horizontal bar of the "U".

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of the "overlap-tile strategy" in U-Net, and why is it important in medical image analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The overlap-tile strategy allows U-Net to process **arbitrarily large input images** (e.g., whole-slide histopathology images) by dividing them into overlapping tiles of fixed size. *(1 mark)*
- The context around each tile is mirrored/padded to provide sufficient border context for accurate segmentation, since U-Net uses *unpadded* (valid) convolutions that shrink feature maps. *(1 mark)*
- This is critical in medical imaging because whole-slide images can be **gigapixel-scale**, far larger than GPU memory allows in a single forward pass, yet accurate per-pixel classification is required. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the receptive field of the U-Net encoder after $n$ downsampling blocks, and compute its value at the bottleneck of a 4-block U-Net. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the weighted cross-entropy loss function used in U-Net, including the formula and the role of the weight map $w(x)$ in handling class imbalance and emphasising inter-cell boundaries. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Receptive Field Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Define a U-Net block as **two $3\times3$ convolutions** (stride 1) followed by a **$2\times2$ max-pool** (stride 2). *(1 mark)*

Step 2: After one conv, receptive field is 3. After two convs in the same block: $r = 2(3) - 1 = 5$. After the pool, the stride of all prior layers doubles. *(1 mark)*

Step 3: Write the recurrence relation. Let $r_n$ be the receptive field after $n$ complete blocks (conv-conv-pool):

$$\begin{aligned}
r_0 &= 3 \\
r_{n+1} &= 2 r_n + 4
\end{aligned}$$

*(1 mark)*

Step 4: Solve the recurrence.

Homogeneous solution: $r_n^{(h)} = A \cdot 2^n$.
Particular solution (constant ansatz $r^* = c$): $c = 2c + 4 \Rightarrow c = -4$.
General solution: $r_n = A \cdot 2^n - 4$. *(1 mark)*

Step 5: Apply initial condition $r_0 = 3$:
$$3 = A - 4 \implies A = 7$$
$$\boxed{\,r_n = 7 \cdot 2^n - 4\,}$$
*(1 mark)*

Step 6: Evaluate at the bottleneck of a 4-block U-Net ($n = 4$):
$$r_4 = 7 \cdot 16 - 4 = 112 - 4 = 108 \text{ pixels}$$
*(1 mark)*

Step 7: Interpret — at the bottleneck, each activation "sees" **108 × 108 pixels** of the original 572 × 572 input, enabling truly global context encoding. *(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — Weighted Cross-Entropy Loss (7 marks):**

Step 1: Standard cross-entropy loss summed over the image domain $\Omega$:
$$E = - \sum_{x \in \Omega} \log p_{\ell(x)}(x)$$
where $p_k(x) = \frac{\exp(a_k(x))}{\sum_{k'} \exp(a_{k'}(x))}$. *(1 mark)*

Step 2: Introduce the per-pixel weight $w(x)$ to handle two issues:
- **Class imbalance** (e.g., cells occupy < 5% of pixels) → term $w_c(x)$.
- **Boundary precision** (misclassification near cell borders is worse than far from them) → exponential distance term. *(1 mark)*

Step 3: The full loss becomes:
$$E = \sum_{x \in \Omega} w(x) \cdot \log p_{\ell(x)}(x)$$
with
$$w(x) = w_c(x) + w_0 \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{(d_1(x) + d_2(x))^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$
*(2 marks)*

Step 4: **Interpretation of terms:**
- $w_c(x)$ — a pre-computed class-frequency balancing weight; higher for the minority class.
- $d_1(x), d_2(x)$ — distances from pixel $x$ to the *nearest* and *second-nearest* cell boundary, respectively.
- $w_0, \sigma$ — hyperparameters (original paper: $w_0 = 10$, $\sigma \approx 5$ pixels).
- The exponential term $\to 1$ near boundaries (heavy penalty) and $\to 0$ far from boundaries. *(1 mark)*

Step 5: **Why it matters:** Without this weighting, the network would predict "background" everywhere and still achieve > 95% pixel accuracy, achieving **zero useful segmentation**. The weighted loss forces the network to focus on the *rare* foreground class and on *thin boundaries*. *(1 mark)*

Step 6: **Empirical result** (cite original paper): U-Net achieved a **warping error of 0.000352** on the ISBI 2015 cell-tracking challenge, **beating the prior best by a large margin**, thanks in part to this weighted loss. *(1 mark)*

---

#### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of U-Net. Clearly label the encoder, decoder, bottleneck, and skip connections. (7 marks)**

**(b) Consider a U-Net input of size $256 \times 256 \times 1$. Each encoder block has two $3 \times 3$ valid convolutions followed by $2 \times 2$ max-pooling with stride 2. There are 4 encoder blocks. Compute the size of the feature map at the bottleneck, and calculate the number of trainable parameters of the second encoder block. Assume the channel widths follow: 32, 64, 128, 256, 512. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — U-Net Architecture Diagram (7 marks):**

The U-Net consists of the following:

1. **Contracting Path (Encoder):** Four blocks, each with two $3\times3$ conv + ReLU and a $2\times2$ max-pool. Channels: $32 \to 64 \to 128 \to 256$. *(2 marks)*

2. **Bottleneck:** Two $3\times3$ conv + ReLU at $512$ channels, lowest spatial resolution. *(1 mark)*

3. **Expansive Path (Decoder):** Four blocks, each starting with a $2\times2$ transposed conv that upsamples spatially and halves channels, followed by concatenation with the corresponding encoder feature map (cropped to size), then two $3\times3$ conv + ReLU. Channels: $256 \to 128 \to 64 \to 32$. *(2 marks)*

4. **Skip Connections:** Horizontal arrows that concatenate the encoder feature maps with the corresponding decoder feature maps along the channel axis. They preserve high-resolution spatial information lost during pooling. *(1 mark)*

5. **Final layer:** $1\times1$ convolution maps the 32-channel decoder output to $K$ class scores, followed by pixel-wise softmax. *(1 mark)*

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 above for the full schematic.

---

**Part (b) — Size & Parameter Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Feature-map size at the bottleneck.**

Starting from $256 \times 256$, each encoder block applies 2 valid $3 \times 3$ convolutions (shrinking by 2 each, i.e. $-4$ per block) and one $2 \times 2$ pool (halving):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{After block 1:} \quad & \frac{(256 - 4)}{2} = 126 \text{ pixels} \\
\text{After block 2:} \quad & \frac{(126 - 4)}{2} = 61 \text{ pixels} \\
\text{After block 3:} \quad & \frac{(61 - 4)}{2} = 28 \text{ pixels (rounded down)} \\
\text{After block 4:} \quad & \frac{(28 - 4)}{2} = 12 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}$$

Then the bottleneck has 2 more $3 \times 3$ valid convolutions (no pool):
$$12 - 2 - 2 = 8$$

Therefore the **bottleneck feature map size is $8 \times 8 \times 512$**. *(3 marks)*

**Step 2: Parameter count of the second encoder block.**

The second block takes the output of the first pool ($128 \times 128 \times 32$ input) and applies two $3 \times 3$ convolutions.

- **Conv 2a:** input $C_{\text{in}} = 32$, output $C_{\text{out}} = 64$, filter $3 \times 3$.
$$N_{2a} = (3 \times 3) \times 32 \times 64 + 64 = 9 \cdot 32 \cdot 64 + 64 = 18{,}432 + 64 = 18{,}496$$

- **Conv 2b:** input $C_{\text{in}} = 64$, output $C_{\text{out}} = 64$, filter $3 \times 3$.
$$N_{2b} = 9 \times 64 \times 64 + 64 = 36{,}864 + 64 = 36{,}928$$

- **Total for block 2:**
$$N_{\text{block 2}} = 18{,}496 + 36{,}928 = 55{,}424 \text{ parameters}$$
*(4 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "valid" vs "same" convolution:** U-Net uses *unpadded* (valid) convolutions. Students who assume padding = "same" will get the bottleneck size wrong and lose **2-3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the bias term:** When counting parameters, the bias $+C_{\text{out}}$ per layer is mandatory. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Mis-stating skip connection operation:** It is **concatenation** along the channel axis, *not element-wise addition* (which is ResNet). Confusing these costs a full mark.
> 4. **Drawing the U-Net upside down:** A surprisingly common error — the encoder is on the *left*, decoder on the *right*, bottleneck at the *bottom*. Marks are deducted for inverted diagrams.
> 5. **Skipping the "why" of skip connections:** Examiners award marks for *justification*, not just naming. Always state that skip connections *recover spatial detail lost during pooling*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for U-Net (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [ ] U-Net = **Encoder + Decoder + Skip Connections**, named for its U-shape.
- [ ] Original paper: **Ronneberger et al. (2015)**, University of Freiburg, for ISBI cell-tracking.
- [ ] **Encoder:** Two $3\times3$ valid conv + ReLU, then $2\times2$ max-pool; channels **double** at every step.
- [ ] **Decoder:** $2\times2$ transposed conv halves channels and doubles spatial resolution; followed by **concatenation** with cropped encoder feature map; then two $3\times3$ conv + ReLU.
- [ ] **Skip connections are CONCATENATION, not addition.**
- [ ] **Bottleneck** has 1024 channels (original) and the largest receptive field.
- [ ] **Final layer:** $1\times1$ conv + pixel-wise softmax → $K$ class probability map.
- [ ] **Receptive field** after $n$ blocks: $r_n = 7 \cdot 2^n - 4$; at $n=4$, $r_4 = 108$ pixels.
- [ ] **Loss function:** Weighted cross-entropy with weight map $w(x) = w_c(x) + w_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{(d_1+d_2)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$.
- [ ] **Overlap-tile strategy** enables inference on arbitrarily large whole-slide images.
- [ ] **Validation metrics:** Dice coefficient and IoU (Jaccard index) for evaluating segmentation quality.
- [ ] **Why U-Net excels at medical imaging:** Limited data, severe class imbalance, need for pixel-perfect boundaries, support for any-size inputs.
- [ ] **Variants to know:** Attention U-Net, U-Net++, V-Net (3D U-Net), ResU-Net — these are common follow-up exam questions.
- [ ] **Number of conv layers in original U-Net:** 23.
- [ ] **Two fundamental innovations:** (1) Symmetric U-shape with skip connections, (2) Weighted loss for class-imbalanced data.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Image Registration: Clinical Applications of Image Registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT865 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Image Registration: Clinical Applications of Image Registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Image Registration** is the process of spatially aligning two or more images of the same scene or object, acquired at different times, from different viewpoints, or by different sensors (modalities), into a single, geometrically consistent coordinate system. In the clinical context, image registration establishes a point-to-point spatial correspondence between a **Fixed Image (Reference Image, $I_F$)** and a **Moving Image (Source Image, $I_M$)** by computing an optimal spatial transformation $T$ that maps points in $I_M$ onto their corresponding locations in $I_F$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Block (High-Yield):** Image registration is defined as the determination of a one-to-one mapping $T: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$ such that for every point $x$ in the fixed image, the transformed moving image $I_M(T(x))$ is spatially aligned with $I_F(x)$. The optimal transformation $T^*$ is the one that minimizes a chosen similarity (or cost) function $S$ subject to a chosen transformation model $\mathcal{T}$.

Formally, the registration problem is posed as an optimization problem:

$$
T^{*} = \arg\min_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \; \mathcal{S}\big(I_F(x),\; I_M(T(x))\big)
$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ is the similarity (or dissimilarity) measure, $T$ belongs to a chosen transformation space $\mathcal{T}$, and $x \in \Omega$ denotes a spatial coordinate in the image domain $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ (with $n = 2$ for 2D and $n = 3$ for 3D medical volumes).

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Image Registration as "Overlapping Two Maps of the Same City"**

Imagine you have two printed maps of a city: **Map A** is a 1950s road map, and **Map B** is a modern satellite map. They are both maps of the same city, but they are scaled differently, oriented differently, and some roads have changed names. To compare them meaningfully — for example, to find which modern buildings occupy the locations of 1950s landmarks — you must **align** them.

Image registration does exactly this for medical images:

- The **Fixed Image** = the "anchor" map (e.g., a pre-operative MRI scan of the brain).
- The **Moving Image** = the "transformable" map (e.g., an intra-operative ultrasound of the same brain, possibly rotated and deformed).
- The **Transformation $T$** = the set of stretching, rotating, and shifting operations needed to overlay the moving map perfectly onto the fixed map.
- The **Similarity Metric $\mathcal{S}$** = a "matching score" that tells you how well the two maps overlap (a smaller score = a better match).

Once the two maps are aligned, you can extract insights that neither map could provide alone — for example, locating a tumor visible on the MRI but not on the ultrasound, or measuring how the brain has shifted during surgery.

## 1.3 Key Terminology — KTU Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Fixed Image $I_F$** | The reference image that remains stationary; serves as the target of alignment. |
| **Moving Image $I_M$** | The image that is geometrically transformed to align with the fixed image. |
| **Transformation $T$** | A spatial mapping (rigid, affine, deformable) that warps $I_M$ toward $I_F$. |
| **Similarity Metric $\mathcal{S}$** | A scalar function quantifying the quality of alignment. |
| **Optimizer** | The algorithm that searches for the transformation minimizing (or maximizing) $\mathcal{S}$. |
| **Interpolator** | A function estimating intensity values at non-grid positions after warping (e.g., bilinear, B-spline). |
| **Multimodal Registration** | Registration between images from different modalities (e.g., CT and MRI). |
| **Mono-modal Registration** | Registration between images from the same modality (e.g., MRI-to-MRI). |
| **Intra-patient Registration** | Registration of images from the same patient (e.g., pre-op vs. post-op). |
| **Inter-patient Registration** | Registration of images from different patients (e.g., for atlas-based analysis). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Image Registration Matters in Clinical AI:** Nearly every modern clinical AI pipeline — tumor segmentation, radiation therapy planning, longitudinal monitoring, image-guided surgery, atlas-based labeling — depends on robust image registration as a critical preprocessing step. Understanding it is essential for designing end-to-end medical image analysis systems.

---

## 1.4 High-Level Categorization of Registration

Registration methods are commonly classified along four independent axes:

1. **Dimensionality:** 2D/2D, 3D/3D, 2D/3D (e.g., a 2D X-ray to a 3D CT volume).
2. **Nature of the Transformation:**
   - **Rigid** (6 DOF: 3 translations + 3 rotations)
   - **Affine** (12 DOF: rigid + 6 scales/skews)
   - **Deformable / Non-rigid** (thousands of DOF, e.g., B-spline free-form deformations, optical flow)
3. **Image Modality:**
   - **Mono-modal** (same modality)
   - **Multi-modal** (different modalities)
4. **Interaction:**
   - **Automatic** (no human input)
   - **Semi-automatic** (initial alignment or landmarks from the user)
   - **Interactive** (real-time user steering)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Spotlight:** Module 3 focuses on *Image Registration* as a foundational topic. The clinical applications discussed in this section will lay the groundwork for understanding the more advanced AI-based registration methods (e.g., learning-based registration with CNNs, VoxelMorph) covered later in the syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Decomposition of the Registration Pipeline

Every classical registration algorithm, regardless of modality or application, contains **four logical building blocks**. Understanding these components is critical for both KTU exam answers and practical implementation.

### 2.1.1 Block 1 — Transformation Model $\mathcal{T}$

The transformation model defines the *space* of allowable spatial warps. It encodes a prior assumption about the geometric relationship between $I_F$ and $I_M$.

**A. Rigid Transformation (6 DOF in 3D):**

$$
T_{\text{rigid}}(x) = R \cdot x + t
$$

where $R \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ is a rotation matrix ($R^\top R = I,\; \det R = +1$) and $t \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is a translation vector. In 3D, the rotation can be parameterized by three Euler angles $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ or by a unit quaternion $(q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3)$:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 2(q_1^2 + q_2^2) & 2(q_1 q_0 - q_2 q_3) & 2(q_1 q_3 + q_2 q_0) \\ 2(q_1 q_0 + q_2 q_3) & 1 - 2(q_0^2 + q_2^2) & 2(q_2 q_3 - q_1 q_0) \\ 2(q_1 q_3 - q_2 q_0) & 2(q_2 q_3 + q_1 q_0) & 1 - 2(q_0^2 + q_1^2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**B. Affine Transformation (12 DOF in 3D):**

$$
T_{\text{affine}}(x) = A \cdot x + t, \qquad A \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3} \text{ (any invertible matrix)}
$$

The affine model adds scaling and shearing, and is used to correct scanner-induced anisotropic voxel sizes.

**C. Deformable Transformation (high-DOF):**

$$
T_{\text{def}}(x) = x + u(x)
$$

where $u(x)$ is a displacement field with the same dimension as the image. Common parameterizations include:

- **B-spline Free-Form Deformations (FFD):** $u(x) = \sum_{i} \beta_i(x) \, c_i$, where $\beta_i$ are B-spline basis functions and $c_i$ are control-point displacements.
- **Dense displacement field:** one displacement vector per voxel.
- **Diffeomorphic transforms:** smooth and invertible, $T(x) = x + v(x)$ with $\phi_t = v \circ \phi_t$ following a stationary velocity field (e.g., SyN, LDDMM).

### 2.1.2 Block 2 — Similarity (Objective) Function $\mathcal{S}$

The similarity function quantifies alignment quality. The choice of $\mathcal{S}$ depends on whether the registration is mono-modal or multi-modal.

**A. Mono-modal (Intensity-based) Metrics:**

**Sum of Squared Differences (SSD):**
$$
\mathcal{S}_{\text{SSD}} = \frac{1}{\vert \Omega \vert} \sum_{x \in \Omega} \big[ I_F(x) - I_M(T(x)) \big]^2
$$

**Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC):**
$$
\mathcal{S}_{\text{NCC}} = \frac{\sum_{x} \big(I_F(x) - \bar{I}_F\big)\big(I_M(T(x)) - \bar{I}_M\big)}{\sqrt{\sum_{x}\big(I_F(x)-\bar{I}_F\big)^2 \cdot \sum_{x}\big(I_M(T(x))-\bar{I}_M\big)^2}}
$$

**B. Multi-modal Metrics:**

**Mutual Information (MI) — the workhorse of multimodal registration:**

$$
\mathcal{S}_{\text{MI}} = H(I_F) + H(I_M) - H(I_F, I_M)
$$

where $H$ denotes Shannon entropy. Equivalently, in terms of the joint and marginal histograms $p(a,b),\; p(a),\; p(b)$:

$$
\mathcal{S}_{\text{MI}} = \sum_{a,b} p(a,b) \, \log \frac{p(a,b)}{p(a)\,p(b)}
$$

**Normalized Mutual Information (NMI):**

$$
\mathcal{S}_{\text{NMI}} = \frac{H(I_F) + H(I_M)}{H(I_F, I_M)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for MI:** When the two images are misaligned, the joint histogram of intensities is diffuse. As registration improves, corresponding anatomical structures (e.g., bone in CT ↔ bone in MRI) produce sharp clusters in the joint histogram, lowering $H(I_F, I_M)$ and raising MI. The optimizer drives MI **upward** for multi-modal registration.

### 2.1.3 Block 3 — Optimizer

The optimizer is the search engine that updates the transformation parameters $\theta$ to extremize $\mathcal{S}$. Common choices include:

- **Gradient Descent / Gradient Ascent:** $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k \pm \eta \, \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{S}$
- **Powell's Method** (derivative-free, used in classic ITK/VTK pipelines)
- **Levenberg–Marquardt** (for non-linear least-squares)
- **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** (used in learning-based registration, e.g., VoxelMorph)
- **Adam / AdamW** (default optimizer in deep-learning registration networks)

### 2.1.4 Block 4 — Interpolator

After applying $T$, voxel centers in the moving image no longer fall on the original grid. The interpolator estimates intensity at the warped location. Common choices:

- **Nearest-Neighbor** (preserves label values — required for segmentation masks)
- **Bilinear** (2D) / **Trilinear** (3D)
- **B-spline interpolation** (cubic, of order 3 — most common in medical imaging)
- **Sinc interpolation** (theoretical optimum, expensive in computation)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **the single most important quick-reference block** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 — Image Registration.

| # | Concept / Formula | Expression | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Generic registration objective | $T^{*} = \arg\min_{T} \; \mathcal{S}(I_F,\; I_M \circ T)$ | Universal statement of the registration problem. |
| 2 | Rigid transform (3D) | $T(x) = R\,x + t$ | Same anatomy, no deformation (e.g., bone, intra-cranial). |
| 3 | Affine transform (3D) | $T(x) = A\,x + t$ | Correcting for scaling, shearing, scanner anisotropy. |
| 4 | Deformable (FFD) transform | $T(x) = x + u(x)$ | Soft-tissue, breathing, surgical shift, growth. |
| 5 | Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) | $\frac{1}{\vert\Omega\vert}\sum_{x}\big(I_F(x) - I_M(T(x))\big)^2$ | Mono-modal, intensity-preserving modality. |
| 6 | Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) | $\frac{\sum(I_F-\bar I_F)(I_M-\bar I_M)}{\sqrt{\sum(I_F-\bar I_F)^2 \sum(I_M-\bar I_M)^2}}$ | Mono-modal, robust to linear intensity shifts. |
| 7 | Mutual Information (MI) | $\sum_{a,b} p(a,b)\log\frac{p(a,b)}{p(a)p(b)}$ | **Multi-modal (CT–MRI, PET–MRI)**. |
| 8 | Normalized MI (NMI) | $\frac{H(I_F)+H(I_M)}{H(I_F,I_M)}$ | Multi-modal, more robust to overlap changes. |
| 9 | Shannon entropy | $H(I) = -\sum_i p(i)\log p(i)$ | Building block for MI. |
| 10 | Gradient-descent update | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k - \eta\,\nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{S}$ | Generic iterative optimization. |
| 11 | Jacobian determinant of $T$ | $J(x) = \det(\nabla T(x))$ | Must be positive everywhere for a **diffeomorphism**. |
| 12 | Target Registration Error (TRE) | $\text{TRE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert x_i^{\text{true}} - T(x_i^{\text{mov}})\vert^2}$ | Gold standard for evaluating registration accuracy. |
| 13 | Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) for overlap | $\text{DSC} = \frac{2\,\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Used to validate registration via segmentation overlap. |
| 14 | Hausdorff Distance (HD) | $d_H(A,B) = \max\big(\max_{a\in A}\min_{b\in B} d(a,b),\; \max_{b\in B}\min_{a\in A} d(b,a)\big)$ | Boundary-based registration error. |
| 15 | Diffeomorphism (key property) | $T$ smooth, bijective, $J(x) > 0 \; \forall x$ | Required for anatomical plausibility in deformable registration. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the *minimization* and *maximization* conventions. SSD is **minimized**; NCC and MI are **maximized**. When writing the objective, always state the direction explicitly.

## 2.3 Why These Models Matter — Real-World Engineering Rationale

- **Rigid registration** is the clinical workhorse for **brain imaging, orthopedic imaging, and dental CBCT** because bone is rigid. It is computationally cheap and unambiguous.
- **Affine registration** is the mandatory first step in almost every pipeline — it normalizes voxel sizes and corrects global scanner mis-calibrations before non-rigid refinement.
- **Deformable registration** is essential for **thoracic imaging (breathing motion), abdominal imaging (organ shift), and longitudinal tumor monitoring**, where tissue warps significantly between scans.
- **Mutual Information** is what made **CT–MRI fusion in image-guided surgery** clinically viable in the 1990s (Viola & Wells, 1997; Maes et al., 1997); without it, multimodal alignment would be impossible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rigid Transformation Matrix from Euler Angles

To prove that a 3D rotation can be written as a product of three planar rotations, we begin with the three elementary rotation matrices in 3D and compose them.

### Step 1: Define elementary rotations

**Rotation about the X-axis by angle $\alpha$ (roll):**

$$
R_x(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Rotation about the Y-axis by angle $\beta$ (pitch):**

$$
R_y(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Rotation about the Z-axis by angle $\gamma$ (yaw):**

$$
R_z(\gamma) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Reasoning:** Each elementary rotation rotates the coordinate system in a single plane while leaving the perpendicular axis unchanged. By combining them, we can produce any orientation in 3D space.

### Step 2: Compose the rotations

Using the convention $R = R_z(\gamma)\,R_y(\beta)\,R_x(\alpha)$ (extrinsic Z-Y-X), we compute the product:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Carry out the inner product $R_y R_x$

$$
R_y R_x = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & \sin\beta\,\sin\alpha & \sin\beta\,\cos\alpha \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ -\sin\beta & \cos\beta\,\sin\alpha & \cos\beta\,\cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Reasoning:** Each entry of $R_y R_x$ is obtained by the standard row-times-column multiplication.

### Step 4: Multiply by $R_z$ on the left

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\gamma\,\cos\beta & \cos\gamma\,\sin\beta\,\sin\alpha - \sin\gamma\,\cos\alpha & \cos\gamma\,\sin\beta\,\cos\alpha + \sin\gamma\,\sin\alpha \\ \sin\gamma\,\cos\beta & \sin\gamma\,\sin\beta\,\sin\alpha + \cos\gamma\,\cos\alpha & \sin\gamma\,\sin\beta\,\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma\,\sin\alpha \\ -\sin\beta & \cos\beta\,\sin\alpha & \cos\beta\,\cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Reasoning:** This is the **explicit closed-form 3D rotation matrix in Z-Y-X Euler convention**, parameterised by exactly three parameters $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$. It is orthogonal ($R^\top R = I$) and has determinant $+1$, as required of a proper rotation.

### Step 5: Convert Euler to quaternion (alternative parameterisation)

To avoid gimbal lock, the rotation is often reparameterised as a unit quaternion $q = (q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3)$:

$$
q_0 = \cos\frac{\theta}{2}, \qquad q_1 = n_x \sin\frac{\theta}{2}, \qquad q_2 = n_y \sin\frac{\theta}{2}, \qquad q_3 = n_z \sin\frac{\theta}{2}
$$

where $\theta$ is the total rotation angle and $\hat{n} = (n_x, n_y, n_z)$ is the unit rotation axis. The 6-DOF rigid transformation in 3D is therefore $(t_x, t_y, t_z, q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3)$ with the constraint $q_0^2+q_1^2+q_2^2+q_3^2 = 1$, i.e., **6 free parameters** in total.

## 3.2 Derivation of Mutual Information from Information Theory

### Step 1: Define the marginal and joint intensity distributions

Let $a \in A$ index discrete intensity bins of $I_F$ and $b \in B$ index bins of $I_M$. The marginals are:

$$
p(a) = \sum_{b} p(a,b), \qquad p(b) = \sum_{a} p(a,b)
$$

The joint $p(a,b)$ is computed from a 2D histogram of intensity co-occurrences at spatially corresponding voxels.

> **Reasoning:** $p(a,b)$ measures how often a voxel of intensity $a$ in $I_F$ appears aligned with a voxel of intensity $b$ in $I_M$. Perfect alignment produces sharp clusters.

### Step 2: Define Shannon entropy

$$
H(I_F) = -\sum_a p(a)\log p(a), \qquad H(I_M) = -\sum_b p(b)\log p(b), \qquad H(I_F, I_M) = -\sum_{a,b} p(a,b)\log p(a,b)
$$

### Step 3: Derive MI from Kullback–Leibler divergence

The Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence between the joint distribution and the product of marginals measures statistical dependence:

$$
D_{\text{KL}}\big(p(a,b) \;\Vert\; p(a)p(b)\big) = \sum_{a,b} p(a,b)\, \log \frac{p(a,b)}{p(a)\,p(b)}
$$

This is non-negative and equals zero **iff** the two images are statistically independent (i.e., uncorrelated — typically a sign of misalignment). Therefore, MI is defined as:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{MI}(I_F, I_M) = \sum_{a,b} p(a,b)\, \log \frac{p(a,b)}{p(a)\,p(b)}\;}
$$

### Step 4: Establish the equivalence with the entropy formulation

Using $H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - I(X;Y)$, we obtain:

$$
\text{MI}(I_F, I_M) = H(I_F) + H(I_M) - H(I_F, I_M)
$$

> **Reasoning:** Maximising MI is equivalent to (a) increasing individual entropies (richer information content) and (b) reducing the joint entropy (tighter coupling between intensities). At perfect alignment, the joint histogram is concentrated and $H(I_F, I_M)$ drops, so MI rises.

### Step 5: Normalize to obtain NMI

To make MI invariant to the size of the overlap region (important in clinical scans with FOV differences), Studholme et al. (1999) proposed:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{NMI}(I_F, I_M) = \frac{H(I_F) + H(I_M)}{H(I_F, I_M)}\;}
$$

> **Reasoning:** NMI penalises changes in $H(I_F, I_M)$ that are merely due to overlap variation rather than misalignment. It is the default metric in many clinical registration toolkits (e.g., ITK, Elastix, 3D Slicer).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Common Registration Approaches

This is the **single most valuable comparative table for KTU long-answer questions** on registration.

| Method | DOF | Modalities | Strengths | Weaknesses | Typical Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rigid (ICP/SSD/MI)** | 6 | Mono / Multi | Fast, unambiguous, robust | Cannot model tissue deformation | Brain, bone, dental, prostate (intra-fraction) |
| **Affine (MI/NMI)** | 12 | Mono / Multi | Corrects global scaling/shear | Still no local warping | Pre-processing step in almost every pipeline |
| **B-spline FFD (MI/NCC)** | $\sim 10^3$–$10^5$ | Mono / Multi | Smooth, locally controllable | Local minima, slow | Thoracic (breathing), abdominal, longitudinal |
| **Demons (diffusion-based)** | Dense | Mono | Fast, fully parallel | Not diffeomorphic, intensity-only | Cardiac motion, brain (BRAINSDemon) |
| **SyN (Symmetric Normalization)** | Dense | Mono / Multi | Diffeomorphic, topologically preserved | Computationally expensive | Brain MRI, neuro (ANTs toolkit) |
| **VoxelMorph (DL-based)** | Dense | Mono / Multi | Inference in <1s, unsupervised | Requires training data, generalization | Cardiac, brain, multi-site studies |
| **Keypoint-based (SIFT, SuperPoint)** | Sparse | Mono / Multi | Robust to large initial misalignment | Keypoint detection failure | Initial alignment, surgical navigation |
| **Deformable via DL (CycleMorph, etc.)** | Dense | Multi | Joint deformable + contrast translation | Mode collapse, hallucination | Cross-modal deformable registration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** When asked to *compare* registration methods, the answer must explicitly mention **DOF**, **modality support**, **computational cost**, **diffeomorphism guarantee**, and **clinical validation status** — all of which are covered in the matrix above.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — 2D SSD on Synthetic Data

**Setup:** A 2D fixed image $I_F$ of size $2 \times 2$ with intensity values

$$
I_F = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 20 \\ 30 & 40 \end{bmatrix}
$$

and a moving image $I_M$ identical to $I_F$ but translated by $(+1, 0)$ — i.e., one column to the right:

$$
I_M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 10 & 20 \\ 0 & 30 & 40 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(zero-padded to align grids)}
$$

**Goal:** Find the optimal translation $t^* = (t_x, 0)$ minimizing SSD.

**Step 1:** For $t_x = 0$, the overlap region is the full $2 \times 2$ block. SSD is

$$
\mathcal{S}(t_x = 0) = \frac{1}{4}\big[(10-10)^2 + (20-20)^2 + (30-30)^2 + (40-40)^2\big] = 0
$$

(Wait — actually, if $I_M$ has been zero-padded, the pixel at the first column is $0$, so let us recompute carefully. The correct formulation treats unaligned pixels as missing or uses a mask. Using a mask, the SSD for $t_x = 0$ on the **overlap region** is **0**.)

**Step 2:** For $t_x = -1$ (i.e., shift $I_M$ back by one column, which is the ground truth), the overlap region collapses to nothing — SSD is undefined.

> **Reasoning:** This example illustrates the **discrete nature of translation** in voxel grids and motivates the use of **continuous parameter optimization with interpolation** (e.g., trilinear interpolation plus gradient-based search). In real clinical software, $\theta$ is a continuous vector and the optimizer uses **gradient descent, Adam, or Levenberg–Marquardt** to navigate the cost surface smoothly.

## 3.5 Analytical Insight — Why Multi-Modal Registration is Hard

For mono-modal registration, SSD works because $I_F(x)$ and $I_M(T(x))$ measure the same physical quantity (e.g., MRI intensity). For multi-modal, intensities in $I_F$ and $I_M$ have **different physical meaning**:

- **CT intensity** = Hounsfield unit (linear in electron density).
- **MRI intensity** = proton density, $T_1$, $T_2$-weighted (no fixed physical unit).

There is no function $f$ such that $I_F(x) = f(I_M(T(x)))$ pointwise. Hence SSD, which assumes $I_F - I_M \approx 0$, fails catastrophically. **Mutual Information** succeeds because it depends only on the **statistical co-occurrence** of intensities, not on their numerical values — exactly the invariance required for cross-modality alignment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Image Registration Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical clinical image registration pipeline, including all four components discussed in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fixed Image I_F] --> C[Similarity Metric S]
    B[Moving Image I_M] --> D[Interpolator Resample]
    D --> C
    C --> E[Compute Cost Value]
    E --> F[Optimizer]
    F --> G[Update Transformation Parameters theta]
    G --> H[Transformation Model T theta]
    H --> D
    I[Initial Transform theta_0] --> H
    F --> J{Convergence Reached}
    J -->|No| G
    J -->|Yes| K[Output Registered Image I_reg and T star]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The optimizer starts with an initial guess $\theta_0$ for the transformation parameters. The transformation model applies $T(\theta)$ to the moving image, the interpolator resamples $I_M$ at the warped coordinates, and the similarity metric evaluates alignment quality. The cost value is fed back to the optimizer, which updates $\theta$ to reduce (or increase) $\mathcal{S}$. The loop terminates when $\mathcal{S}$ stops improving or $\vert \Delta\theta \vert$ falls below a threshold.

## 4.2 Clinical Decision Flow — Choosing a Registration Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose Registration Method] --> Q1{Are modalities same?}
    Q1 -->|Yes Mono| Q2{Is tissue rigid?}
    Q1 -->|No Multi| M1[Use MI or NMI]
    M1 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Rigid SSD or NCC]
    Q2 -->|No Soft Tissue| D1[B-spline FFD with MI or NCC]
    D1 --> V1[Validate with Dice or TRE]
    R1 --> V1
    M1 --> V1
    V1 --> END[Output Registered Image]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The decision flow guides a clinical engineer or researcher through the registration design choice. The first branching is by modality (mono vs. multi), the second by tissue type (rigid vs. soft). Validation using Dice or TRE is a non-optional final step.

## 4.3 Clinical Application Map (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid graph maps how the registration pipeline is reused across multiple clinical applications — emphasizing its role as a foundational utility in medical image analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Modalities]
        CT[CT Scan]
        MRI[MRI Scan]
        PET[PET Scan]
        US[Ultrasound]
        XR[X-ray]
    end

    subgraph REG[Registration Engine]
        RIGID[Rigid Registration]
        AFF[Affine Registration]
        DEF[Deformable FFD]
        DL[Deep Learning VoxelMorph]
        MI[Mutual Information]
    end

    subgraph APPS[Clinical Applications]
        A1[Tumor Monitoring Longitudinal]
        A2[Image Guided Surgery]
        A3[Radiation Therapy Planning]
        A4[Atlas Based Segmentation]
        A5[Cardiac Motion Analysis]
        A6[Brain Atlas Mapping]
    end

    CT --> RIGID
    MRI --> RIGID
    CT --> MI
    MRI --> MI
    PET --> MI
    US --> AFF
    XR --> AFF
    RIGID --> DEF
    AFF --> DEF
    MI --> DL

    DEF --> A1
    DEF --> A2
    DL --> A2
    DL --> A3
    DL --> A4
    DL --> A5
    DL --> A6
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The input modalities feed into different registration engines based on their characteristics (CT and MRI to rigid + MI; ultrasound and X-ray to affine for FOV correction; the output of any registration is then passed to a deformable engine for fine alignment). The downstream clinical applications consume the registered image. The diagram emphasizes that **registration is a shared infrastructure component**, not a stand-alone task.

## 4.4 Conceptual Diagram — Multiscale Registration Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Coarse Resolution Low Sigma] --> P2[Medium Resolution]
    P2 --> P3[Fine Resolution High Sigma]
    P3 --> P4[Final Registered Output]

    P1 --> P1A[Estimate Global Transform R t]
    P2 --> P2A[Estimate Local Affine]
    P3 --> P3A[Estimate Deformable Field u x]
    P1A --> P2A
    P2A --> P3A
    P3A --> P4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Coarse-to-fine (multiscale) registration is a standard engineering pattern: it avoids local minima by first aligning at low resolution, then progressively refining at higher resolutions. The transformation parameters estimated at a coarser scale are used to **initialize** the next finer scale — drastically improving robustness and convergence speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Visual Literacy Note:** In the KTU 2024 exam, schematic diagrams of the registration pipeline, similarity metric, and clinical application flow are **high-scoring** answers. A clear, labelled block diagram (such as the Mermaid blocks above) can fetch full marks even when the textual explanation is moderate.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A contains 3-mark direct-concept questions. Answers should be 4–6 lines, with at least one mathematical expression or labelled diagram where applicable.

---

### **Q1. Define image registration. State the mathematical formulation of the registration problem.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEBMT865]`

**Model Answer:**

Image registration is the process of spatially aligning two images — a fixed image $I_F$ and a moving image $I_M$ — by computing a spatial transformation $T$ that maps points in $I_M$ onto their corresponding locations in $I_F$. It is the foundational step for multimodal image fusion, longitudinal comparison, and atlas-based analysis.

The problem is formally stated as the following optimization:

$$
T^{*} = \arg\min_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \; \mathcal{S}\big(I_F(x),\; I_M(T(x))\big)
$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ is the similarity measure and $\mathcal{T}$ is the transformation space. The optimizer seeks the transformation $T^*$ that best aligns the two images according to $\mathcal{S}$.

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between mono-modal and multi-modal image registration. Give one example of a similarity metric used in each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEBMT865]`
