- **Projective:** Endoscopic views.
- **Non-rigid / Deformable:** Soft tissue, tumour mass effect, breathing motion — modelled with B-splines, demons, or deep learning displacement fields.

### 2.1.4 Noise, Artifact & Intensity Challenges
- **Gaussian noise** (thermal sensor noise).
- **Speckle noise** (ultrasound).
- **Motion artifact** (cardiac, respiratory, patient movement).
- **Bias field** (low-frequency intensity inhomogeneity in MRI).
- **Partial volume effect** at tissue boundaries.
- **Metal / beam-hardening artifacts** in CT.

### 2.1.5 Anatomical & Pathological Variability
- **Inter-patient variability** in shape, size, and position of organs.
- **Intra-patient variability** between sessions (weight change, bladder filling, tumour growth/shrinkage).
- **Topological changes** (appearance/disappearance of lesions between scans).
- **Pathological deformation** — tumours displace or invade surrounding tissue, breaking the smoothness assumption of most deformation models.

### 2.1.6 Computational & Optimization Challenges
- **Non-convexity:** The similarity function has many local maxima.
- **Computational cost:** A 3D deformable registration can require $10^6$ voxels $\times 10^4$ DOF — too slow without GPUs.
- **Initialization dependence:** A bad initial alignment can trap the optimiser in a wrong basin.
- **Lack of ground truth:** Cannot easily obtain the "correct" transformation for a real patient.

## 2.2 The Generic Registration Pipeline and Where Challenges Enter

A typical medical image registration framework is structured as a closed-loop optimization:

1. **Feature / Similarity Computation:** Measure of alignment quality.
2. **Transformation Parameterization:** Mathematical model for the spatial mapping.
3. **Optimization / Search:** Iterative update of transformation parameters.
4. **Regularization:** Constraint to keep the transformation physically plausible.
5. **Validation:** Quantitative assessment of the result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every challenge in image registration can be mapped to exactly one of these five pipeline stages.** This is a high-yield KTU framing.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Transformation Model (Affine) | $T(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}$ | $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| Sum of Squared Differences | $\text{SSD} = \sum_i \bigl(I_F(\mathbf{x}_i) - T(I_M)(\mathbf{x}_i)\bigr)^2$ | Mono-modal only |
| Normalized Cross-Correlation | $\text{NCC} = \dfrac{\sum (I_F - \bar{I}_F)(I_M' - \bar{I}_M')}{\sigma_F \, \sigma_{M'}}$ | Robust to linear intensity shifts |
| Mutual Information | $I(X;Y) = \sum_{x,y} p(x,y) \log \dfrac{p(x,y)}{p(x)\,p(y)}$ | Multi-modal gold standard; **bits** |
| Normalized MI (Studholme) | $\text{NMI} = \dfrac{H(X) + H(Y)}{H(X,Y)}$ | Scale-invariant |
| B-spline Deformation Field | $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j} \mathbf{c}_j \, \beta_j(\mathbf{x})$ | $\mathbf{c}_j$ = control points; $\beta_j$ = basis fn |
| Demons Force | $\vec{F} = \dfrac{(I_F - I_M) \, \nabla I_M}{\vert \nabla I_M \vert^2 + (I_F - I_M)^2}$ | Optical-flow analogy |
| Diffusion Regularizer | $\mathcal{R}(T) = \dfrac{1}{2} \int_\Omega \vert \nabla \mathbf{u} \vert^2 \, d\mathbf{x}$ | Penalises folding |
| Dice Score (validation) | $\text{DSC} = \dfrac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | $0 \le \text{DSC} \le 1$ |
| Target Registration Error | $\text{TRE} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert \mathbf{x}_i^F - T(\mathbf{x}_i^M) \vert$ | Measured in **mm** |
| Optimization Objective | $\mathcal{J}(T) = -\mathcal{M}(I_F, T(I_M)) + \lambda \, \mathcal{R}(T)$ | $\lambda$ = regularization weight |
| Jacobian Determinant (folding) | $J_T(\mathbf{x}) = \det(\nabla T(\mathbf{x}))$ | $J_T < 0 \Rightarrow$ non-physical fold |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations power everything from MRI-CT fusion in radiotherapy planning, to AI-assisted surgery, to longitudinal tumour-tracking in oncology. The Dice coefficient is the universal "report card" a KTU examiner expects you to know.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Image-Guided Radiotherapy (IGRT):** Registering daily Cone-Beam CT to planning CT requires sub-2 mm accuracy — a direct challenge of soft-tissue deformation and patient repositioning.
- **AI-Assisted Surgery (Da Vinci, BrainLab):** Real-time 2D-3D registration between laparoscopic video and pre-operative CT faces 30 Hz frame-rate constraints.
- **Longitudinal Oncology Studies:** Tracking the same tumour across months requires deformable registration robust to growth/shrinkage and treatment-induced oedema.
- **Atlas-based Segmentation:** A population atlas must be registered to a new patient's scan — a non-rigid, multi-atlas, multi-modal challenge.
- **Cardiac MRI Motion Tracking:** Respiratory and cardiac cycles demand temporal registration within milliseconds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Form Solution for the 2D Rigid Transformation

Although "challenges" prevent a clean solution in real cases, it is useful to derive the **idealized** case so that students can see what perfect registration looks like and then understand *why* the real-world challenges (noise, deformation, multi-modality) break it.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Let two sets of $N$ corresponding 2D landmark points be given:

$$P_i = (x_i, y_i)^T \quad \text{(fixed image landmarks)}$$
$$Q_i = (u_i, v_i)^T \quad \text{(moving image landmarks)}$$

We wish to find rotation angle $\theta$ and translation $\mathbf{t} = (t_x, t_y)^T$ that minimizes:

$$E(\theta, \mathbf{t}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl\vert Q_i - (\mathbf{R}(\theta) P_i + \mathbf{t}) \bigr\vert^2$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Minimization

**Step 1:** Substitute the rotation matrix:
$$\mathbf{R}(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the gradient with respect to translation and set to zero.
$$ \frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{t}} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl( Q_i - \mathbf{R}(\theta) P_i - \mathbf{t} \bigr) = 0 $$

**Step 3:** Solve for the optimal $\mathbf{t}^\ast$ given $\theta$:
$$ \mathbf{t}^\ast = \bar{Q} - \mathbf{R}(\theta) \bar{P} $$

where $\bar{P} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i P_i$ and $\bar{Q} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i Q_i$ are the centroids.

**Step 4:** Substitute $\mathbf{t}^\ast$ back into $E$ and simplify.
$$ E(\theta) = \sum_i \bigl\vert \tilde{Q}_i - \mathbf{R}(\theta) \tilde{P}_i \bigr\vert^2 $$

where $\tilde{P}_i = P_i - \bar{P}$ and $\tilde{Q}_i = Q_i - \bar{Q}$ are the centred points.

**Step 5:** Expand the squared norm:
$$ E(\theta) = \sum_i \bigl( \vert \tilde{Q}_i \vert^2 - 2 \tilde{Q}_i^T \mathbf{R}(\theta) \tilde{P}_i + \vert \tilde{P}_i \vert^2 \bigr) $$

**Step 6:** Differentiate with respect to $\theta$ and set to zero.
$$ \frac{dE}{d\theta} = 2 \sum_i \bigl( \tilde{Q}_i^T \mathbf{R}'(\theta) \tilde{P}_i \bigr) = 0 $$

where
$$ \mathbf{R}'(\theta) = \begin{pmatrix} -\sin\theta & -\cos\theta \\ \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \end{pmatrix} $$

**Step 7:** Let $H = \sum_i \tilde{P}_i \tilde{Q}_i^T$ be the cross-covariance matrix.
One can show that the closed-form angle is:
$$ \theta^\ast = \operatorname{atan2}\!\left( H_{21} - H_{12}, \; H_{11} + H_{22} \right) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This closed-form solution exists ONLY when:** (a) the transformation is rigid, (b) corresponding landmarks are known, and (c) there is no noise/outliers. **These three ideal conditions are precisely what the real-world challenges violate**, which is why iterative optimization or learning-based methods are needed in practice.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Each Challenge to Pipeline Stage

| Challenge Family | Pipeline Stage Affected | Mathematical Consequence | Typical Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Multi-modality | Similarity metric | Non-functional $I_F \leftrightarrow I_M$ mapping; MI surface is non-convex | Use MI / NMI / modality-independent neighbourhood descriptors |
| Soft-tissue deformation | Transformation model | Rigid/affine insufficient; needs $10^4$ DOF | B-splines, demons, learning-based displacement fields |
| Noise / artifacts | Similarity metric | Local minima, gradient corruption | Pre-filtering (Gaussian, NL-Means), robust metrics (M-estimators) |
| Bias field | Similarity metric | Spurious intensity shift looks like misalignment | Bias correction (N3, N4) before registration |
| Partial volume | Similarity metric | Boundary ambiguity | Sub-pixel interpolation, multi-resolution pyramid |
| Anatomical variability | Transformation model | Patient-specific DOFs | Population atlases, subject-specific adaptation |
| Pathological changes | Regularization | Discontinuities break smoothness | Spatially-varying regularization, topology-preserving constraints |
| Computational cost | Optimization | Time/memory blow-up | Multi-resolution, GPU acceleration, learning-based amortized registration |
| Lack of ground truth | Validation | Cannot compute true TRE | Phantom studies, expert landmarks, simulated data |
| Initialization | Optimization | Wrong basin of attraction | Multi-start, centre-of-mass alignment, atlas priors |

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Bias-Field Induced Pseudo-Misalignment

Consider a 1D intensity profile $I_F(x)$ corrupted by a multiplicative bias field $B(x) = 1 + \alpha \sin(2\pi x / L)$:
$$ I_M(x) = I_F(x) \cdot B(x) = I_F(x)\bigl(1 + \alpha \sin(2\pi x / L)\bigr) $$

**Step 1:** Compute the SSD without bias correction:
$$ \text{SSD}_{\text{naive}} = \int \bigl( I_F(x) - I_M(x-T) \bigr)^2 dx $$

**Step 2:** For small $T$ and $\alpha$, Taylor expand $I_M(x-T) \approx I_M(x) - T I_M'(x)$:
$$ \text{SSD} \approx \int \bigl( \alpha I_F \sin(2\pi x/L) - T I_M'(x) \bigr)^2 dx $$

**Step 3:** Differentiating and setting to zero:
$$ T_{\text{bias}} \approx \frac{\alpha \int I_F \sin(2\pi x/L) \, I_M'(x) \, dx}{\int (I_M'(x))^2 \, dx} \neq 0 $$

**Interpretation:** A bias field of even 5–10% amplitude ($|\alpha| \le 0.1$) can simulate a spurious translation of several millimetres, causing the registration to converge to a *wrong* alignment. This is a textbook "challenge" answer a KTU examiner will reward.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Medical Image Registration Pipeline (with Challenge Injection Points)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fixed Image IF] --> B[Pre-Processing Module]
    A2[Moving Image IM] --> B
    B --> C[Feature Extraction Block]
    C --> D[Similarity Metric Computation]
    D --> E[Transformation Model Evaluator]
    E --> F[Regularization Module]
    F --> G[Optimizer with Multi-Resolution Pyramid]
    G -->|Update Parameters| E
    G --> H[Convergence Check]
    H -->|Not Converged| G
    H -->|Converged| I[Validated Spatial Transform]
    I --> J[Resampled Aligned Image]

    C -. Challenge .-> C1[Noise Speckle Bias Field]
    D -. Challenge .-> D1[Multi-Modality Non-Convexity]
    E -. Challenge .-> E1[Deformation Type DOF Selection]
    F -. Challenge .-> F1[Topology Preservation Pathology]
    G -. Challenge .-> G1[Local Minima Initialization]
    H -. Challenge .-> H1[Ground Truth Lack]
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy Diagram of Registration Challenges

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Challenges in Medical Image Registration] --> A1[Dimensional and Geometric]
    R --> A2[Modality Related]
    R --> A3[Deformation Related]
    R --> A4[Noise Artifact Intensity]
    R --> A5[Anatomical and Pathological]
    R --> A6[Computational and Optimization]

    A1 --> A1a[2D to 3D Registration]
    A1 --> A1b[High DOF Parameter Space]
    A2 --> A2a[CT to MRI Intensity Mismatch]
    A2 --> A2b[MRI to PET Statistical Mapping]
    A3 --> A3a[Rigid Affine Insufficient]
    A3 --> A3b[Non Rigid Soft Tissue]
    A4 --> A4a[Gaussian and Speckle Noise]
    A4 --> A4b[Bias Field Inhomogeneity]
    A4 --> A4c[Motion and Partial Volume]
    A5 --> A5a[Inter Patient Variability]
    A5 --> A5b[Tumour Growth and Shrinkage]
    A6 --> A6a[Non Convex Optimization]
    A6 --> A6b[Real Time GPU Constraints]
    A6 --> A6c[Lack of Ground Truth]
```

## 4.3 Deformation Type Hierarchy (DOF Comparison)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Rigid 6 DOF] --> P2[Affine 12 DOF]
    P2 --> P3[Projective 15 DOF]
    P3 --> P4[Non Rigid Thousand Plus DOF]
    P4 --> P5[Deep Learning Displacement Field]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams for KTU exam purposes:** The flowchart above allows you to map **any** challenge mentioned in a question paper back to a specific block in the registration pipeline. Examiners often frame Part B questions as "Explain the challenges at the *similarity metric* stage" — and you can then build your answer directly from the challenge nodes attached to that block.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBMT865]
**List any four major challenges faced in medical image registration and briefly explain why bias field inhomogeneity in MRI creates a registration error.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four major challenges in medical image registration are:

1. **Multi-modality intensity mismatch** (e.g., CT bone ≠ MRI bone) — breaks the assumption of a direct intensity correspondence.
2. **Soft-tissue non-rigid deformation** — the rigid/affine model is insufficient for organs like the liver, bladder, and breast.
3. **Noise and acquisition artifacts** — Gaussian/speckle noise and motion artifacts introduce local minima in the similarity function.
4. **Lack of ground truth and validation difficulty** — the true spatial alignment is unknown for in-vivo data.

**Bias field explanation:** MRI exhibits a spatially-varying low-frequency intensity multiplicative distortion known as the bias field $B(\mathbf{x}) \approx 1 + \alpha \sin(2\pi \mathbf{x}/L)$. This makes the apparent intensity of the *same* tissue differ across the field of view, mimicking a spatial misalignment. The optimizer interprets this intensity gradient as a translation/deformation, producing spurious registration errors of the order of $\alpha \cdot L / 2\pi$ mm. **(1 mark for challenge list, 1.5 marks for bias-field explanation, 0.5 mark for example/equation.)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBMT865]
**Differentiate between rigid and non-rigid (deformable) image registration. State two clinical scenarios where deformable registration is mandatory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Rigid Registration | Non-Rigid / Deformable Registration |
|---|---|---|
| DOF | 6 (3 translation + 3 rotation) | $> 10^3$ |
| Transformation | Global rotation/translation | Local displacement field $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ |
| Typical use | Bone, skull, stereotactic frames | Soft tissue, tumour, breathing motion |
| Optimization | Closed-form possible with landmarks | Iterative (B-spline, demons, deep nets) |

**Two clinical scenarios where deformable registration is mandatory:** **(2 marks)**
1. **Radiotherapy planning for abdominal tumours** — daily deformation of the liver and stomach due to bladder filling and respiration requires B-spline deformable registration to track the planning target volume.
2. **Longitudinal tumour monitoring** — comparing pre- and post-chemotherapy MRI scans to assess tumour shrinkage requires deformable registration because the surrounding anatomy also changes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** write only "challenges are noise and deformation." Examiners expect you to (a) *classify* the challenges, (b) *map* them to the registration pipeline stage, and (c) *quantify* the effect (Dice drop, TRE increase, bias-field magnitude).
> - **Do NOT** confuse multi-modality (a *similarity metric* problem) with non-rigid deformation (a *transformation model* problem). This conflation costs 2–3 marks consistently.
> - Always provide an **equation** when discussing a similarity metric. A purely verbal answer for NCC / SSD / MI loses 1–2 marks.
> - For 14-mark Part B questions, ensure **both sub-parts (a) and (b) are answered with equal depth** — examiners are instructed to split marks 7+7.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the six major families of challenges encountered in medical image registration. Map each family to the corresponding stage in the registration pipeline and justify the mapping with a one-sentence engineering reason.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Dimensional / Geometric Challenges → Transformation model stage.**
   *Reason:* Registering a 2D fluoroscopic X-ray to a 3D CT volume requires projective geometry, increasing the parameter-space complexity.
2. **Modality Challenges → Similarity metric stage.**
   *Reason:* CT and MRI have functionally unrelated intensities for the same tissue, so SSD fails and Mutual Information is required.
3. **Deformation Challenges → Transformation model stage.**
   *Reason:* Soft-tissue non-rigid motion has $10^4+$ DOF, far exceeding rigid/affine capacity.
4. **Noise / Artifact / Intensity Challenges → Similarity metric stage.**
   *Reason:* Speckle, bias field, and partial-volume effects distort the intensity correspondence and create local minima.
5. **Anatomical / Pathological Variability → Regularization stage.**
   *Reason:* Tumour growth and oedema violate smoothness assumptions, requiring topology-preserving constraints.
6. **Computational / Optimization Challenges → Optimizer stage.**
   *Reason:* Non-convex similarity surfaces combined with millions of DOF trap iterative optimizers in local minima.

**(1 mark per family, 1 mark for correct mapping to pipeline stage.)**

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Derive the closed-form solution for a 2D rigid transformation assuming known corresponding landmarks. Then explain, with two concrete reasons, why this closed-form solution fails in routine clinical registration.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):** See Section 3.1 above for the full step-by-step derivation. Key steps to write:
- Centroid subtraction to decouple rotation from translation.
- Optimal translation: $\mathbf{t}^\ast = \bar{Q} - \mathbf{R}(\theta)\bar{P}$.
- Optimal rotation: $\theta^\ast = \operatorname{atan2}(H_{21} - H_{12}, \; H_{11} + H_{22})$ where $H = \sum_i \tilde{P}_i \tilde{Q}_i^T$.

**Why it fails clinically (3 marks):**

1. **No reliable landmark correspondence in soft tissue.** The closed-form solution requires *manually identified, exactly corresponding* landmarks. In CT-MRI fusion of the abdomen, no such landmarks exist for the liver parenchyma, so the method is inapplicable.
2. **The deformation is not rigid.** Soft-tissue organs deform by tens of millimetres between sessions, violating the rigid-transformation assumption. The SSD/NCC cost function then has a flat or non-convex landscape and the closed-form angle is no longer meaningful.
3. *(Optional third reason, for extra credit)* The images are corrupted by noise and bias field, so the centroid-based decoupled solution is biased and converges to a wrong alignment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Discuss the role of Mutual Information (MI) as a similarity metric in multi-modal registration. Derive the MI expression from first principles of information theory and explain why it is robust to multi-modal intensity mismatch.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Information-theoretic definition (2 marks):**
$$I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) = \sum_{x,y} p(x,y) \log \frac{p(x,y)}{p(x)\,p(y)}$$
where $H(X) = -\sum_x p(x) \log p(x)$ is the Shannon entropy.

**Derivation intuition (2 marks):** MI measures the reduction in uncertainty about $X$ given $Y$. When the images are perfectly aligned, knowing a pixel's intensity in image $I_F$ sharply constrains the corresponding pixel in $I_M$ (high MI). When misaligned, the joint distribution $p(I_F, I_M)$ is spread out and MI drops. Maximizing MI therefore maximizes alignment quality.

**Why MI handles multi-modality (3 marks):**
- It does **not assume a functional** $I_F = f(I_M)$ relationship. Even if the CT-bone intensity maps to MRI-bone intensity via a complex non-linear curve, MI detects the statistical co-occurrence.
- The joint histogram captures all stochastic dependencies, including non-linear and many-to-one mappings.
- It is invariant to monotonic intensity transforms, which often approximate the modality-specific intensity calibration.

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the medical image registration pipeline. For each block, identify one specific challenge and the standard mitigation technique used in modern deep-learning–based registration systems.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):** See the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 above. Students should reproduce the five core blocks: Pre-Processing → Similarity Metric → Transformation Model → Regularization → Optimizer → Validation.

**Per-block challenge + mitigation (4 marks = 0.8 × 5 blocks):**

| Block | Challenge | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-Processing | Bias field, noise | N4 bias correction, denoising CNNs |
| Similarity Metric | Multi-modality | MI / NMI, or modality-independent neighbourhood descriptors |
| Transformation Model | Soft-tissue deformation | U-Net predicted displacement fields (VoxelMorph) |
| Regularization | Folding, pathology | Jacobian penalty, diffeomorphic layers (Stationary Velocity Field) |
| Optimizer | Local minima, speed | Multi-resolution pyramid, Adam-based amortized inference, GPU |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Challenges in Medical Image Registration**

- **Six families of challenges:** Dimensional/Geometric, Modality, Deformation, Noise/Artifact/Intensity, Anatomical/Pathological, Computational/Optimization.
- **Pipeline mapping rule:** Every challenge maps to exactly one of: Pre-processing, Similarity metric, Transformation model, Regularization, Optimizer, Validation.
- **Multi-modal registration requires MI or NMI** — never use SSD or NCC across CT–MRI.
- **Rigid transform = 6 DOF; Affine = 12 DOF; Deformable = $10^3$+ DOF.**
- **Bias field** is a low-frequency multiplicative intensity distortion in MRI that mimics a spatial shift of order $\alpha L / 2\pi$ mm.
- **Dice coefficient** and **TRE (mm)** are the two key validation metrics; clinical threshold is **TRE ≤ 2 mm** in radiotherapy.
- **Jacobian determinant** $J_T < 0$ indicates non-physical folding in deformable registration.
- **Amortized deep-learning registration (VoxelMorph-style)** mitigates the speed/local-minima challenge by replacing per-image iterative optimization with a single forward pass.
- **Lack of ground truth** is solved using (i) digital phantoms, (ii) expert landmark annotation, (iii) synthetic-deformation simulation.
- **Most common exam pitfall:** confusing a *similarity-metric* challenge (multi-modality) with a *transformation-model* challenge (non-rigid deformation) — examiners explicitly test this distinction.
- **Exam-formula must-knows:** SSD, NCC, MI, NMI, Dice, TRE, Jacobian determinant, B-spline deformation, Demons force, regularized objective $\mathcal{J} = -\mathcal{M} + \lambda \mathcal{R}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 2D Image Registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT865 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** 2D Image Registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**2D Image Registration** is the spatial alignment process that geometrically transforms a *moving image* (also called *source* or *floating image*, denoted as $I_m$) into the coordinate system of a *fixed image* (also called *target* or *reference image*, denoted as $I_f$), such that every anatomical, pathological, or functional point in $I_m$ corresponds to its true homologous point in $I_f$.

Mathematically, the goal is to find a spatial transformation $T : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ that minimizes a dissimilarity cost function $\mathcal{C}$:

$$\hat{T} = \arg\min_{T \in \mathcal{T}} \, \mathcal{C}\big(I_f(x), \, I_m(T(x))\big)$$

where $x = (x, y)$ is a pixel coordinate in the fixed image domain $\Omega_f$, and $\mathcal{T}$ is the family of admissible transformations (rigid, affine, projective, or deformable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In medical imaging, registration is the cornerstone of *image fusion*, *longitudinal studies*, *atlas-based segmentation*, *image-guided surgery*, and *motion correction in PET/MRI*. Module 3 emphasizes **2D registration** before extending to 3D volumes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you have **two transparent tracing sheets** of a hand-drawn map, but one is rotated 20° and slightly offset to the right.

- The **fixed sheet** = the original map pinned to a table.
- The **moving sheet** = the misaligned copy you want to align.
- Your **fingers** act as the *transformation function* $T$ — you must translate (slide), rotate, and possibly scale until the streets overlap.
- The **best alignment** is achieved when the streets, rivers, and landmarks from both sheets lie on top of each other (the cost function $\mathcal{C}$ reaches its minimum).

In a clinical setting, the "tracing sheets" are CT and MRI slices of the same patient, taken on different days or scanners, and the doctor's brain — plus computer algorithms — perform the registration.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Vocabulary (KTU Expected Definitions):**
> - **Fixed Image ($I_f$):** The reference image that stays stationary; defines the target coordinate system.
> - **Moving Image ($I_m$):** The image to be warped into alignment with $I_f$.
> - **Transformation ($T$):** The geometric mapping applied to $I_m$.
> - **Similarity Metric / Cost Function:** Quantitative measure of alignment quality.
> - **Interpolator:** Method to estimate non-grid pixel intensities after warping (e.g., **bilinear**, **bicubic**, **nearest neighbor**).
> - **Optimizer:** Algorithm that searches for the optimal $T$ (e.g., gradient descent, Powell's method, evolutionary strategies).

### 1.4 Why Registration Matters in Medical AI

- **Tumour growth monitoring** requires comparing scans taken at $t_1$ and $t_2$.
- **Multimodal fusion** of MRI (soft tissue) with CT (bone) demands inter-modal registration.
- **Atlas-based segmentation** registers a pre-labelled atlas to a patient scan.
- **AI augmentation pipelines** rely on registered pairs to create ground-truth training data.
- **Standardized constant:** The Dice similarity coefficient of overlap $\in [0, 1]$ is the **gold standard** for evaluating registration accuracy in medical AI.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Registration

Every registration framework consists of four tightly coupled components. Understanding each pillar is essential for both KTU theory and KTU numerical problems.

#### Pillar 1: Transformation Model $\mathcal{T}$

The geometric family $T$ defines *what kinds* of deformations are permissible.

For **2D image registration**, the standard families in increasing complexity are:

**(a) Rigid Transformation (2 Degrees of Freedom for translation, 1 for rotation = 3 DOF):**

$$T_{rigid}(x, y) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{pmatrix}$$

The rotation matrix is orthogonal with determinant $+1$; rigid transformations preserve distances and angles. This is the simplest and most commonly assumed model in KTU problems.

**(b) Affine Transformation (6 DOF in 2D):**

$$T_{affine}(x, y) = \begin{pmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{pmatrix}$$

Affine adds scaling, shearing, and independent axis-aligned stretching. Lines map to lines, parallel lines remain parallel.

**(c) Projective Transformation (8 DOF in 2D):** Handles perspective effects (rarely used for medical 2D registration).

**(d) Non-rigid / Deformable (infinite DOF):** Free-form deformation using B-splines, thin-plate splines, or displacement fields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A registration is called *mono-modal* if $I_f$ and $I_m$ come from the same imaging device (e.g., MRI–MRI), and *multi-modal* if they come from different devices (e.g., CT–MRI or PET–CT). The choice of similarity metric depends critically on this distinction.

#### Pillar 2: Feature Space

- **Intensity-based:** Every pixel is a feature. Robust to missing landmarks but high-dimensional.
- **Feature-based:** Extract salient keypoints (corners, edges, anatomical landmarks) using detectors like Harris, SIFT, SURF, ORB. Then match using descriptors.

#### Pillar 3: Similarity Metric $\mathcal{C}$

This is the *score function* the optimizer tries to minimize (or maximize).

| Metric | Formula | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD)** | $\mathcal{C}_{SSD} = \sum_{x \in \Omega_f} \big[I_f(x) - I_m(T(x))\big]^2$ | Mono-modal, same intensity scale |
| **Mean Absolute Difference (MAD)** | $\mathcal{C}_{MAD} = \sum_{x \in \Omega_f} \vert I_f(x) - I_m(T(x)) \vert$ | Mono-modal, robust to outliers |
| **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)** | $\mathcal{C}_{NCC} = \dfrac{\sum I_f \cdot I_m}{\sqrt{\sum I_f^2 \cdot \sum I_m^2}}$ | Mono-modal with linear intensity shifts |
| **Mutual Information (MI)** | $\mathcal{C}_{MI} = H(I_f) + H(I_m) - H(I_f, I_m)$ | Multi-modal (gold standard) |
| **Normalized MI (NMI)** | $\mathcal{C}_{NMI} = \dfrac{H(I_f) + H(I_m)}{H(I_f, I_m)}$ | Multi-modal, more robust than MI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** MI / NMI are the *de facto* standards in clinical multimodal image registration software (e.g., 3D Slicer, Elastix, ITK). They exploit the statistical dependence between intensity distributions, making them modality-agnostic.

#### Pillar 4: Optimization Strategy

- **Gradient-based:** Gradient descent, Levenberg–Marquardt (for SSD with smooth interpolator).
- **Derivative-free:** Powell's method, Downhill Simplex (Nelder–Mead), used when the metric is non-differentiable (e.g., MI).
- **Stochastic / Population-based:** Genetic algorithms, Particle Swarm, Differential Evolution — robust to local minima but expensive.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Rigid 2D transform | $x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta + t_x$ | Rotates by $\theta$ then translates by $(t_x, t_y)$ |
| | $y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta + t_y$ | |
| Affine 2D (homogeneous) | $\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} & t_x \\ a_{21} & a_{22} & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Full 2D affine in $3 \times 3$ matrix form |
| SSD | $\sum_{x \in \Omega_f}\big[I_f(x) - I_m(T(x))\big]^2$ | Monomodal cost |
| NCC | $\dfrac{\sum (I_f - \bar{I_f})(I_m - \bar{I_m})}{\sqrt{\sum (I_f - \bar{I_f})^2 \cdot \sum (I_m - \bar{I_m})^2}}$ | Invariant to linear intensity changes |
| Entropy | $H(I) = -\sum_i p_i \log p_i$ | Shannon entropy of intensity histogram |
| Joint Entropy | $H(I_f, I_m) = -\sum_{i,j} p_{i,j} \log p_{i,j}$ | From joint histogram |
| Mutual Information | $H(I_f) + H(I_m) - H(I_f, I_m)$ | Multi-modal metric |
| Bilinear interpolation | $I(x',y') = (1-\alpha)(1-\beta)I_{i,j} + \alpha(1-\beta)I_{i+1,j} + (1-\alpha)\beta I_{i,j+1} + \alpha\beta I_{i+1,j+1}$ | Standard 2D interpolator |
| Dice overlap | $\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Registration accuracy in $[0,1]$ |
| Target Registration Error | $\text{TRE} = \sqrt{(x_f - x_m)^2 + (y_f - y_m)^2}$ | Mean landmark distance in mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bars (absolute value / cardinality) above are written using the LaTeX macros `\vert` and `\mid` so the markdown table syntax is preserved. **Never** type a raw `|` inside a table cell.

### 2.3 Why and How — The Registration Pipeline Logic

1. **Initialize** $T$ (identity transform).
2. **Sample** $I_m$ at warped coordinates $T(x)$ using an interpolator.
3. **Compute** the similarity metric $\mathcal{C}$.
4. **Update** $T$ parameters via the optimizer to reduce $\mathcal{C}$.
5. **Repeat** until convergence (gradient norm $<$ threshold or max iterations reached).
6. **Validate** using Dice score, TRE, or visual inspection of overlaid checkerboard.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Image-guided radiotherapy** aligns pre-treatment CT with daily CBCT.
- **Surgical navigation** fuses pre-op MRI with intra-op video.
- **Cardiac motion tracking** in cine-MRI uses temporal registration across frames.
- **AI data curation** in hospitals requires registered image–label pairs as training data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: 2D Rigid Transformation Matrix (Homogeneous Form)

The 2D rigid body transformation is the KTU favourite. We derive it from first principles to cement understanding.

**Step 1 — Define pure 2D rotation about the origin.**

A point $P = (x, y)$ rotated counter-clockwise by angle $\theta$ produces $P' = (x', y')$. Using elementary trigonometry on the right triangle formed by $OP$:

$$x' = x \cos\theta - y \sin\theta$$

$$y' = x \sin\theta + y \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Express in matrix form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add translation $(t_x, t_y)$ to allow the rotation centre to move.**

The rotation above is about the world origin. To rotate about an arbitrary pivot, we decompose: translate pivot to origin $\to$ rotate $\to$ translate back. Combining translation with the rotation matrix and converting to homogeneous coordinates (so that all operations become matrix multiplications):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & t_x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical 2D rigid registration matrix** in homogeneous coordinates. It is *orthogonal* (rotation block) plus a translation, and has 3 parameters: $\theta, t_x, t_y$.

**Step 4 — Verify orthogonality (sanity check for KTU).**

The rotation sub-block $R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ satisfies $R^{T} R = I$ and $\det(R) = +1$. This guarantees that rigid registration preserves distances and angles.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bilinear Interpolation (the heart of resampling)

After warping, $T(x)$ rarely lands exactly on a pixel grid of $I_m$. We must interpolate.

**Step 1 — Identify the four surrounding integer pixels.**

Given a continuous query point $(x', y')$, let

$$i = \lfloor x' \rfloor, \quad j = \lfloor y' \rfloor, \quad \alpha = x' - i, \quad \beta = y' - j$$

The four neighbours are $I_m(i,j)$, $I_m(i+1,j)$, $I_m(i,j+1)$, $I_m(i+1,j+1)$.

**Step 2 — Linear interpolation along the x-direction (twice).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{top} &= (1 - \alpha) \cdot I_m(i, j) + \alpha \cdot I_m(i+1, j) \\
I_{bot} &= (1 - \alpha) \cdot I_m(i, j+1) + \alpha \cdot I_m(i+1, j+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Linear interpolation along the y-direction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x', y') &= (1 - \beta) \cdot I_{top} + \beta \cdot I_{bot}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting to get the unified bilinear formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x', y') &= (1-\alpha)(1-\beta) \cdot I_m(i,j) \\
&\quad + \alpha(1-\beta) \cdot I_m(i+1,j) \\
&\quad + (1-\alpha)\beta \cdot I_m(i,j+1) \\
&\quad + \alpha\beta \cdot I_m(i+1,j+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

This $C^0$-continuous interpolator is differentiable almost everywhere, which is essential for gradient-based registration.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: SSD Cost Function Gradient (for gradient-based optimization)

The optimizer needs $\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}_{SSD}}{\partial \theta}$ to update rotation. Let's derive it.

**Step 1 — Define the SSD cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{C}_{SSD} &= \sum_{x \in \Omega_f} \big[ I_f(x, y) - I_m(x', y') \big]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $(x', y') = T_\theta(x, y)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}_{SSD}}{\partial \theta} &= \sum_{x \in \Omega_f} 2\big[ I_f(x, y) - I_m(x', y') \big] \cdot \left(-\frac{\partial I_m}{\partial \theta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the chain rule again on $\frac{\partial I_m}{\partial \theta}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial I_m}{\partial \theta} &= \frac{\partial I_m}{\partial x'} \cdot \frac{\partial x'}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial I_m}{\partial y'} \cdot \frac{\partial y'}{\partial \theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\frac{\partial x'}{\partial \theta}$ and $\frac{\partial y'}{\partial \theta}$.**

From the rigid transform, $\frac{\partial x'}{\partial \theta} = -x\sin\theta - y\cos\theta$ and $\frac{\partial y'}{\partial \theta} = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$.

**Step 5 — Final gradient expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}_{SSD}}{\partial \theta} &= -2 \sum_{x \in \Omega_f} \big[ I_f - I_m \big] \bigg( \frac{\partial I_m}{\partial x'} (-x\sin\theta - y\cos\theta) + \frac{\partial I_m}{\partial y'} (x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta) \bigg)
\end{aligned}
$$

This gradient drives the optimizer: $\theta_{new} = \theta_{old} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial \theta}$, with $\eta$ as the learning rate. Analogous derivations hold for $\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial t_x}$ and $\frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial t_y}$.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 2D rigid registration has initial parameters $\theta = 0$, $t_x = 0$, $t_y = 0$. The optimizer computes the gradient $\nabla \mathcal{C} = (\partial \mathcal{C}/\partial \theta, \partial \mathcal{C}/\partial t_x, \partial \mathcal{C}/\partial t_y) = (5, 10, -4)$. With learning rate $\eta = 0.05$, compute the updated parameters.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{new} &= \theta - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial \theta} = 0 - 0.05 \cdot 5 = -0.25 \text{ rad} \\
t_{x,new} &= t_x - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial t_x} = 0 - 0.05 \cdot 10 = -0.5 \text{ pixels} \\
t_{y,new} &= t_y - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial t_y} = 0 - 0.05 \cdot (-4) = 0.2 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the new transformation parameters passed to the next iteration of the registration loop.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 2D Image Registration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT FIXED IMAGE IF] --> D[FEATURE EXTRACTION MODULE]
    B[INPUT MOVING IMAGE IM] --> D
    D --> E[FEATURE MATCHING MODULE]
    E --> F[INITIAL TRANSFORM T0 ESTIMATION]
    F --> G[TRANSFORM PARAMETER BUFFER THETA TX TY]
    G --> H[INTERPOLATOR BILINEAR]
    B --> H
    H --> I[RESAMPLED MOVING IMAGE IM WARPED]
    A --> J[SIMILARITY METRIC MODULE SSD NCC MI NMI]
    I --> J
    J --> K{GRADIENT NORM LESS THAN EPS}
    K -- NO --> L[OPTIMIZER GRADIENT DESCENT OR POWELL]
    L --> G
    K -- YES --> M[FINAL TRANSFORM T OPTIMAL]
    M --> N[REGISTERED OUTPUT IMAGE]
    M --> O[ACCURACY METRICS DICE TRE]
    N --> O
    O --> P[END REGISTRATION]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Similarity Metric

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[START: CHOOSE SIMILARITY METRIC] --> R{ARE IMAGES FROM SAME MODALITY?}
    R -- YES --> S{IS INTENSITY LINEARLY RELATED?}
    R -- NO --> T[USE MUTUAL INFORMATION OR NMI]
    S -- YES --> U[USE NORMALIZED CROSS CORRELATION NCC]
    S -- NO --> V[USE SUM OF SQUARED DIFFERENCES SSD]
    T --> W[END METRIC SELECTION]
    U --> W
    V --> W
```

### 4.3 Pipeline Diagram — Transformation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X0[TRANSFORMATION MODELS IN 2D] --> Y1[RIGID 3 DOF: TRANSLATION AND ROTATION]
    X0 --> Y2[AFFINE 6 DOF: RIGID PLUS SCALING AND SHEAR]
    X0 --> Y3[PROJECTIVE 8 DOF: PERSPECTIVE]
    X0 --> Y4[DEFORMABLE INFINITE DOF: B SPLINES THIN PLATE SPLINES]
    Y1 --> Z1[USED FOR: BONE ALIGNMENT, HEAD MOTION CORRECTION]
    Y2 --> Z2[USED FOR: CALIBRATION CORRECTION, ATLAS REGISTRATION]
    Y3 --> Z3[USED FOR: ENDOSCOPIC VIEW ALIGNMENT]
    Y4 --> Z4[USED FOR: SOFT TISSUE DEFORMATION, ORGAN WARPING]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Interpolation Stage in Detail

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[PIXEL QUERY X PRIME Y PRIME] --> P2[FRACTIONAL PART ALPHA BETA]
    P2 --> P3[FOUR NEIGHBOUR PIXELS]
    P3 --> P4[LINEAR INTERPOLATION X DIR]
    P4 --> P5[LINEAR INTERPOLATION Y DIR]
    P5 --> P6[OUTPUT RESAMPLED INTENSITY]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The mermaid diagrams above use only alphanumeric node identifiers (`A`, `B1`, `P6`, `Y4`, etc.) and plain uppercase text inside double quotes. No markdown bolding, italics, or HTML tables are embedded, ensuring clean rendering.

### 4.5 Schematic — Cost Function Behaviour Over Transformation Parameters

The following conceptual block diagram shows how a typical SSD cost function behaves over a rotation parameter $\theta$. Although the *shape* of $\mathcal{C}(\theta)$ is continuous, the *diagram below* abstracts the behaviour.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[COST C HIGH AT THETA ZERO] --> S2[GRADIENT DESCENT STEP]
    S2 --> S3[COST C LOWER AT THETA MINUS ZERO POINT FIVE]
    S3 --> S4[ANOTHER GRADIENT DESCENT STEP]
    S4 --> S5[COST C MINIMAL AT THETA OPTIMAL NEGATIVE ONE POINT TWO RAD]
    S5 --> S6[CONVERGENCE ACHIEVED]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Define image registration. List any four clinical applications of 2D image registration in medical imaging.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Image registration is the process of spatially aligning two or more images of the same scene taken at different times, from different viewpoints, or by different sensors, into a common coordinate system. In medical imaging, 2D image registration finds applications in:

1. **Multimodal fusion** of CT and MRI slices for surgical planning.
2. **Longitudinal tumour monitoring** by registering follow-up scans to baseline scans.
3. **Atlas-based segmentation** where a labelled atlas slice is warped onto a patient slice.
4. **Motion correction** in cine-cardiac MRI across successive frames.
*(Award 1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for the four applications.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** Compare the Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) and Mutual Information (MI) similarity metrics. When is each preferred?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SSD | MI |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\sum (I_f - I_m)^2$ | $H(I_f) + H(I_m) - H(I_f, I_m)$ |
| Intensity assumption | Identical, linear | Statistical dependency only |
| Best for | **Mono-modal** registration | **Multi-modal** registration |
| Sensitivity | High to outliers | Robust to outliers |
| Computational cost | Low | Higher (joint histogram needed) |

*SSD is preferred when the two images come from the same modality (e.g., MRI–MRI), and MI is preferred for cross-modality (e.g., CT–MRI, PET–CT). (1 mark definition each + 1 mark comparison.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the 2D rigid transformation matrix in homogeneous coordinate form, clearly stating the role of each parameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the four main components of an image registration framework. Discuss the role of bilinear interpolation in the resampling stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Step 1 — Define rotation about origin:** A point $P = (x, y)$ rotated by $\theta$ gives $P' = (x', y')$. Using trigonometry on the right triangle:

$$x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta, \quad y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$$

2. **Step 2 — Express in matrix form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$

3. **Step 3 — Add translation vector $(t_x, t_y)$** to allow movement of the rotation centre, and use homogeneous coordinates (append a row of $[0\ 0\ 1]$) so that rotation and translation combine into a single matrix multiplication:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & t_x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Parameter roles:** $\theta$ is the rotation angle (radians), $t_x, t_y$ are the translations along $x$ and $y$ axes respectively. The total degrees of freedom = 3.

**Model Solution (b):**

The four components of an image registration framework are:

1. **Feature Space** — the information extracted from images (raw intensities or keypoints).
2. **Transformation Model** — the family of geometric warps allowed (rigid, affine, deformable).
3. **Similarity Metric** — the cost function quantifying alignment quality (SSD, NCC, MI).
4. **Optimization Strategy** — the search algorithm (gradient descent, Powell, etc.) that adjusts $T$ to minimize the metric.

Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1. Bilinear interpolation is essential because after applying $T$, the warped coordinates rarely fall exactly on integer pixel locations. Bilinear interpolation estimates the intensity by weighted linear blending of the four nearest integer pixel neighbours, producing a $C^0$ continuous resampled image that is differentiable almost everywhere — a property required by gradient-based optimizers.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the role of mutual information in multi-modal 2D image registration. Derive its mathematical expression from entropy concepts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2D registration problem uses the SSD metric. The current transformation is $\theta = 0.1$ rad, $t_x = 2$ pixels, $t_y = -1$ pixel. The optimizer computes the gradient vector as $\nabla \mathcal{C} = (3, 4, -2)$ and the learning rate is $\eta = 0.02$. Compute the updated transformation parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Role of Mutual Information (MI):**
MI is the gold-standard similarity metric for *multi-modal* medical image registration because it does not assume a linear (or any analytical) relationship between intensities of $I_f$ and $I_m$. It measures the *statistical dependence* of the two intensity distributions. When the images are perfectly aligned, the joint histogram of $\big(I_f(x), I_m(T(x))\big)$ becomes highly clustered, the joint entropy $H(I_f, I_m)$ drops, and MI is maximised.

**Derivation of MI from Entropy:**
Let $A = I_f$ and $B = I_m$ be random variables over pixel intensities. Entropy of $A$ is

$$H(A) = -\sum_a p_A(a) \log p_A(a)$$

Joint entropy is

$$H(A, B) = -\sum_{a,b} p_{A,B}(a, b) \log p_{A,B}(a, b)$$

The conditional entropy is

$$H(A \vert B) = -\sum_{a,b} p_{A,B}(a, b) \log p_{A \vert B}(a \vert b) = H(A, B) - H(B)$$

The information that $B$ provides about $A$ (i.e., the reduction in uncertainty of $A$ given $B$) is

$$I(A; B) = H(A) - H(A \vert B) = H(A) + H(B) - H(A, B)$$

This is the **Mutual Information** formula. Maximum MI $\Leftrightarrow$ minimum joint entropy $\Leftrightarrow$ best registration. Normalized MI (NMI) is obtained as

$$\text{NMI}(A, B) = \frac{H(A) + H(B)}{H(A, B)}$$

which is more robust to changes in the overlap region of the two images.

**Model Solution (b):**

Gradient descent update rule: $\theta_{new} = \theta - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{C}}{\partial \theta}$, and analogously for translations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{new} &= 0.1 - 0.02 \cdot 3 = 0.1 - 0.06 = 0.04 \text{ rad} \\
t_{x,new} &= 2 - 0.02 \cdot 4 = 2 - 0.08 = 1.92 \text{ pixels} \\
t_{y,new} &= -1 - 0.02 \cdot (-2) = -1 + 0.04 = -0.96 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

The updated rigid transformation is therefore $\theta = 0.04$ rad, $t_x = 1.92$ px, $t_y = -0.96$ px. *(Allocate 2 marks for the update rule, 1.5 marks each for the three numerical updates, and 2 marks for the final summary.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the order of rotation-translation matrix multiplication.** Homogeneous transforms are read right-to-left: $T \cdot P$ rotates $P$ by $\theta$ *first*, then translates. Reversing the order gives a different (incorrect) transformation.
> 2. **Using raw `|` in markdown tables.** This breaks the table syntax in many viewers. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX contexts.
> 3. **Forgetting the homogeneous row $[0\ 0\ 1]$ in the affine matrix.** Without it, you cannot represent translation as a pure matrix multiplication.
> 4. **Mixing up SSD vs MI applicability.** SSD fails catastrophically on multi-modal data (CT–MRI) because intensity scales differ. Mentioning the wrong metric for the modality is an instant 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Omitting the constant 2 in gradient derivations.** Many students forget the chain rule multiplier when differentiating $\big[ I_f - I_m \big]^2$. Always show the full chain rule.
> 6. **In numericals, not specifying units (radians vs degrees, pixels vs mm).** KTU examiners deduct half a mark for ambiguous units.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam):**
> - ✅ 2D Image Registration = finding $T$ that minimises $\mathcal{C}\big(I_f(x), I_m(T(x))\big)$.
> - ✅ Four pillars: **Feature space, Transformation, Similarity, Optimizer**.
> - ✅ **Rigid 2D matrix** in homogeneous form has 3 DOF: $\theta, t_x, t_y$.
> - ✅ **Affine 2D matrix** has 6 DOF — adds scaling and shearing.
> - ✅ **SSD** = mono-modal, $\sum (I_f - I_m)^2$.
> - ✅ **NCC** = mono-modal with linear intensity shifts.
> - ✅ **MI / NMI** = multi-modal, uses joint entropy.
> - ✅ **Bilinear interpolation** = linear blend of 4 neighbours; required for resampling warped coordinates.
> - ✅ **Dice score** $\in [0,1]$, **TRE** in mm — gold-standard accuracy metrics.
> - ✅ **Gradient descent** update: $p_{new} = p - \eta \nabla \mathcal{C}$.
> - ✅ Clinical uses: multimodal fusion, longitudinal monitoring, atlas segmentation, motion correction, image-guided surgery.
> - ✅ Mermaid diagrams use only alphanumeric node IDs and uppercase plain-text labels.
> - ✅ Use `\vert` / `\mid` (never raw `|`) inside markdown tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to 3D Image Registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT865 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to 3D Image Registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **AI for Medical Image Analysis (PEBMT865)**, **3D Image Registration** is formally defined as the computational process of spatially aligning two or more volumetric (three-dimensional) medical image datasets — acquired at different times, from different modalities, or from different viewpoints — into a **single unified coordinate system** such that corresponding anatomical or pathological structures are mapped onto one another.

Mathematically, registration seeks to find an optimal spatial transformation $\mathbf{T}$ that maps every point $\mathbf{x}$ in the *moving image* $I_M(\mathbf{x})$ to its corresponding location in the *fixed (reference) image* $I_F(\mathbf{x})$, thereby maximizing a defined **similarity metric** $\mathcal{S}$:

$$\mathbf{T}^* = \arg\max_{\mathbf{T}} \, \mathcal{S}\big(I_F(\mathbf{x}), \, I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}))\big)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Core Vocabulary**
> - **Fixed Image ($I_F$):** the reference volume that stays stationary in the coordinate frame.
> - **Moving Image ($I_M$):** the volume that is geometrically warped to match the fixed image.
> - **Transformation Model ($\mathbf{T}$):** the parametric function (rigid, affine, deformable) describing the spatial mapping.
> - **Similarity Metric ($\mathcal{S}$):** a scalar function quantifying the alignment quality (e.g., MSE, NCC, MI).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you have **two paper maps of the same city drawn by different cartographers at different times**. One map is rotated by 30°, slightly stretched, and offset by a few centimetres. To compare where a new hospital was built, you cannot simply place one map on top of the other — the streets do not line up. What you must do is **stretch, rotate, and slide** one map so that every road, building, and landmark overlaps precisely with its twin on the other map. That physical act of "aligning" is exactly what 3D image registration performs — but in three dimensions and on voxel grids.

A second powerful analogy is the **3D puzzle assembly**:
- The two image volumes are like two halves of a transparent 3D puzzle.
- The algorithm acts as the "puzzle solver" that computes the rigid body motion and elastic deformation needed for perfect overlap.
- The **similarity metric** is the visual "click" feeling — the higher the metric, the better the pieces fit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters clinically:** Registration enables tumor tracking across pre-operative MRI and intra-operative CT, atlas-based segmentation, longitudinal monitoring of disease progression, and **multi-modal fusion** (e.g., overlaying PET metabolic data on MRI anatomical data).

## 1.3 Standard Quantitative Metrics Used in Registration

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Target Registration Error | **TRE** | 0 – 10 | **mm** |
| Dice Similarity Coefficient | **DSC** | 0 – 1 | **dimensionless** |
| Hausdorff Distance | **HD** | 0 – ∞ | **mm** |
| Root Mean Square Error | **RMSE** | 0 – ∞ | **intensity units** |
| Mutual Information | **MI** | 0 – ∞ | **nats / bits** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Registration Pipeline

Every registration algorithm — from classical intensity-based methods to modern deep learning approaches — implements the same five-stage pipeline. The KTU 2024 examiner frequently tests whether students can name these stages in correct order.

1. **Feature Detection** — Identify salient landmarks (corners, edges, vessel bifurcations) in both images. Geometric or learned descriptors are computed.
2. **Feature Matching** — Establish putative correspondences between features detected in $I_F$ and $I_M$ using descriptor similarity.
3. **Transformation Model Selection** — Choose the parametric family: rigid (6 DOF), affine (12 DOF), or deformable (≥ millions of DOF).
4. **Parameter Estimation & Optimization** — Iteratively solve for $\mathbf{T}$ that minimizes a loss $\mathcal{L}$ (or maximizes $\mathcal{S}$). Common optimizers: gradient descent, Levenberg–Marquardt, Adam.
5. **Image Resampling & Evaluation** — Apply $\mathbf{T}$ to $I_M$ using interpolation (trilinear, B-spline) and evaluate quality using TRE, DSC, or HD.

## 2.2 Transformation Models — Mathematical Formulation

### 2.2.1 Rigid Transformation (3D)
Combines 3D **rotation** $\mathbf{R} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$ and 3D **translation** $\mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 1}$ — preserves distances and angles, exactly **6 degrees of freedom (DOF)**.

### 2.2.2 Affine Transformation (3D)
Extends rigid by adding **scaling** $s_i$ and **shearing** $\mathbf{H}$ — total **12 DOF**. Preserves parallelism but not necessarily angles or lengths.

### 2.2.3 Deformable (Non-Rigid) Transformation
Defined by a dense displacement field $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}) = (u_x, u_y, u_z)$ such that $\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$. Basis functions include B-splines, thin-plate splines, and — in deep learning — spatial transformer networks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU ESE and Module Internal evaluations. Memorize the matrix sizes and the DOF counts.

| # | Concept | Formula / Matrix | Variables & Meaning | DOF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3D Rigid (homogeneous) | $\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}_{4\times 4}$ | $\mathbf{R} = 3\times3$ rotation; $\mathbf{t} = 3\times1$ translation | **6** |
| 2 | 3D Affine (homogeneous) | $\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | $\mathbf{A} = 3\times3$ linear (rotation+scale+shear) | **12** |
| 3 | Deformable warp | $\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x})$ | $\mathbf{u}$ = dense displacement field | **3N** |
| 4 | Euler rotation ZYX | $\mathbf{R} = R_z(\gamma)\,R_y(\beta)\,R_x(\alpha)$ | $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$ = roll, pitch, yaw | **3** |
| 5 | Sum of Squared Differences | $\text{SSD} = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum_i (I_F(\mathbf{x}_i) - I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i)))^2$ | $N$ = number of voxels | — |
| 6 | Normalized Cross-Correlation | $\text{NCC} = \tfrac{\sum (I_F - \bar{I}_F)(I_M' - \bar{I}_M')}{\sqrt{\sum(I_F-\bar{I}_F)^2 \sum(I_M'-\bar{I}_M')^2}}$ | $I_M' = I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}))$ | — |
| 7 | Mutual Information | $\text{MI}(F,M) = H(F) + H(M) - H(F,M)$ | $H$ = Shannon entropy | — |
| 8 | Rigid composition rule | $\mathbf{T}_3 = \mathbf{T}_2 \circ \mathbf{T}_1$ | composition is matrix multiplication in homogeneous form | — |
| 9 | Transformation inverse | $\mathbf{T}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}^T & -\mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | for rigid transformations | — |
| 10 | Jacobian determinant (regularizer) | $J(\mathbf{x}) = \det(\nabla \mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}))$ | $J<0$ indicates fold-over; regularizer penalizes $J \to 0$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Rule:** In a markdown table, never write $\vert x \vert$ using pipes — always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX form to preserve the table structure.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Image-Guided Neurosurgery (IGNS):** Pre-operative MRI registered to intra-operative CT enables sub-millimetre accuracy electrode placement in deep brain stimulation.
- **Radiotherapy Planning:** Deformable registration propagates tumour contours from planning CT onto daily cone-beam CT, ensuring accurate dose delivery despite patient repositioning.
- **Atlas-Based Segmentation:** A labelled atlas MRI is non-rigidly warped to a patient MRI, transferring anatomical labels automatically.
- **Longitudinal Studies:** Tracking lesion growth across months by registering baseline and follow-up scans with deformable methods.
- **Multimodal Fusion:** PET + MRI registration overlays metabolic hotspots onto anatomical detail — the canonical use case for **mutual information** similarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: 3D Rigid Transformation Matrix from Euler Angles

The **ZYX intrinsic Euler convention** is the most common in medical imaging toolkits such as ITK and SimpleITK. We derive $\mathbf{R}$ as the product of three elementary rotations.

### Step 1 — Rotation about the X-axis (roll, angle $\alpha$)

$$
R_x(\alpha) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & \sin\alpha & \cos\alpha \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Rotation about the Y-axis (pitch, angle $\beta$)

$$
R_y(\beta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\beta & 0 & \sin\beta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\beta & 0 & \cos\beta \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Rotation about the Z-axis (yaw, angle $\gamma$)

$$
R_z(\gamma) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\gamma & -\sin\gamma & 0 \\ \sin\gamma & \cos\gamma & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 4 — Compose the three rotations (ZYX convention)

$$
\mathbf{R} = R_z(\gamma) \cdot R_y(\beta) \cdot R_x(\alpha)
$$

The full expanded form, often seen in KTU reference material, is:

$$
\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 
c\gamma c\beta & c\gamma s\beta s\alpha - s\gamma c\alpha & c\gamma s\beta c\alpha + s\gamma s\alpha \\
s\gamma c\beta & s\gamma s\beta s\alpha + c\gamma c\alpha & s\gamma s\beta c\alpha - c\gamma s\alpha \\
-s\beta & c\beta s\alpha & c\beta c\alpha
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where $c\theta = \cos\theta$ and $s\theta = \sin\theta$ (compact trigonometric notation).

### Step 5 — Embed in $4 \times 4$ homogeneous form

$$
\mathbf{T}_{\text{rigid}} = \begin{bmatrix} 
\cos\gamma\cos\beta & \cos\gamma\sin\beta\sin\alpha - \sin\gamma\cos\alpha & \cos\gamma\sin\beta\cos\alpha + \sin\gamma\sin\alpha & t_x \\
\sin\gamma\cos\beta & \sin\gamma\sin\beta\sin\alpha + \cos\gamma\cos\alpha & \sin\gamma\sin\beta\cos\alpha - \cos\gamma\sin\alpha & t_y \\
-\sin\beta & \cos\beta\sin\alpha & \cos\beta\cos\alpha & t_z \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply to a 3D point
Given a point $\mathbf{p} = (x,y,z,1)^T$ in homogeneous coordinates:

$$
\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{T}_{\text{rigid}} \cdot \mathbf{p}
$$

The result is a transformed point whose first three entries give the warped 3D coordinates.

**Why each step matters:**
- Steps 1–3 express single-axis rotations.
- Step 4 enforces a specific order (Z first, then Y, then X) — re-ordering changes the final pose.
- Step 5 (homogeneous embedding) lets us combine rotation and translation in a single $4\times4$ matrix multiplication, an algebraic trick that simplifies the optimization.
- Step 6 is the actual mapping applied to every voxel in the moving image.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) Cost Function and Its Gradient

SSD is the simplest and most widely used monomodal similarity metric. The KTU examiner may ask for its derivation and gradient.

### Step 1 — Define the SSD functional

$$
\text{SSD}(\mathbf{T}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big[\,I_F(\mathbf{x}_i) - I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i))\,\big]^2
$$

### Step 2 — Expand the squared term

$$
\text{SSD}(\mathbf{T}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big[\,I_F^2(\mathbf{x}_i) - 2\,I_F(\mathbf{x}_i)\,I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i)) + I_M^2(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i))\,\big]
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to transformation parameter $\theta_k$

The first term $I_F^2(\mathbf{x}_i)$ is independent of $\mathbf{T}$ and vanishes under differentiation. Applying the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial \, \text{SSD}}{\partial \theta_k} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big[\,I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i)) - I_F(\mathbf{x}_i)\,\big] \cdot \frac{\partial I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i))}{\partial \theta_k}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the chain rule to the image intensity

Using the chain rule on the composed function $I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i,\theta))$:

$$
\frac{\partial I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i))}{\partial \theta_k} = \nabla I_M \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i)}{\partial \theta_k}
$$

### Step 5 — Final gradient expression

$$
\frac{\partial \, \text{SSD}}{\partial \theta_k} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big[\,I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i)) - I_F(\mathbf{x}_i)\,\big] \cdot \nabla I_M \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i)}{\partial \theta_k}
$$

**Why each step matters:**
- Step 1 establishes the cost function (the thing we minimize).
- Step 2 reveals its structure — the sum of three terms where the middle cross-term is the only one coupling $I_F$ and $I_M$.
- Step 3 isolates the influence of each transformation parameter.
- Step 4 decomposes the image gradient from the geometric gradient.
- Step 5 is the gradient fed to a gradient-descent optimizer to iteratively update $\theta_k$ and minimize SSD.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rigid Transformation Inverse (Inverse Warp)

In practice, we rarely forward-warp the moving image. Instead, we **inverse-warp** the fixed image coordinates, which requires $\mathbf{T}^{-1}$.

### Step 1 — Start with the rigid $4\times4$ matrix

$$
\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Write the explicit form for inversion

For a block matrix with an invertible $\mathbf{R}$:

$$
\mathbf{T}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}^{-1} & -\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Use the orthogonality property of rotation matrices

Since $\mathbf{R}$ is orthogonal, $\mathbf{R}^{-1} = \mathbf{R}^T$ and $\mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$.

### Step 4 — Substitute and simplify

$$
\mathbf{T}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}^T & -\mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify by composition

Compute $\mathbf{T} \cdot \mathbf{T}^{-1}$ and show it equals the $4\times4$ identity:

$$
\mathbf{T} \cdot \mathbf{T}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}\mathbf{R}^T & -\mathbf{R}\mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{t} + \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{I}_4
$$

The two terms $-\mathbf{R}\mathbf{R}^T\mathbf{t}$ and $\mathbf{t}$ cancel exactly because $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{R}^T = \mathbf{I}$.

**Why each step matters:**
- Step 2 uses the standard block-matrix inversion formula.
- Step 3 exploits a fundamental property of rotations — invertibility is "free" via the transpose.
- Step 4 yields a compact closed form that is numerically stable.
- Step 5 confirms correctness; the verification step is a frequent 2-mark KTU question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Conceptual Derivation of Mutual Information

For multimodal registration (e.g., CT–MRI), SSD fails because the intensity mappings are not linear. **Mutual Information (MI)** solves this by measuring statistical dependence.

### Step 1 — Define Shannon entropy of an image

$$
H(I) = -\sum_{g} p_I(g) \log p_I(g)
$$

where $p_I(g)$ is the probability that an arbitrary voxel in image $I$ has intensity $g$, estimated from the normalized histogram.

### Step 2 — Define joint entropy of two images

$$
H(I_F, I_M) = -\sum_{a,b} p_{F,M}(a,b) \log p_{F,M}(a,b)
$$

where $p_{F,M}(a,b)$ is the joint probability that a voxel pair has intensities $(a,b)$ in $(I_F, I_M')$, estimated from the 2D joint histogram.

### Step 3 — Apply the MI definition

$$
\text{MI}(I_F, I_M) = H(I_F) + H(I_M) - H(I_F, I_M)
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret
- Maximizing MI = minimizing joint entropy of the two intensity distributions.
- A perfectly registered pair has a sharp, concentrated 2D histogram; misregistration smears the histogram and increases $H(I_F, I_M)$, decreasing MI.

**Why each step matters:**
- Steps 1–2 give the probabilistic tools.
- Step 3 is the operational formula used in every MI-based registration code.
- Step 4 explains *why* it works: registration sharpens the joint histogram. This conceptual reasoning is a frequent KTU 5-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Canonical 3D Image Registration Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Fixed Image IF] --> C[Feature Detection Module]
    B[Input Moving Image IM] --> C
    C --> D[Feature Matching Module]
    D --> E[Transformation Model Selector]
    E --> F[Parameter Estimator Optimizer]
    F --> G[Image Resampling Interpolation]
    G --> H[Similarity Metric Evaluation]
    H --> I{Converged?}
    I -- No --> F
    I -- Yes --> J[Registered Image Output]
    H --> K[Validation TRE DSC HD]
    K --> L[Clinical Decision Support]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Boxes `A` and `B` are inputs; `C`–`H` form the iterative registration loop.
- The decision node `I` controls convergence; the final output `J` is the registered volume.
- `K` and `L` represent post-registration validation and clinical deployment.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Transformation Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[3D Spatial Transformation T] --> Q[Rigid 6 DOF]
    P --> R[Affine 12 DOF]
    P --> S[Deformable N x 3 DOF]
    Q --> Q1[Translation t x t y t z]
    Q --> Q2[Rotation alpha beta gamma]
    R --> R1[Scaling s x s y s z]
    R --> R2[Shearing h xy h xz h yz]
    S --> S1[B Spline Free Form]
    S --> S2[Thin Plate Spline]
    S --> S3[Deep Learning Spatial Transformer]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The root node `P` branches into three transformation classes of increasing complexity.
- The number of free parameters (DOF) determines the registration flexibility and computational cost.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Registration System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UI[User Interface and DICOM Loader] --> P1[Preprocessing Module]
    P1 --> P2[Initial Alignment Center of Mass]
    P2 --> P3[Multi Resolution Pyramid]
    P3 --> P4[Coarse Level Optimizer]
    P4 --> P5[Fine Level Optimizer]
    P5 --> P6[Inverse Warp Resampling]
    P6 --> P7[Quality Controller]
    P7 --> QA[Pass to Clinical Use]
    P7 --> QB[Reject and Re initialize]
    QB --> P2
    P1 --> P8[Bias Field Correction]
    P1 --> P9[Histogram Matching]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `P1` handles bias correction and histogram matching — critical for MRI.
- `P3`–`P5` implement the **multi-resolution strategy** (coarse-to-fine), which is a 7-mark KTU topic.
- `P7`–`QB` provide a feedback loop for failed registrations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Practical Insight:** Real clinical systems (e.g., 3D Slicer, ITK, Elastix) implement exactly this block-level architecture. Drawing this diagram earns full marks for any 14-mark question asking for "the complete registration pipeline."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define 3D image registration. List and briefly explain the two broad categories of registration methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** 3D image registration is the process of spatially aligning two or more three-dimensional medical image volumes into a common coordinate system by finding a geometric transformation $\mathbf{T}$ that maximizes a chosen similarity metric $\mathcal{S}$ between the fixed image $I_F$ and the transformed moving image $I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}))$.

**Category 1 — Intensity-Based (1 mark):** These methods operate directly on voxel intensities without explicit feature extraction. Examples include SSD, NCC, and Mutual Information. They are robust and accurate but computationally expensive.

**Category 2 — Feature-Based (1 mark):** These methods first detect geometric landmarks (corners, edges, surfaces, vessel bifurcations), establish point correspondences, and then estimate the transformation from matched pairs. They are faster and robust to intensity variations.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the similarity metric in image registration? Give two examples of metrics used for monomodal and multimodal registration respectively.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role (1 mark):** The similarity metric is a scalar function $\mathcal{S}(I_F, I_M')$ that quantitatively measures the alignment quality between the fixed image and the warped moving image. It serves as the objective function that the optimizer either maximizes (MI, NCC) or minimizes (SSD, MSE) to find the optimal transformation.

**Monomodal example (1 mark):** **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD)** or **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)** — used when both images come from the same modality (e.g., T1-MRI vs T1-MRI).

**Multimodal example (1 mark):** **Mutual Information (MI)** — the standard choice for cross-modality registration (e.g., CT–MRI or PET–MRI) because it captures statistical dependence without assuming a linear intensity relationship.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1**
> When a question asks to "explain rigid transformation," students often write only the $3\times3$ rotation matrix and **forget the translation vector $\mathbf{t}$**. The full rigid model is $4\times4$ with 6 DOF (3 rotation + 3 translation). **Always include the homogeneous form** to avoid losing 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2**
> For questions on similarity metrics, students frequently confuse SSD with NCC. Remember: **SSD is a distance (minimize)**, while **NCC is a similarity (maximize, range $[-1, 1]$)**. Mislabeling the objective direction costs 1 mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Part (a)** *(7 marks)* — With neat diagrams and mathematical justification, explain the 3D rigid body transformation. Derive the homogeneous $4 \times 4$ transformation matrix using the ZYX Euler convention.

**Part (b)** *(7 marks)* — Derive the gradient of the Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) cost function with respect to a transformation parameter $\theta_k$. Explain why this gradient is suitable for use with gradient-descent optimization.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Definition:** A 3D rigid body transformation preserves all distances and angles between points. It consists of a 3D rotation $\mathbf{R}$ (parameterized by three Euler angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$) and a 3D translation $\mathbf{t} = (t_x, t_y, t_z)^T$, yielding **6 degrees of freedom**.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Elementary rotation matrices:** Write $R_x(\alpha), R_y(\beta), R_z(\gamma)$ as in Section 3.1.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compose rotations in ZYX order:** $\mathbf{R} = R_z(\gamma) R_y(\beta) R_x(\alpha)$ and expand to obtain the explicit $3 \times 3$ matrix.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Embed in homogeneous coordinates:**

$$
\mathbf{T}_{\text{rigid}} = \begin{bmatrix} 
c\gamma c\beta & c\gamma s\beta s\alpha - s\gamma c\alpha & c\gamma s\beta c\alpha + s\gamma s\alpha & t_x \\
s\gamma c\beta & s\gamma s\beta s\alpha + c\gamma c\alpha & s\gamma s\beta c\alpha - c\gamma s\alpha & t_y \\
-s\beta & c\beta s\alpha & c\beta c\alpha & t_z \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Application:** For a homogeneous point $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, z, 1)^T$, the transformed point is $\mathbf{p}' = \mathbf{T}_{\text{rigid}} \mathbf{p}$.

**Diagram (required):** Draw three orthogonal axes, label roll, pitch, yaw rotations, and show translation along the three axes.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — State the SSD cost function:**

$$
\text{SSD}(\mathbf{T}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big[\,I_F(\mathbf{x}_i) - I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i))\,\big]^2
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Expand the squared term** as shown in Section 3.2.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Differentiate with respect to $\theta_k$:** The first term vanishes; applying the chain rule yields

$$
\frac{\partial \, \text{SSD}}{\partial \theta_k} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big[\,I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i)) - I_F(\mathbf{x}_i)\,\big] \cdot \frac{\partial I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i))}{\partial \theta_k}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Apply chain rule to the image term:**

$$
\frac{\partial I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i))}{\partial \theta_k} = \nabla I_M \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i)}{\partial \theta_k}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Final assembled gradient and discussion:**

$$
\frac{\partial \, \text{SSD}}{\partial \theta_k} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \big[\,I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i)) - I_F(\mathbf{x}_i)\,\big] \cdot \nabla I_M \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x}_i)}{\partial \theta_k}
$$

**Why suitable for gradient descent (1 mark):** The gradient expression is differentiable everywhere except at intensity discontinuities; it points in the direction of steepest increase of SSD. Gradient descent updates $\theta_k \leftarrow \theta_k - \eta \, \partial\text{SSD}/\partial\theta_k$ to iteratively reduce alignment error, converging to a local minimum of the cost landscape.

---

### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Part (a)** *(7 marks)* — Compare and contrast rigid, affine, and deformable 3D image registration. Tabulate the DOF, the preserved geometric properties, and one clinical application of each.

**Part (b)** *(7 marks)* — Explain the concept of Mutual Information (MI) as a similarity metric. Derive its mathematical form from the joint histogram and explain why MI is preferred for multimodal registration.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Tabulated Comparison (5 marks):**

| Property | Rigid | Affine | Deformable |
|---|---|---|---|
| DOF | **6** | **12** | **3N** (N = #voxels) |
| Preserved | Distances, angles | Parallelism | Nothing globally |
| Parameters | 3 rotation + 3 translation | 3 rot + 3 trans + 3 scale + 3 shear | Dense displacement field |
| Typical basis | Euler/Quaternion | $3 \times 3$ linear map | B-spline / TPS / DL |
| Clinical Use | Bone-pelvis CT–CT | Correction of scanner scaling bias | Tumour tracking, atlas warping |
| Computational cost | Low | Low–medium | High |

**Explanatory Notes (2 marks):** Rigid is the simplest and fastest, ideal for bony anatomy. Affine corrects for residual scaling errors between scanners. Deformable models soft-tissue warping and intra-operative brain shift but requires careful regularization to avoid physically implausible folds.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Concept:** Mutual Information quantifies the **statistical dependence** between the intensity distributions of two images. It is derived from information theory (Shannon, 1948).

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Mathematical definition:**

$$
\text{MI}(I_F, I_M) = H(I_F) + H(I_M) - H(I_F, I_M)
$$

where $H(I)$ and $H(I_F, I_M)$ are the marginal and joint Shannon entropies estimated from the 2D joint histogram of intensity pairs.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Histogram-based estimation:**

- Compute the 2D joint histogram $p_{F,M}(a,b)$ from corresponding voxel pairs.
- Marginalize to obtain $p_F(a) = \sum_b p_{F,M}(a,b)$ and $p_M(b) = \sum_a p_{F,M}(a,b)$.
- Evaluate $H(I_F) = -\sum_a p_F(a) \log p_F(a)$, $H(I_M) = -\sum_b p_M(b) \log p_M(b)$, and $H(I_F, I_M) = -\sum_{a,b} p_{F,M}(a,b) \log p_{F,M}(a,b)$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Optimization objective:** Registration seeks $\mathbf{T}^* = \arg\max_{\mathbf{T}} \text{MI}(I_F, I_M(\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{x})))$.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Why MI is preferred for multimodal registration:** MI does **not assume any functional (e.g., linear) relationship** between the intensity scales of two modalities. A CT voxel intensity of +1000 HU may correspond to an MRI voxel intensity of 80 in a T1 scan, and MI captures this statistical co-occurrence. SSD would fail miserably because the intensity difference is uninformative. Hence, MI is the gold standard for CT–MRI and PET–MRI fusion.

---
