## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Master Inspection Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete KTU Module 4 inspection workflow**, from the generator output to the final clinical validation report. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with Mermaid safeguards. Labels contain clean uppercase text only.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input: Real Medical Image Set I_real] --> B0[Step 1: Pre-trained Inception-V3 Feature Extractor]
    A1[Input: Generated Image Set I_gen] --> B0
    B0 --> C1{Pillar 1: Quantitative Statistical Inspection}
    B0 --> C2{Pillar 2: Pixel Level Reconstruction Inspection}
    A1 --> C3{Pillar 3: Clinical Anatomical Validity Inspection}
    A1 --> C4{Pillar 4: Downstream Task Inspection}

    C1 --> D1[Compute mu_r, Sigma_r for I_real]
    C1 --> D2[Compute mu_g, Sigma_g for I_gen]
    D1 --> E1[Output: FID Score]
    D2 --> E1
    D1 --> E2[Output: IS Score via softmax p(y|x)]
    D2 --> E2

    C2 --> F1[Compute MSE per pixel]
    F1 --> F2[Output: PSNR in dB]
    F1 --> F3[Compute local mu, sigma on 11x11 windows]
    F3 --> F4[Output: Mean SSIM]

    C3 --> G1[Expert Radiologist Likert Scoring 1 to 5]
    C3 --> G2[Automated nnU-Net Segmentation Consistency Check]
    G1 --> G3[Output: Clinical Validity Score]
    G2 --> G3

    C4 --> H1[Train Classifier Segmentor on I_real UNION I_gen]
    H1 --> H2[Evaluate on Held-out Real Test Set]
    H2 --> H3[Output: Dice Score, AUC, Sensitivity]

    E1 --> Z0[Final 4D Quality Vector Q = F, D, V, U]
    E2 --> Z0
    F2 --> Z0
    F4 --> Z0
    G3 --> Z0
    H3 --> Z0
    Z0 --> Z1[Decision: DEPLOY or REJECT or RETRAIN]
```

### 4.2 Decision-Flow Schematic for the 4D Quality Vector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Inspection Results] --> T1{FID less than 50?}
    T1 -- Yes --> T2{SSIM greater than 0.85?}
    T1 -- No --> X1[REJECT: Distribution Mismatch]
    T2 -- Yes --> T3{Clinical Likert greater than 3?}
    T2 -- No --> X2[REJECT: Pixel Level Failure]
    T3 -- Yes --> T4{Downstream Dice improved?}
    T3 -- No --> X3[REJECT: Anatomical Inconsistency]
    T4 -- Yes --> Y1[APPROVE: Deploy to Clinical Pipeline]
    T4 -- No --> X4[REJECT: No Utility Gain]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This decision tree is a **simplified KTU-style heuristic** for the final report. Real clinical deployment requires regulatory approval (FDA / CDSCO), but this captures the *engineering logic* of Module 4.

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Mode Collapse Detection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Generator G] --> M1[Output Batch 1: 64 Images]
    M1 --> M2[Project to Inception Feature Space]
    M2 --> M3[Compute Pairwise Cosine Distances]
    M3 --> M4{Distance Variance greater than threshold epsilon?}
    M4 -- No --> M5[WARNING: Mode Collapse Detected]
    M4 -- Yes --> M6[OK: Diverse Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The Inception Score (IS) is mathematically incapable of detecting mode collapse when the collapsed mode happens to be a *confidently classified* class (e.g., a generator that only produces "healthy" lungs). The cosine-distance variance check shown above is the **modern workaround** used in 2024 medical imaging papers.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse FID with IS directionality.** FID $\downarrow$ is better, IS $\uparrow$ is better. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Do NOT report PSNR without specifying $MAX_I$.** A bare number like "PSNR = 30" is incomplete; always state $MAX_I = 255$ or $1.0$.
> 3. **Do NOT use SSIM for unpaired data.** SSIM requires pixel-wise correspondence. Reporting SSIM for unpaired GAN outputs is an automatic zero for that sub-part.
> 4. **Always mention the Inception-V3 backbone** when writing about FID/IS. Examiners test whether you know *which* feature extractor is used.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) and explain why it is preferred over the Inception Score (IS) for evaluating generated medical images.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
FID is the Fréchet distance (Wasserstein-2 distance) between two multi-variate Gaussians fitted to the Inception-V3 features of real and generated image sets. It is computed as:
$$FID = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert^2 + \text{Tr}\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$$
FID is preferred over IS because (i) it compares *joint* statistics of real and generated distributions rather than only the marginal of generated data, making it sensitive to mode collapse, and (ii) it is robust to the Inception-V3 classifier's bias toward natural images, which is critical for medical modalities like CT and MRI that differ from ImageNet. **(3 Marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**List and briefly define any three pixel-level inspection metrics used for evaluating generated medical images.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **MSE (Mean Squared Error):** Average squared difference between generated and ground-truth pixel intensities; lower is better. **(1 Mark)**
2. **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** Ratio of peak signal power to noise power in decibels; higher is better; values above 40 dB indicate excellent reconstruction. **(1 Mark)**
3. **SSIM (Structural Similarity Index):** Measures luminance, contrast, and structural similarity on local $11 \times 11$ windows; ranges in $[-1, 1]$; higher is better. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical formulation of the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) used for inspecting generated medical images. Clearly state all assumptions and the role of the Inception-V3 backbone. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Step 1 — Feature Extraction (2 Marks):** Pass $N$ real images and $N$ generated images through a pre-trained Inception-V3 network. Extract the 2048-dimensional activations from the final pooling layer. This yields feature sets $X_r$ and $X_g$.

**Step 2 — Gaussian Assumption (1 Mark):** Assume each feature set is drawn from a multi-variate Gaussian: $X_r \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_r, \Sigma_r)$ and $X_g \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_g, \Sigma_g)$.

**Step 3 — Parameter Estimation (1 Mark):** Estimate mean and covariance via MLE:
$$\mu_r = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_r^{(i)}, \quad \Sigma_r = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_r^{(i)} - \mu_r)(x_r^{(i)} - \mu_r)^T$$

**Step 4 — Fréchet Distance Formula (3 Marks):** The squared Fréchet distance (Wasserstein-2) is:
$$FID = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g \Vert_2^2 + \text{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$$

The first term penalizes the mean shift; the second penalizes covariance mismatch. Inception-V3 serves as a *perceptual feature extractor* — its ImageNet-pretrained weights capture high-level semantics transferable to medical imaging, although domain-specific backbones (e.g., RadImageNet) are now preferred for medical-specific FID variants.

**(b)** A research team synthesizes 1,000 brain MR images using a diffusion model. They report FID = 15.2 and IS = 9.8. Critically analyze whether these scores are sufficient to declare the model clinically valid. What additional inspection steps are mandatory? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Step 1 — Interpreting the Reported Scores (2 Marks):** An FID of 15.2 is moderately good (lower is better; values below 10 are excellent for natural images, but medical-image FIDs are typically higher due to domain shift). An IS of 9.8 is high and indicates that the generated images are confidently classifiable into distinct categories. However, *both metrics are blind to clinical validity* — they only check distributional and classifier-level properties.

**Step 2 — The Limitation of FID/IS Alone (2 Marks):** FID/IS cannot verify that a generated tumor has the correct size, shape, location, or texture. A generator could memorize training images (low FID, high IS) without producing *new* clinically useful samples. They are also insensitive to mode collapse in specific pathology classes.

**Step 3 — Mandatory Additional Inspection (3 Marks):**
1. **Clinical/Anatomical Validity (Pillar 3):** Expert radiologist Likert scoring (1–5) on realism, lesion morphology, and diagnostic value. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Downstream Task Inspection (Pillar 4):** Train a tumor segmentation model (e.g., nnU-Net) on a mix of real and synthetic MRIs, then evaluate on a held-out real test set using Dice score. If Dice improves, utility is proven. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Mode Collapse / Diversity Check:** Compute pairwise cosine distance variance in Inception feature space; report class-wise FID to ensure *all* pathology classes (not just "healthy") are well-represented. **(1 Mark)**

**Conclusion:** The scores are *necessary but not sufficient*. Clinical deployment requires the full 4D Quality Vector $Q = \langle F, D, V, U \rangle$ before any regulatory submission.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Adapted]**

**(a)** With neat mathematical derivation, explain the Structural Similarity Index (SSIM). Why is SSIM preferred over MSE for medical image inspection? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition and Local Windows (2 Marks):** SSIM is computed over local $11 \times 11$ sliding windows. For two patches $x$ and $y$, compute local statistics: $\mu_x, \mu_y$ (means), $\sigma_x^2, \sigma_y^2$ (variances), and $\sigma_{xy}$ (cross-covariance).

**Step 2 — Stability Constants (1 Mark):** Define $c_1 = (0.01 L)^2$ and $c_2 = (0.03 L)^2$, where $L$ is the dynamic range (e.g., 255 for 8-bit).

**Step 3 — Final Formula (3 Marks):**
$$SSIM(x,y) = \dfrac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + c_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + c_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + c_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + c_2)}$$

This can be decomposed into luminance, contrast, and structure terms. The mean SSIM over all windows is the final reported value, ranging in $[-1, 1]$.

**Step 4 — Why SSIM > MSE for Medical Images (1 Mark):** MSE penalizes *all* pixel errors equally and is notoriously insensitive to perceptual quality — a slightly blurred image can have a low MSE while being diagnostically useless. SSIM, by modeling luminance, contrast, and structure, aligns with the human visual system and with radiological perception of anatomical boundaries. Hence SSIM is preferred.

**(b)** Consider a CT denoising model producing an output with $MSE = 89.5$ on 8-bit images ($MAX_I = 255$). Compute the PSNR. The hospital's clinical standard requires PSNR $\geq 42$ dB. Does this model pass the clinical quality gate? Suggest two engineering improvements. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the PSNR Formula (3 Marks):**
$$PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{MAX_I^2}{MSE}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{255^2}{89.5}\right)$$

$$PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{65025}{89.5}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(726.54)$$

$$PSNR = 10 \times 2.8613 \approx 28.61 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Compare to Threshold (1 Mark):** $28.61$ dB $< 42$ dB. **The model FAILS the clinical quality gate.** A difference of more than 13 dB indicates substantial reconstruction error.

**Step 3 — Engineering Improvements (3 Marks):**
1. **Use a Perceptual Loss Function:** Replace pixel-wise MSE with a perceptual loss (e.g., VGG-feature loss) or a structural loss (1 - SSIM) to better preserve anatomical edges. **(1.5 Marks)**
2. **Adversarial Fine-Tuning:** Append a PatchGAN discriminator and train the denoiser with an adversarial objective to encourage sharper, more realistic textures consistent with real CT noise statistics. **(1.5 Marks)**

**Additional suggestion (optional):** Train on paired low-dose / full-dose CT data at a higher resolution; incorporate physics-informed noise models specific to the CT scanner manufacturer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Inspecting generated medical images** is a **four-pillar framework**: Quantitative Statistical, Pixel-Level Reconstruction, Clinical/Anatomical Validity, and Downstream Task inspection.
- The **4D Quality Vector** is $Q = \langle F, D, V, U \rangle$ — fidelity, diversity, validity, utility.
- **FID** is the Fréchet / Wasserstein-2 distance between Inception-V3 feature Gaussians; **lower is better**; standard for unpaired synthesis.
- **IS** measures sharpness and diversity via the KL divergence between conditional and marginal class probabilities; **higher is better**; can miss mode collapse.
- **PSNR** $= 10 \log_{10}(MAX_I^2 / MSE)$ in **dB**; **higher is better**; values $\geq 40$ dB are excellent.
- **SSIM** combines luminance, contrast, and structure on $11 \times 11$ windows; range $[-1, 1]$; **higher is better**; requires paired images.
- **Dice Score** on downstream segmentation tasks is the *gold standard* for proving clinical utility in published medical AI research.
- The **stability constants** $c_1 = (0.01 L)^2$ and $c_2 = (0.03 L)^2$ in SSIM prevent division by zero — always state them.
- **Mode collapse** can be detected via pairwise cosine distance variance in feature space, supplementing IS.
- **KTU 2024 mandate:** Always specify $MAX_I$ when reporting PSNR; always specify the feature extractor when reporting FID/IS; never use SSIM for unpaired data.
- **Order of metrics to report in a KTU answer:** (1) FID + IS for distributional similarity, (2) PSNR + SSIM for pixel fidelity (if paired), (3) Expert Likert for clinical validity, (4) Downstream Dice for utility.
- The **Inception-V3 backbone** is pre-trained on ImageNet; for purely medical synthesis, **RadImageNet-pretrained** backbones give more meaningful FID values.
- **Clinical decision rule (simplified):** FID $< 50$ AND SSIM $> 0.85$ AND Likert $> 3$ AND Downstream Dice improves $\Rightarrow$ APPROVE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image-to-Image Translation: Paired vs Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT865 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Image-to-Image Translation: Paired vs Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image-to-Image Translation (I2I)** is a class of **deep generative learning** problems in which a model learns a *mapping function* $G: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that transforms an input image from a **source domain** $\mathcal{X}$ into a structurally/semantically corresponding output image in a **target domain** $\mathcal{Y}$, while preserving anatomical or scene-level geometry.

In the **medical imaging** context, I2I is critical for tasks such as:
- **MR → CT synthesis** (pseudo-CT for radiation therapy dose calculation, PET attenuation correction).
- **CT → MR synthesis** (MR-only radiotherapy workflows).
- **Low-dose CT → Normal-dose CT** (denoising and dose reduction).
- **T1-weighted → T2-weighted / FLAIR MRI** (cross-modal contrast synthesis).
- **Modality translation for data harmonization** in multi-center clinical studies.

I2I is broadly classified based on the **availability of paired training samples**:

### 🔹 Paired Image-to-Image Translation
> [!NOTE]
> A **paired** dataset consists of training tuples $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $x_i \in \mathcal{X}$ and $y_i \in \mathcal{Y}$ are **spatially co-registered, voxel-wise corresponding** images. The model is *supervised* — for every input there exists a known ground-truth target.

**Canonical architecture:** *Pix2Pix* (Isola et al., 2017) — a **conditional GAN (cGAN)** with a **U-Net generator** and a **PatchGAN discriminator**.

### 🔹 Unpaired Image-to-Image Translation
> [!NOTE]
> An **unpaired** dataset contains two **independent** image collections $\mathcal{X} = \{x_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and $\mathcal{Y} = \{y_j\}_{j=1}^{M}$ with **no direct correspondence** between samples ($M$ and $N$ need not even be equal). The model must learn the translation *unsupervisedly* using statistical and structural priors.

**Canonical architecture:** *CycleGAN* (Zhu et al., 2017) — two generators $(G, F)$ with a **cycle-consistency loss** that enforces $F(G(x)) \approx x$ and $G(F(y)) \approx y$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine you are a medical illustrator translating between two medical atlases.**

### 🎨 Paired I2I (Pix2Pix Analogy)
Think of a **bilingual medical dictionary** where the *English anatomy* of every sentence is on the left page and the *Latin anatomical term* is on the right page — **word-by-word aligned**. The illustrator simply learns to copy the right-page content given the left-page content. This is supervised: every input has an exact "answer key."

> *Real medical example:* A patient undergoes **both MRI and CT scans on the same day** in the same position. The CT and MR voxels are **rigidly registered** so that voxel $(i,j,k)$ in the MR maps to voxel $(i,j,k)$ in the CT. This is a paired dataset.

### 🎭 Unpaired I2I (CycleGAN Analogy)
Now imagine you are given **a stack of English anatomy book pages** and **a completely separate stack of Latin anatomy book pages** — but they are **shuffled and from different books**. You have no idea which English page corresponds to which Latin page. However, you can still learn the translation by demanding **consistency**: translating English → Latin → English should bring you back to the original English page. This round-trip constraint is the **cycle-consistency loss**.

> *Real medical example:* You have **MRI scans from Hospital A** (Siemens 3T scanner) and **CT scans from Hospital B** (GE scanner), acquired from **different patient populations** with **no registration possible**. This is unpaired data — yet you still want to train an MR→CT translator for downstream dose planning.

### 📊 Quick Visual Comparison

| Property | Paired (Pix2Pix) | Unpaired (CycleGAN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Training data structure** | $(x, y)$ tuples | $\{x_i\}, \{y_j\}$ sets |
| **Supervision** | Supervised (pixel-wise ground truth) | Unsupervised (cycle constraint) |
| **Loss** | L1/L2 + Adversarial | Adversarial + Cycle + Identity |
| **Network pairs** | 1 Generator, 1 Discriminator | 2 Generators, 2 Discriminators |
| **Medical challenge** | Hard to obtain co-registered multi-modal scans | Easier — uses any available scans |
| **Sample efficiency** | High (each pair teaches a lot) | Lower (needs more samples) |
| **Output fidelity** | Sharper, more accurate | Risk of hallucination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In clinical AI deployment, **unpaired methods are often the only feasible option** because obtaining paired multi-modal medical scans is *expensive, time-consuming, and exposes patients to additional radiation*. Understanding both paradigms is essential.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Both Paradigms

The following quantitative measures are universally used to evaluate I2I translation quality in both paired and unpaired settings (the bolded entries are the **K TU board-expected primary metrics**):

- **MAE (Mean Absolute Error)** — $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ — measures pixel/voxel intensity deviation.
- **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** — **higher is better**; measured in **dB**.
- **SSIM (Structural Similarity Index)** — $\text{SSIM} \in [-1, 1]$ — perceptual/structural fidelity.
- **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance)** — lower is better; measures distributional similarity of real vs. generated.
- **Dice / DSC (Dice-Sørensen Coefficient)** — **medical-specific**; measures overlap of segmented anatomical structures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Theory — Why and How

### 🅰️ Paired Translation: The Pix2Pix Paradigm

**Why paired data helps:** A direct pixel-wise supervision signal $L_{\text{L1}}(G) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}\left[\Vert y - G(x)\Vert_1\right]$ provides a **strong, unambiguous gradient** at every voxel. The model does not have to "guess" what the target looks like.

**How it works (operational logic):**
1. **Conditional Generator $G$**: Implemented as a **U-Net** (encoder–decoder with skip connections). Skip connections preserve high-frequency anatomical details (edges of organs, vessel boundaries) that would otherwise be lost during downsampling.
2. **PatchGAN Discriminator $D$**: Instead of classifying the *whole image* as real/fake, $D$ classifies each $N \times N$ patch. This forces the generator to produce **high-frequency correctness** locally, while global structure is handled by the L1 term.
3. **Combined Objective**: Adversarial loss produces *realistic* textures; L1 loss produces *correct* global structure.

### 🅱️ Unpaired Translation: The CycleGAN Paradigm

**Why unpaired data is harder:** Without pixel-wise ground truth, an adversarial loss alone is **under-constrained** — the generator can map *any* $x$ to *any* plausible $y$ (mode collapse, hallucination). The cycle-consistency loss adds a **bijection prior**.

**How it works (operational logic):**
1. **Two Generators**: $G: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ and $F: \mathcal{Y} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$.
2. **Two Discriminators**: $D_{\mathcal{Y}}$ (distinguishes real $y$ vs. fake $G(x)$) and $D_{\mathcal{X}}$ (distinguishes real $x$ vs. fake $F(y)$).
3. **Forward Cycle**: $x \xrightarrow{G} \hat{y} \xrightarrow{F} \hat{x} \approx x$ — penalizes $\Vert F(G(x)) - x \Vert_1$.
4. **Backward Cycle**: $y \xrightarrow{F} \hat{x} \xrightarrow{G} \hat{y} \approx y$ — penalizes $\Vert G(F(y)) - y \Vert_1$.
5. **Identity Loss** (optional regularizer): $G(y) \approx y$ when $y$ is fed to $G$ — preserves color/tone distribution of the target domain.

> [!TIP]
> **Why cycle-consistency works:** It enforces a **bijection** (one-to-one mapping) at the population level. Without it, the model could collapse $G$ into a constant output (e.g., always generating a "normal" brain regardless of input pathology), which trivially fools the discriminator but is clinically useless.

---

## 2.2 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Units / Range | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | GAN Adversarial Loss | $L_{\text{GAN}}(G,D) = \mathbb{E}_{y}\!\left[\log D(y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\log(1-D(G(x)))\right]$ | dimensionless, scalar | Both |
| 2 | Conditional GAN Loss | $L_{\text{cGAN}}(G,D) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}\!\left[\log D(x,y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x,z}\!\left[\log(1-D(x,G(x,z)))\right]$ | dimensionless | Pix2Pix |
| 3 | L1 Reconstruction Loss | $L_{\text{L1}}(G) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}\!\left[\Vert y - G(x,z)\Vert_1\right]$ | intensity units | Pix2Pix |
| 4 | Pix2Pix Total Objective | $G^{*} = \arg\min_{G}\max_{D}\; L_{\text{cGAN}}(G,D) + \lambda\, L_{\text{L1}}(G)$ | scalar, $\lambda \approx 100$ | Pix2Pix |
| 5 | Cycle-Consistency Loss | $L_{\text{cyc}}(G,F) = \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\Vert F(G(x))-x\Vert_1\right] + \mathbb{E}_{y}\!\left[\Vert G(F(y))-y\Vert_1\right]$ | intensity units | CycleGAN |
| 6 | Identity Loss | $L_{\text{id}}(G,F) = \mathbb{E}_{y}\!\left[\Vert G(y)-y\Vert_1\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\Vert F(x)-x\Vert_1\right]$ | intensity units | CycleGAN |
| 7 | CycleGAN Full Loss | $L(G,F,D_{\mathcal{X}},D_{\mathcal{Y}}) = L_{\text{GAN}}(G,D_{\mathcal{Y}}) + L_{\text{GAN}}(F,D_{\mathcal{X}}) + \lambda_{\text{cyc}} L_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}} L_{\text{id}}$ | scalar, $\lambda_{\text{cyc}} \approx 10$ | CycleGAN |
| 8 | MAE (Pixel Error) | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i\vert$ | intensity (HU / a.u.) | Both |
| 9 | PSNR | $\text{PSNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{MAX}^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$, $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | **dB** (higher is better) | Both |
| 10 | SSIM | $\text{SSIM}(x,y) = \dfrac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + c_1)(2\sigma_{xy}+c_2)}{(\mu_x^2+\mu_y^2+c_1)(\sigma_x^2+\sigma_y^2+c_2)}$ | $\vert\text{SSIM}\vert \leq 1$ | Both |
| 11 | FID | $\text{FID} = \Vert \mu_r - \mu_g\Vert^2 + \text{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r\Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | scalar (lower is better) | Both |
| 12 | Dice Coefficient | $\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A\vert + \vert B \vert} = \dfrac{2\text{TP}}{2\text{TP} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}}$ | $[0,1]$ | Medical eval |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "derive" the loss, the examiner expects you to start from the **binary cross-entropy** form of the discriminator and explicitly show the $\log$ terms. Do **not** skip the $\mathbb{E}[\,\cdot\,]$ (expectation) notation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radiation Oncology (MR-only workflows)**: CycleGAN-style MR→CT synthesis enables dose calculation **without exposing the patient to CT radiation**, reducing cumulative dose in pediatric and longitudinal retreatment cases.
- **PET Attenuation Correction**: Pseudo-CT from MR allows **MR-based PET attenuation maps** in hybrid PET-MR scanners.
- **Low-Dose CT Denoising**: Paired (low-dose, normal-dose) supervised models enable 60–80% dose reduction while preserving diagnostic quality.
- **Multi-Center Data Harmonization**: Unpaired translation normalizes contrast/appearance across vendors (Siemens vs. GE vs. Philips), enabling robust deep-learning segmentation models.
- **Data Augmentation**: Synthetic CT or MR can augment rare-pathology training sets, addressing **class imbalance** in tumor segmentation tasks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Vanilla GAN Objective to Pix2Pix Conditional GAN

### Step 1 — Original GAN Formulation (Goodfellow et al., 2014)

The minimax game between generator $G$ and discriminator $D$ over data $y \sim p_{\text{data}}$:

$$
\min_{G}\max_{D}\; V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{y \sim p_{\text{data}}(y)}\!\left[\log D(y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}(z)}\!\left[\log\!\big(1 - D(G(z))\big)\right]
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $D(y)$ is the probability that a real sample $y$ is judged **real**.
- $D(G(z))$ is the probability that a generated sample $G(z)$ is judged **real**.
- The discriminator wants to push $D(y) \to 1$ and $D(G(z)) \to 0$ (i.e., $\log(1 - D(G(z))) \to 0$).
- The generator wants the **opposite**: $D(G(z)) \to 1$.

### Step 2 — Conditioning on the Input Image $x$

For **image-to-image** translation, $G$ must be a function of an input image $x$ (not just noise $z$). We condition both the generator and the discriminator on $x$:

$$
G: \{x, z\} \rightarrow y, \qquad D: \{x, y\} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}
$$

The conditional GAN objective becomes:

$$
L_{\text{cGAN}}(G,D) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}\!\left[\log D(x,y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x,z}\!\left[\log\!\big(1 - D(x, G(x,z))\big)\right]
$$

**Why add $x$ to the discriminator?** So $D$ learns to check *joint* realness of the **(input, output) pair**, not just output realism. This prevents the generator from producing an arbitrarily realistic but *mismatched* target.

### Step 3 — Adding L1 Regularization for Low-Frequency Correctness

Pure cGAN losses produce sharp but **unfaithful** outputs (the generator prioritizes fooling $D$ over matching $y$). We add an L1 penalty between generated and ground-truth:

$$
L_{\text{L1}}(G) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y,z}\!\left[\Vert y - G(x,z)\Vert_1\right]
$$

**Why L1 and not L2?** L1 encourages **median-seeking** behavior (less blur); L2 encourages **mean-seeking** (smoother but blurrier outputs). For medical images where **edges and boundaries matter** (e.g., tumor margins), L1 is preferred.

### Step 4 — The Final Pix2Pix Objective

$$
G^{*} = \arg\min_{G}\max_{D}\; L_{\text{cGAN}}(G,D) + \lambda\, L_{\text{L1}}(G)
$$

**Hyperparameter $\lambda$:** Controls the trade-off. Pix2Pix uses $\lambda = 100$, meaning the L1 term is **weighted 100× more** than the adversarial term — ensuring the model does not sacrifice pixel accuracy for realism.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cycle-Consistency Loss for Unpaired Translation

### Step 5 — The Under-Constrained Problem

In the unpaired setting we have only $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^{N} \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)$ and $\{y_j\}_{j=1}^{M} \sim p_{\text{data}}(y)$. If we train only with adversarial loss:

$$
L_{\text{GAN}}(G, D_{\mathcal{Y}}) = \mathbb{E}_{y}\!\left[\log D_{\mathcal{Y}}(y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\log\!\big(1 - D_{\mathcal{Y}}(G(x))\big)\right]
$$

then $G$ is **not uniquely determined**. Many mappings from $\mathcal{X}$ to $\mathcal{Y}$ can fool $D_{\mathcal{Y}}$. To illustrate, consider the pathological mapping:

$$
G^{*}(x) = y_{0} \quad \forall x
$$

(i.e., always output the *same* target image). This minimizes $L_{\text{GAN}}$ trivially but is clinically useless.

### Step 6 — Imposing Bijectivity via Cycle-Consistency

**Key insight:** If $G$ translates $x \to \hat{y}$ and $F$ translates $\hat{y} \to \hat{x}$, then demanding $\hat{x} \approx x$ forces $G$ to **preserve enough information** to allow reconstruction.

**Forward cycle** ($x \to G \to F \to \hat{x}$):

$$
L_{\text{forward}} = \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\Vert F(G(x)) - x \Vert_1\right]
$$

**Backward cycle** ($y \to F \to G \to \hat{y}$):

$$
L_{\text{backward}} = \mathbb{E}_{y}\!\left[\Vert G(F(y)) - y \Vert_1\right]
$$

**Combined cycle loss:**

$$
L_{\text{cyc}}(G,F) = \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\Vert F(G(x)) - x \Vert_1\right] + \mathbb{E}_{y}\!\left[\Vert G(F(y)) - y \Vert_1\right]
$$

### Step 7 — Adding Identity Loss (Color Preservation Regularizer)

Without identity loss, $G$ may arbitrarily shift the intensity histogram of $\mathcal{Y}$. Forcing $G(y) \approx y$ (when the target-domain image is fed) **preserves color/tone statistics**:

$$
L_{\text{id}}(G,F) = \mathbb{E}_{y}\!\left[\Vert G(y) - y \Vert_1\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\Vert F(x) - x \Vert_1\right]
$$

### Step 8 — The Final CycleGAN Objective

$$
L(G,F,D_{\mathcal{X}},D_{\mathcal{Y}}) = L_{\text{GAN}}(G,D_{\mathcal{Y}}) + L_{\text{GAN}}(F,D_{\mathcal{X}}) + \lambda_{\text{cyc}}\, L_{\text{cyc}}(G,F) + \lambda_{\text{id}}\, L_{\text{id}}(G,F)
$$

where typically $\lambda_{\text{cyc}} = 10$ and $\lambda_{\text{id}} = 0.5 \cdot \lambda_{\text{cyc}} = 5$.

**Optimal parameters:**

$$
G^{*}, F^{*} = \arg\min_{G,F}\max_{D_{\mathcal{X}},D_{\mathcal{Y}}}\; L(G,F,D_{\mathcal{X}},D_{\mathcal{Y}})
$$

---

## 3.3 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Pix2Pix (Paired) | CycleGAN (Unpaired) |
|---|---|---|
| **Generator $G$** | U-Net (skip connections) | ResNet-9 (residual blocks) |
| **Discriminator $D$** | PatchGAN ($70 \times 70$ receptive field) | PatchGAN ($70 \times 70$ receptive field) |
| **# of Networks** | 2 (G, D) | 4 (G, F, $D_{\mathcal{X}}$, $D_{\mathcal{Y}}$) |
| **Loss terms** | 2 (cGAN + L1) | 4 (2 GAN + Cycle + Identity) |
| **Training data requirement** | Strictly paired, co-registered | Loosely related, two independent sets |
| **Mode collapse risk** | Low (L1 anchors output) | Moderate–High (mitigated by cycle loss) |
| **Memory footprint** | ~$2\times$ (G + D) | ~$4\times$ (G + F + $D_{\mathcal{X}}$ + $D_{\mathcal{Y}}$) |
| **Hallucination risk** | Low (ground-truth supervision) | Moderate (no direct supervision) |
| **Clinical deployment readiness** | Higher | Requires rigorous validation |
| **Best medical use case** | Low-dose CT denoising, super-resolution | Cross-vendor harmonization, MR→CT synthesis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Paired Translation: Pix2Pix Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input Image X"] --> GenG["Generator G: U-Net Encoder Decoder"]
    Z["Noise Vector z (dropout)"] --> GenG
    GenG --> FakeY["Generated Image G_X_Z"]
    
    A --> Stack1["Concat X with candidate Y along channels"]
    FakeY --> Stack1
    Stack1 --> DiscD["Discriminator D: PatchGAN 70x70"]
    
    GT["Ground Truth Y"] --> Stack2["Concat X with Y along channels"]
    A --> Stack2
    Stack2 --> DiscD
    
    DiscD --> OutD["Real or Fake patch map"]
    
    FakeY --> L1["L1 Loss vs Ground Truth Y"]
    GT --> L1
    L1 --> TotalL["Total Loss: cGAN + lambda L1"]
    
    FakeY --> AdvL["Adversarial Loss"]
    AdvL --> TotalL
    
    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef disc fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    
    class A,Z,GT input
    class GenG,FakeY gen
    class Stack1,Stack2,DiscD,OutD disc
    class L1,AdvL,TotalL loss
```

### 📖 Diagram Walkthrough (Pix2Pix)
- **Input image $X$** enters the **U-Net generator** along with **noise $z$** (applied as dropout at inference for stochasticity).
- The **generated image $G(x,z)$** is concatenated with $X$ and fed to the **PatchGAN discriminator $D$**, alongside the **ground-truth pair** $(X, Y)$.
- $D$ outputs a **patch-wise real/fake map**, each entry judging a $70 \times 70$ receptive field.
- Two losses combine: **L1 loss** (pixel accuracy) and **adversarial loss** (perceptual realism), weighted by $\lambda = 100$.

---

## 4.2 Unpaired Translation: CycleGAN Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    X["Real Image from Domain X"] --> G1["Generator G: X to Y"]
    G1 --> FakeY["Fake Y: G_X"]
    FakeY --> DY["Discriminator DY: distinguishes real Y vs fake Y"]
    RealY["Real Image from Domain Y"] --> DY
    DY --> OutDY["Real or Fake label"]
    
    FakeY --> F1["Generator F: Y to X"]
    F1 --> ReconX["Reconstructed X: F_G_X"]
    X --> F1Loss["Cycle loss: F_G_X vs X"]
    ReconX --> F1Loss
    
    RealY --> G2["Generator G: Y to Y (identity)"]
    G2 --> IdY["Identity output G_Y"]
    RealY --> IdLossY["Identity loss: G_Y vs Y"]
    IdY --> IdLossY
    
    RealY --> F2["Generator F: Y to X"]
    F2 --> FakeX["Fake X: F_Y"]
    FakeX --> DX["Discriminator DX: distinguishes real X vs fake X"]
    X --> DX
    DX --> OutDX["Real or Fake label"]
    
    FakeX --> G3["Generator G: X to Y"]
    G3 --> ReconY["Reconstructed Y: G_F_Y"]
    RealY --> G3Loss["Cycle loss: G_F_Y vs Y"]
    ReconY --> G3Loss
    
    X --> F3["Generator F: X to X (identity)"]
    F3 --> IdX["Identity output F_X"]
    X --> IdLossX["Identity loss: F_X vs X"]
    IdX --> IdLossX
    
    classDef realX fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef realY fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d46b08,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef disc fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    
    class X,ReconX,IdX,FakeX realX
    class RealY,FakeY,IdY,ReconY realY
    class G1,G2,G3,F1,F2,F3 gen
    class DY,DX,OutDY,OutDX disc
    class F1Loss,G3Loss,IdLossX,IdLossY loss
```

### 📖 Diagram Walkthrough (CycleGAN)
- **Forward path:** $X \xrightarrow{G} \hat{Y} \xrightarrow{F} \hat{X}$, and the **forward cycle loss** is $\Vert \hat{X} - X \Vert_1$.
- **Backward path:** $Y \xrightarrow{F} \hat{X} \xrightarrow{G} \hat{Y}$, and the **backward cycle loss** is $\Vert \hat{Y} - Y \Vert_1$.
- **Two adversarial losses** on $D_{\mathcal{X}}$ and $D_{\mathcal{Y}}$ ensure the generated images lie in the correct domain distributions.
- **Identity regularization** on $G$ and $F$ (e.g., $G(y) \approx y$) prevents unnecessary histogram shifts.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing Paired vs Unpaired

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["New I2I Medical Task"] --> Q1{"Are co-registered\nmulti-modal scans\navailable per patient?"}
    
    Q1 -- "YES" --> Q2{"Is pixel-perfect\nfidelity critical?\n(e.g., tumor margin)"}
    Q1 -- "NO" --> Unpaired["Use Unpaired\n(CycleGAN / CUT / NICE-GAN)"]
    
    Q2 -- "YES" --> Paired["Use Paired\n(Pix2Pix / SPADE / pGAN)"]
    Q2 -- "NO" --> Q3{"Need diverse\nappearance variation?"}
    
    Q3 -- "YES" --> Hybrid["Consider Diffusion-based\n(e.g., SynDiff, Med-DDPM)"]
    Q3 -- "NO" --> Paired
    
    Unpaired --> Val1["Validate via downstream\nsegmentation Dice score"]
    Paired --> Val2["Validate via MAE / PSNR / SSIM\non held-out pairs"]
    
    classDef start fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef method fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef val fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    
    class Start start
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 decision
    class Paired,Unpaired,Hybrid method
    class Val1,Val2 val
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🅰️ Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between paired and unpaired image-to-image translation with one medical-imaging example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Paired I2I | Unpaired I2I |
|---|---|---|
| **Data structure** | Training tuples $(x_i, y_i)$ — co-registered, voxel-wise correspondence | Independent image sets $\{x_i\}$ and $\{y_j\}$ — no correspondence |
| **Supervision** | Supervised (pixel-wise ground truth available) | Unsupervised (no direct supervision) |
| **Loss function** | L1/L2 + Adversarial loss | Adversarial + Cycle-consistency + Identity loss |
| **Medical example** | Low-dose CT → Normal-dose CT denoising (same patient, both scans) | MR → CT synthesis for radiation therapy (different patients, no registration) |
| **Architecture** | Single $G$ + single $D$ (e.g., Pix2Pix) | Two generators $(G, F)$ + two discriminators $(D_{\mathcal{X}}, D_{\mathcal{Y}})$ (e.g., CycleGAN) |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is cycle-consistency loss in unpaired image-to-image translation? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** Cycle-consistency loss enforces that translating an image from domain $\mathcal{X}$ to $\mathcal{Y}$ and back to $\mathcal{X}$ should recover the original image:
> $$L_{\text{cyc}}(G,F) = \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\Vert F(G(x)) - x \Vert_1\right] + \mathbb{E}_{y}\!\left[\Vert G(F(y)) - y \Vert_1\right]$$

> **Why necessary (2 reasons):**
> 1. **Prevents mode collapse:** Without it, $G$ can map every $x$ to a single target image $y_0$, trivially fooling the discriminator but destroying the input–output correspondence.
> 2. **Enforces bijection:** It ensures $G$ and $F$ are **inverses** of each other, forcing the mapping to preserve enough information for reconstruction — critical in medical applications to avoid hallucinating anatomy.

---

## 5.2 🅱️ Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **omit the expectation operator** $\mathbb{E}[\,\cdot\,]$ when writing loss functions. **-1 mark penalty.**
> - Confusing **adversarial loss** with **reconstruction loss** — they serve different purposes. Mention *both* roles explicitly.
> - Failing to state the **value of $\lambda$** in Pix2Pix ($\lambda = 100$) or CycleGAN ($\lambda_{\text{cyc}} = 10$) loses a mark.
> - Forgetting the **identity loss** term when deriving CycleGAN — it is **often a follow-up question**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* **Explain the architecture and training objective of the Pix2Pix framework for paired image-to-image translation. Include the U-Net generator and PatchGAN discriminator design choices in your answer.**

**(b)** *(7 marks)* **Derive the full conditional GAN loss with L1 regularization used in Pix2Pix. Justify why L1 is preferred over L2 for medical image synthesis.**

#### 📝 Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **Generator $G$ — U-Net (3 marks):**
   - **Encoder–decoder** with skip connections between mirrored layers.
   - **Skip connections** concatenate encoder feature maps with decoder feature maps, preserving **high-frequency spatial details** (organ boundaries, vessel edges) lost during downsampling.
   - For a 256×256 input, the U-Net has 8 encoder + 8 decoder levels with skip connections at every scale.
   - **Why for medical images?** Anatomical boundaries (e.g., tumor margins) are critical; U-Net preserves them.

2. **Discriminator $D$ — PatchGAN (2 marks):**
   - Classifies each $N \times N$ patch (typically $70 \times 70$) of the input as real or fake.
   - Output is a **feature map** of patch-level predictions, not a single scalar.
   - **Why?** Models high-frequency structure locally; combined with L1 (low-frequency), yields sharp and globally consistent outputs.

3. **Conditioning mechanism (2 marks):**
   - Both $G$ and $D$ receive the input $x$ (or $x$ concatenated with candidate $y$) — $D$ judges the **(input, output) pair** as real/fake, not just the output.
   - Noise $z$ is injected via **dropout** at inference time, not as a separate input vector.

**Part (b) — Loss Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the binary cross-entropy form of adversarial loss:

$$
L_{\text{GAN}}(D) = -\!\left[\mathbb{E}_{x,y}\!\log D(x,y) + \mathbb{E}_{x,z}\!\log\!\big(1 - D(x,G(x,z))\big)\right]
$$

The cGAN objective (with $G$ minimizing) becomes:

$$
L_{\text{cGAN}}(G,D) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}\!\left[\log D(x,y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x,z}\!\left[\log\!\big(1 - D(x, G(x,z))\big)\right]
$$

Adding L1 reconstruction:

$$
L_{\text{L1}}(G) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y,z}\!\left[\Vert y - G(x,z)\Vert_1\right]
$$

Full objective:

$$
G^{*} = \arg\min_{G}\max_{D}\; L_{\text{cGAN}}(G,D) + \lambda\, L_{\text{L1}}(G), \quad \lambda = 100
$$

**Why L1 > L2 for medical images (3 marks):**
- L2 penalizes **outliers quadratically** → the model learns to predict the **conditional mean** → produces **blurry** outputs.
- L1 penalizes **outliers linearly** → the model learns the **conditional median** → produces **sharper** outputs.
- In medical imaging, **sharp edges** (tumor boundaries, vessel walls) are diagnostically essential — blur can obscure critical pathology.
- L1 also has a **sparser gradient** (constant magnitude), which is more robust to noisy pixel labels in segmentation-derived ground truths.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* **Explain the CycleGAN framework for unpaired image-to-image translation. Describe the role of forward and backward cycle-consistency losses with a suitable medical-imaging application.**

**(b)** *(7 marks)* **Derive the full CycleGAN training objective. Explain how cycle-consistency loss prevents mode collapse, with a mathematical justification.**

#### 📝 Model Solution

**Part (a) — Framework (7 marks):**

1. **Two generators (2 marks):**
   - $G: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ (e.g., MR → CT)
   - $F: \mathcal{Y} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ (e.g., CT → MR)

2. **Two discriminators (2 marks):**
   - $D_{\mathcal{Y}}$ judges whether an image belongs to the real $\mathcal{Y}$ distribution.
   - $D_{\mathcal{X}}$ judges whether an image belongs to the real $\mathcal{X}$ distribution.

3. **Cycle-consistency losses (3 marks):**
   - **Forward cycle:** $x \xrightarrow{G} \hat{y} \xrightarrow{F} \hat{x} \approx x$
     $$L_{\text{cyc, forward}} = \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\Vert F(G(x)) - x \Vert_1\right]$$
   - **Backward cycle:** $y \xrightarrow{F} \hat{x} \xrightarrow{G} \hat{y} \approx y$
     $$L_{\text{cyc, backward}} = \mathbb{E}_{y}\!\left[\Vert G(F(y)) - y \Vert_1\right]$$
   - **Medical application:** **MR → CT synthesis for MR-only radiotherapy planning.** MR and CT scans from different patient cohorts (no co-registration feasible) are used to train $G$; the reverse mapping $F$ regularizes via cycle-consistency to prevent the generator from hallucinating anatomically impossible CT structures.

**Part (b) — Derivation & Mode Collapse Prevention (7 marks):**

The full CycleGAN loss:

$$
L(G,F,D_{\mathcal{X}},D_{\mathcal{Y}}) = L_{\text{GAN}}(G,D_{\mathcal{Y}}) + L_{\text{GAN}}(F,D_{\mathcal{X}}) + \lambda_{\text{cyc}}\, L_{\text{cyc}}(G,F) + \lambda_{\text{id}}\, L_{\text{id}}(G,F)
$$

with $\lambda_{\text{cyc}} = 10$ and $\lambda_{\text{id}} = 5$.

**Mathematical justification — mode collapse prevention (4 marks):**

Consider a degenerate mapping $G^{*}(x) = y_0$ for all $x$ (constant output, complete mode collapse). The adversarial loss for $G$ is **minimized** because $D_{\mathcal{Y}}(y_0) = 1$ trivially:

$$
L_{\text{GAN}}(G^{*}, D_{\mathcal{Y}}) = \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\log\!\big(1 - D_{\mathcal{Y}}(y_0)\big)\right] = 0
$$

However, the **forward cycle loss is catastrophic**:

$$
L_{\text{cyc, forward}}(G^{*}, F) = \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\Vert F(y_0) - x \Vert_1\right] = \mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\Vert F(y_0) - x \Vert_1\right] \gg 0
$$

For this loss to be small, $F$ would have to map $y_0$ back to **every possible $x$** simultaneously — which is impossible. Therefore $G^{*} = y_0$ is **not a minimizer** of the full objective.

In general, the cycle loss $\Vert F(G(x)) - x\Vert_1 = 0$ requires $F \circ G$ to be the identity on $\mathcal{X}$. Combined with the analogous constraint on $\mathcal{Y}$, this forces $G$ and $F$ to be **bijections** (invertible mappings) — a much stronger constraint than the adversarial loss alone, which only enforces that $G(x)$ lies *somewhere* in the support of $p_{\text{data}}(y)$.

Hence:

$$
(G^{*}, F^{*}) = \arg\min_{G,F}\max_{D_{\mathcal{X}},D_{\mathcal{Y}}}\; L(G,F,D_{\mathcal{X}},D_{\mathcal{Y}})
$$

yields a meaningful one-to-one mapping, **eliminating mode collapse** and preventing the model from producing constant or unrelated outputs.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These Points**

- [x] **I2I translation** = learning $G: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ between image domains.
- [x] **Paired I2I** uses $(x_i, y_i)$ tuples with voxel-wise correspondence → **Pix2Pix** is the canonical model.
- [x] **Unpaired I2I** uses two independent image sets $\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y}$ with no correspondence → **CycleGAN** is the canonical model.
- [x] **Pix2Pix loss:** $L_{\text{cGAN}} + \lambda L_{\text{L1}}$, with $\lambda = 100$.
- [x] **CycleGAN loss:** $L_{\text{GAN}}(G,D_{\mathcal{Y}}) + L_{\text{GAN}}(F,D_{\mathcal{X}}) + \lambda_{\text{cyc}} L_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}} L_{\text{id}}$, with $\lambda_{\text{cyc}} = 10$.
- [x] **Generator architectures:** U-Net (Pix2Pix) vs. ResNet-9 (CycleGAN).
- [x] **Discriminator:** PatchGAN (70×70 receptive field) in **both** models.
- [x] **L1 loss** is preferred over L2 in medical I2I because it produces **sharper edges** (critical for tumor/vessel boundaries).
- [x] **Cycle-consistency** prevents **mode collapse** by enforcing bijection.
- [x] **Identity loss** regularizes color/tone preservation in CycleGAN.
- [x] **Evaluation metrics:** **MAE**, **PSNR** (dB), **SSIM** ($\in [-1,1]$), **FID** (lower better), **Dice** (medical-specific).
- [x] **Medical applications:** MR→CT synthesis, low-dose CT denoising, cross-vendor harmonization, PET attenuation correction.
- [x] **Clinical challenge:** Paired multi-modal scans are **expensive and rare** → unpaired methods (CycleGAN) are often the only feasible option in practice.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Forgetting the $\mathbb{E}[\,\cdot\,]$ operator in loss equations (KTU board penalizes this).
- [x] **Always mention** that **noise $z$ is injected via dropout** in Pix2Pix, not as a separate input channel.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pix2Pix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT865 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pix2Pix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Pix2Pix** is a **Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (cGAN)** framework introduced by Isola, Zhu, Zhou, and Efros (2017) for **paired image-to-image translation**. It learns a mapping from an input image $x$ to an output image $y$, conditioned on the input, using a structured adversarial loss and an $L_1$ reconstruction loss. In medical imaging, Pix2Pix is used to translate between imaging modalities (e.g., MRI $\to$ CT, low-dose CT $\to$ normal-dose CT) and to convert segmentation masks into realistic anatomical images.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Pix2Pix is the **foundational paired translation model** for medical image synthesis. It is a *supervised* model — it requires perfectly registered input–output image pairs during training, distinguishing it from unpaired methods like CycleGAN.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Forensic Sketch Artist"

Imagine a **forensic sketch artist** working with a police department:

- The police (the **Discriminator**) have memorized thousands of real photographs of suspects and real crime scenes.
- The sketch artist (the **Generator**) has never painted before but is shown a coarse verbal description (the **input image**, e.g., a segmentation map of a brain).
- The artist draws a sketch (the **fake/generated image**) and shows it to the police. The police say: *"This looks fake, real photos have realistic textures and lighting."*
- The artist adjusts and re-draws, slowly learning to produce sketches that fool even seasoned officers.

The crucial twist: in Pix2Pix, the artist is *also* given a parallel reference (the **ground-truth target image**), and is told to stay as close as possible to it. This is the **$L_1$ loss** — a teacher holding up a reference painting and saying *"Don't just fool the police, also stay close to the real thing."* Over time, the sketch artist's output becomes indistinguishable from real crime-scene photos *and* faithful to the description.

### Core Components at a Glance

| Component | Role | Architecture Used in Pix2Pix |
|---|---|---|
| **Generator (G)** | Translates input image $\to$ output image | **U-Net** (encoder–decoder with skip connections) |
| **Discriminator (D)** | Distinguishes real vs. fake pairs | **PatchGAN** (classifies each $N \times N$ patch) |
| **Conditioning Input** | The input image $x$ fed to both G and D | Channel-concatenated with target |
| **Loss Function** | Combined adversarial + $L_1$ | $\mathcal{L}_{cGAN} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{L1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "PatchGAN"?** Instead of outputting a single real/fake scalar for the whole image (which encourages blurry outputs), PatchGAN outputs a $30 \times 30$ grid where each cell judges a $70 \times 70$ receptive field patch. This forces the generator to match **high-frequency structure** — a critical feature in medical imaging where lesion edges and tissue boundaries must be crisp.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Conditional GAN Foundation

A standard GAN learns $G: z \to y$ from random noise. A **conditional GAN (cGAN)** learns $G: \{x, z\} \to y$, where $x$ is a conditioning image and $z$ is stochastic noise. The discriminator's job also becomes conditional: it must judge whether a $(x, y)$ pair is real or fake.

The cGAN value function is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D) = \mathbb{E}_{x, y \sim p_{data}(x,y)}\left[\log D(x, y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x),\, z \sim p_z(z)}\left[\log\left(1 - D(x, G(x, z))\right)\right]
$$

The goal of Pix2Pix:

$$
G^* = \arg\min_G \max_D \mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Isola et al., 2017):** In practice, $G$ is provided with **noise only in the form of dropout** applied at inference. This means the generator is **nearly deterministic** at test time — the same input MRI will always produce the same synthesized CT, which is essential for clinical reproducibility.

### 2.2 The Combined Pix2Pix Objective

The breakthrough of Pix2Pix is the addition of an **$L_1$ distance** between generated and ground-truth images:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{L1}(G) = \mathbb{E}_{x, y, z}\left[\Vert y - G(x, z) \Vert_1\right]
$$

The **total generator loss** becomes:

$$
G^* = \arg\min_G \max_D \left[\mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D) + \lambda \, \mathcal{L}_{L1}(G)\right]
$$

where $\lambda$ is a hyperparameter (typically $\lambda = 100$ in the original paper and many medical-imaging follow-ups).

**Why $L_1$ and not $L_2$?**

- $L_2$ (MSE) penalizes large errors quadratically, causing the generator to produce **blurry mean-like images** — disastrous for medical imaging where tumor boundaries matter.
- $L_1$ (Mean Absolute Error) preserves **sharpness** by penalizing all errors linearly, encouraging the generator to commit to specific pixel values.

### 2.3 The Discriminator — PatchGAN

The PatchGAN discriminator $D$ outputs a matrix $\mathbf{R} \in \mathbb{R}^{H' \times W'}$. Each entry $r_{ij} \in [0, 1]$ is the probability that the patch in receptive field $(i, j)$ is real.

For a $256 \times 256$ image, with **PatchGAN of size $70 \times 70$**, the output is a $30 \times 30$ grid. This implicitly models the image as a **Markov random field** — pixels separated by more than one patch diameter are assumed independent.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 ESE question on Pix2Pix.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G^* = \arg\min_G \max_D \mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D)$ | cGAN objective | Adversarial min-max training |
| 2 | $\mathcal{L}_{L1}(G) = \mathbb{E}\left[\Vert y - G(x, z) \Vert_1\right]$ | Pixel reconstruction | Forces output close to ground truth |
| 3 | $\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{cGAN} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{L1}$ | Combined loss | Total generator objective |
| 4 | $\lambda = 100$ | Balancing weight | Standard value in Pix2Pix |
| 5 | Patch size $70 \times 70$ | Receptive field | Original paper default |
| 6 | Generator architecture | **U-Net** | Skip connections preserve spatial detail |
| 7 | Discriminator architecture | **PatchGAN** | Classifies local patches, not full image |
| 8 | Input to $D$ | $(x, y)$ pair-channel concatenated | Conditioning mechanism |
| 9 | Noise injection | Dropout in $G$ only | Makes $G$ nearly deterministic |
| 10 | Output of $D$ | $30 \times 30$ probability map | One score per image patch |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Medical Application | Input $x$ | Output $y$ | Clinical Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MRI $\to$ CT synthesis** | T1-weighted MRI | Synthetic CT | Enables MRI-only radiotherapy planning, removes CT radiation dose |
| **Low-dose $\to$ Normal CT denoising** | Low-dose CT | Normal-dose CT | Reduces patient X-ray exposure |
| **PET $\to$ MRI translation** | PET scan | Pseudo-MRI | Cross-modality data augmentation |
| **Mask $\to$ Realistic image** | Organ/tumor segmentation map | Photorealistic anatomy | Data augmentation for rare diseases |
| **Cross-vendor harmonization** | Scanner-A image | Scanner-B-style image | Multi-center study standardization |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Conditional Adversarial Loss

We begin from the standard GAN loss and **condition** both terms on the input image $x$.

**Step 1 — Standard GAN (Unconditioned):**

$$
\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{y \sim p_{data}(y)}\!\left[\log D(y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z(z)}\!\left[\log(1 - D(G(z)))\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce conditioning on $x$:**

The discriminator now sees the joint input $(x, y)$:

$$
V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x, y \sim p_{data}(x, y)}\!\left[\log D(x, y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x),\, z \sim p_z(z)}\!\left[\log(1 - D(x, G(x, z)))\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite as minimization over $G$ and maximization over $D$:**

$$
G^* = \arg\min_G \max_D V(D, G)
$$

**Step 4 — Add the $L_1$ regularization term** to discourage blurriness and enforce fidelity to the ground-truth target:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{total}(G, D) = V(D, G) + \lambda \cdot \mathbb{E}_{x, y, z}\!\left[\Vert y - G(x, z) \Vert_1\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Final training rule:**

$$
G^* = \arg\min_G \max_D \left[\mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D) + \lambda \, \mathcal{L}_{L1}(G)\right]
$$

This is the **Pix2Pix loss function** as published in the 2017 CVPR paper.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Design Choice Was Made

| Design Choice | Problem It Solves | Mathematical / Empirical Justification | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| **U-Net Generator** | Encoder–decoder loses fine detail across deep layers | Skip connections: $h_{i}^{dec} = \text{Concat}(h_{i}^{enc}, h_{i}^{dec})$ preserve high-resolution features | Higher memory cost |
| **PatchGAN Discriminator** | Full-image $D$ averages out local realism | Smaller parameter count, focuses on **texture/statistics** rather than global structure | May miss large-scale anatomical errors |
| **$L_1$ vs. $L_2$ loss** | $L_2$ causes blurry outputs (pixel-wise mean) | $L_1$ gradient is constant magnitude $\Rightarrow$ no smoothing bias | Less smooth gradients during training |
| **Dropout as noise** | Random noise $z$ was ignored by $G$ in practice | Injecting noise via dropout forces stochasticity only at training time | Limits generative diversity at test time |
| **$\lambda = 100$** | Need to balance adversarial vs. pixel losses | Adversarial term is in $\log$ scale; pixel $L_1$ on $[0,1]$ images is small | Sensitive to image intensity normalization |
| **Channel concatenation for $D$** | Conditioning $D$ on $x$ | $D$ sees both input and (real or fake) target as a stacked tensor | Increases input channels from 3 to 6 |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Computing the Combined Loss

**Scenario:** A Pix2Pix model is translating an MRI slice to a synthetic CT slice. For one training pair:
- Ground-truth CT intensity at one pixel: $y = 0.72$
- Generator's output at the same pixel: $\hat{y} = G(x, z) = 0.65$
- Discriminator's probability that the **fake** pair is real: $D(x, G(x, z)) = 0.40$
- Discriminator's probability that the **real** pair is real: $D(x, y) = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Compute $L_1$ loss at this pixel:**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{L1} = \vert 0.72 - 0.65 \vert = 0.07
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cGAN loss contribution:**

For the real pair:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{real} = -\log(0.85) \approx 0.1625
$$

For the fake pair (the generator wants this to be high):
$$
\mathcal{L}_{fake} = -\log(0.40) \approx 0.9163
$$

**Step 3 — Total generator loss (with $\lambda = 100$):**

$$
\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{cGAN} + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{L1} = 0.9163 + 100 \times 0.07 = 0.9163 + 7.0 = 7.9163
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The dominant term is the $L_1$ loss. This is by design — in medical imaging, **pixel-level fidelity is non-negotiable**; the adversarial term acts as a "realism booster" rather than the primary driver.

### 3.4 Convergence Behaviour

Pix2Pix is trained with **alternating gradient updates** (Algorithm by Goodfellow et al., adapted):

1. Sample $m$ paired examples $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$ from the dataset.
2. Update $D$ by **ascending** its stochastic gradient:

$$
\nabla_{\theta_D} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[\log D(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(x^{(i)}, G(x^{(i)})))\right]
$$

3. Update $G$ by **descending** its stochastic gradient (note: use $-\log D$ trick to avoid vanishing gradients early in training):

$$
\nabla_{\theta_G} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[\log D(x^{(i)}, G(x^{(i)})) + \lambda \Vert y^{(i)} - G(x^{(i)})\Vert_1\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip — The "$- \log D$" Trick:** Early in training, $D$ easily rejects fake images, making $\log(1 - D(\cdot))$ saturate at 0. The original GAN paper proposed flipping the generator's objective to $-\log D(\cdot)$ to provide stronger gradients. Pix2Pix adopts this trick in practice.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Pix2Pix Architecture for Medical Image Translation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INPUT IMAGE X\nMRI Slice 256x256x1"] --> B["U NET GENERATOR G\nEncoder Decoder with Skip Connections"]
    B --> B1["ENC LAYER 1\nConv 4x4 stride 2\nLeakyReLU 64 channels"]
    B1 --> B2["ENC LAYER 2\n128 channels"]
    B2 --> B3["ENC LAYER 3\n256 channels"]
    B3 --> B4["ENC LAYER 4\n512 channels"]
    B4 --> B5["BOTTLENECK\n512 channels\nDropout 50 percent"]
    B5 --> B6["DEC LAYER 1\nSkip from B4\n512 channels"]
    B6 --> B7["DEC LAYER 2\nSkip from B3\n256 channels"]
    B7 --> B8["DEC LAYER 3\nSkip from B2\n128 channels"]
    B8 --> B9["DEC LAYER 4\nSkip from B1\n64 channels"]
    B9 --> C["OUTPUT IMAGE Y HAT\nSynthetic CT 256x256x1\nTanh activation"]
    A --> D["CHANNEL CONCATENATE\nStack X and Y HAT along channels"]
    C --> D
    A --> E["CHANNEL CONCATENATE REAL\nStack X and Y along channels"]
    F["GROUND TRUTH Y\nReal CT Slice"] --> E
    D --> G["PATCHGAN DISCRIMINATOR D\nClassifies 70x70 patches"]
    E --> G
    G --> H["OUTPUT 30x30 PROB MAP\nEach cell is P real for that patch"]
    C --> I["L1 LOSS\nL_L1 equals Y minus Y HAT absolute"]
    F --> I
    C --> J["ADVERSARIAL LOSS\nlog D of X and Y HAT"]
    H --> J
    I --> K["TOTAL GENERATOR LOSS\nL equals L_cGAN plus lambda times L_L1\nlambda equals 100"]
    J --> K
    H --> L["DISCRIMINATOR LOSS\nBinary cross entropy on real and fake"]
```

### 4.2 U-Net Generator — Skip Connection Detail

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1["ENC BLOCK 1\n256x256x64"] -.->|skip| D4["DEC BLOCK 4\n256x256x64"]
    E2["ENC BLOCK 2\n128x128x128"] -.->|skip| D3["DEC BLOCK 3\n128x128x128"]
    E3["ENC BLOCK 3\n64x64x256"] -.->|skip| D2["DEC BLOCK 2\n64x64x256"]
    E4["ENC BLOCK 4\n32x32x512"] -.->|skip| D1["DEC BLOCK 1\n32x32x512"]
    E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4 --> B["BOTTLENECK\n16x16x512"]
    B --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> OUT["OUTPUT\n256x256x1"]
```

### 4.3 PatchGAN Receptive Field Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IMG["INPUT PAIR 256x256\nX stacked with Y HAT or Y"] --> P1["PATCH 1\n70x70 receptive field"]
    IMG --> P2["PATCH 2\n70x70 receptive field"]
    IMG --> P3["PATCH 3 ... 30x30\nSliding window stride 2"]
    IMG --> P30["PATCH 900\n30x30 grid total"]
    P1 --> C1["D outputs scalar 0 to 1"]
    P2 --> C2["D outputs scalar 0 to 1"]
    P3 --> C3["D outputs scalar 0 to 1"]
    P30 --> C30["D outputs scalar 0 to 1"]
    C1 --> OUT["30x30 PROBABILITY MATRIX\nAverage for D loss"]
    C2 --> OUT
    C3 --> OUT
    C30 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Insight:** The U-Net encoder downsamples the input, the bottleneck processes it at low resolution, and the decoder upsamples back. **Skip connections** are the key — they concatenate encoder feature maps with the corresponding decoder feature maps at the same spatial resolution, preventing loss of high-frequency anatomical detail (e.g., tumor edges, vessel boundaries). This is **why U-Net is universally chosen for medical-image translation** rather than a plain encoder–decoder.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the Pix2Pix framework. What type of problem does it solve, and what is the role of the conditioning input?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pix2Pix is a **Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (cGAN)** for **paired image-to-image translation**, proposed by Isola et al. (2017). It solves the problem of learning a **deterministic mapping** $G: x \to y$ from an input image $x$ to an output image $y$, when paired training examples $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$ are available. The **conditioning input** is the source image $x$, which is fed to **both** the generator and the discriminator — the generator uses it to produce the output, while the discriminator uses it (channel-concatenated with $y$) to judge whether the output–input pair is realistic. In medical imaging, this enables tasks like MRI-to-CT synthesis and low-dose CT denoising.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is PatchGAN? Why is it preferred over a regular full-image discriminator in Pix2Pix?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**PatchGAN** is a discriminator architecture in Pix2Pix that classifies each **$N \times N$ patch** of the input image as real or fake, rather than producing a single global real/fake score. For a $256 \times 256$ image, PatchGAN outputs a $30 \times 30$ probability map.

**Why preferred (3 points):**
1. **Fewer parameters** than a fully connected classifier over the whole image.
2. **Captures local high-frequency statistics** (texture, edges) — critical for medical images where tissue boundaries must be sharp.
3. **Treats the image as a Markov random field**, assuming pixel independence beyond a patch diameter — making the model more generalizable across image sizes.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> In Pix2Pix questions, students **consistently lose marks** for the following reasons:
> 1. **Forgetting the conditioning mechanism** — they describe vanilla GANs without the $x$-input concatenation.
> 2. **Confusing U-Net skip connections with ResNet** — skip connections in U-Net are **channel concatenations**, not additive residuals.
> 3. **Omitting the $L_1$ loss** — the combined objective ($\mathcal{L}_{cGAN} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{L1}$) is the **defining feature** of Pix2Pix; losing this means a 7-mark deduction.
> 4. **Mixing up Pix2Pix and CycleGAN** — Pix2Pix is **paired and supervised**; CycleGAN is unpaired.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q1A. [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the **generator and discriminator** in Pix2Pix, with a labelled diagram of the U-Net skip connections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **complete Pix2Pix loss function** from the standard GAN objective, and justify why $L_1$ is preferred over $L_2$ for medical image translation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Generator and Discriminator Architecture (7 marks):**

**Generator — U-Net (4 marks):**
- The U-Net consists of an **encoder path** (downsampling) and a **decoder path** (upsampling).
- The encoder has 8 convolutional blocks (Conv $\to$ BatchNorm $\to$ LeakyReLU) with stride 2, increasing channels from 64 $\to$ 128 $\to$ 256 $\to$ 512 $\to$ 512 $\to$ 512 $\to$ 512 $\to$ 512.
- The bottleneck uses **dropout (50%)** as the source of stochasticity (noise).
- The decoder has 7 transposed-convolution blocks. At each decoder stage, the feature map is **concatenated channel-wise** with the corresponding encoder feature map (skip connection).
- The final layer uses a `tanh` activation to produce an output image of the same spatial size as the input.

**Discriminator — PatchGAN (3 marks):**
- 4 convolutional layers with increasing channels (64 $\to$ 128 $\to$ 256 $\to$ 512), each followed by BatchNorm and LeakyReLU.
- Final output is a $30 \times 30$ feature map where each entry represents the realism of a $70 \times 70$ receptive field.
- The discriminator takes a **6-channel input** = input image (3) + target image (3), concatenated channel-wise.

**(b) Loss Derivation and Justification (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from the standard GAN min-max:
$$
\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{y}\!\left[\log D(y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{z}\!\left[\log(1 - D(G(z)))\right]
$$

Conditioning on $x$ for both $G$ and $D$:
$$
\mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}\!\left[\log D(x, y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x,z}\!\left[\log(1 - D(x, G(x,z)))\right]
$$

Adding the $L_1$ reconstruction term weighted by $\lambda$:
$$
G^* = \arg\min_G \max_D \left[\mathcal{L}_{cGAN}(G, D) + \lambda \cdot \mathbb{E}_{x,y,z}\!\left[\Vert y - G(x, z) \Vert_1\right]\right]
$$

**Why $L_1$ over $L_2$ (3 marks):**
1. $L_2$ (MSE) penalizes large errors quadratically; the optimal generator output becomes the **conditional mean** of $p(y \vert x)$, which appears blurry when the true distribution is multi-modal (e.g., multiple plausible CT intensities for an MRI region).
2. $L_1$ (MAE) has a **constant gradient magnitude** (sign of the error), encouraging the generator to commit to sharp pixel values rather than averaging.
3. In medical imaging, **lesion edges and tissue boundaries must be sharp** — $L_1$ preserves this; $L_2$ does not.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q1B. [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model]**

**(a)** Explain **conditional GANs (cGANs)** and show how Pix2Pix extends them for image-to-image translation. Write down the conditioning mechanism explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **three major medical imaging applications** of Pix2Pix. For each, identify the input modality, output modality, and the clinical benefit. Mention one **limitation** of Pix2Pix in medical use. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Conditional GANs and Pix2Pix Extension (7 marks):**

A **vanilla GAN** learns an unconditional mapping $G: z \to y$ from random noise to a data distribution. A **cGAN** (Mirza & Osindero, 2014) modifies this to $G: \{x, z\} \to y$, where $x$ is auxiliary information (e.g., a class label or another image).

**Conditioning mechanism (5 marks):**
1. The **generator** takes $x$ (input image) plus noise $z$ and produces a fake image $G(x, z)$.
2. The **discriminator** receives two possible inputs:
   - Real pair: $(x, y)$ where $y$ is ground truth.
   - Fake pair: $(x, G(x, z))$.
3. Both are **channel-concatenated** and passed through $D$.
4. The cGAN objective becomes:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{cGAN} = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}\!\left[\log D(x,y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x,z}\!\left[\log(1 - D(x, G(x,z)))\right]
$$

5. Pix2Pix extends this by adding a **$L_1$ pixel loss** with weight $\lambda$ and using a U-Net generator + PatchGAN discriminator (explained in detail in Q1A).

**(b) Medical Applications and Limitation (7 marks):**

| # | Application | Input | Output | Clinical Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **MRI $\to$ CT synthesis** | T1-weighted MRI | Synthetic CT | Enables **MRI-only radiotherapy planning**; eliminates radiation exposure from CT |
| 2 | **Low-dose $\to$ Normal-dose CT** | Low-dose CT (noisy) | Normal-dose CT (clean) | Reduces **patient X-ray exposure** by 4–10$\times$ while preserving diagnostic quality |
| 3 | **PET $\to$ MRI translation** | PET scan | Pseudo-MRI | Provides **anatomical context** for functional PET data; useful for multimodal augmentation |

**Limitation (2 marks):** Pix2Pix requires **perfectly registered paired training data**. In real clinical settings, paired MRI–CT scans are difficult to obtain because:
- Patient motion between scans causes misalignment.
- Pathologies may change between acquisitions.
This restricts Pix2Pix to retrospective, well-curated datasets — and motivates unpaired alternatives like CycleGAN.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pix2Pix**

- ✅ Pix2Pix is a **conditional GAN (cGAN)** for **paired, supervised** image-to-image translation.
- ✅ Generator: **U-Net** with encoder–decoder + channel-wise skip connections.
- ✅ Discriminator: **PatchGAN** — outputs a $30 \times 30$ map of patch-level real/fake scores.
- ✅ Conditioning is achieved by **channel-concatenating** $x$ with $y$ (or $G(x, z)$) before feeding to $D$.
- ✅ Total loss: $\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{cGAN} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{L1}$, with $\lambda = 100$ standard.
- ✅ $L_1$ is preferred over $L_2$ because $L_2$ produces **blurry mean outputs** — fatal for medical imaging sharpness.
- ✅ Noise is injected via **dropout** in the generator bottleneck, not as an explicit input.
- ✅ Generator is **nearly deterministic** at test time — important for clinical reproducibility.
- ✅ Three flagship medical applications: **MRI$\to$CT synthesis, low-dose CT denoising, PET$\to$MRI translation**.
- ✅ **Key limitation:** requires **registered, paired training data** — drives the move toward unpaired models (CycleGAN).
- ✅ In KTU exams, always state: *"Pix2Pix combines an adversarial loss (for realism) with an $L_1$ pixel loss (for fidelity), trained in a paired-supervised manner."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cycle-GAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT865 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cycle-GAN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cycle-Consistent Generative Adversarial Network (Cycle-GAN)** is a deep generative model introduced by *Zhu et al., 2017 (ICCV)* that performs **unpaired image-to-image translation** between two visual domains $X$ and $Y$ using a pair of adversarial networks coupled with a cycle-consistency constraint. Formally, it learns two mappings — $G: X \rightarrow Y$ and $F: Y \rightarrow X$ — such that the distributions of $G(X)$ and $F(Y)$ are statistically indistinguishable from those of $Y$ and $X$ respectively, while simultaneously enforcing $F(G(x)) \approx x$ and $G(F(y)) \approx y$ for all samples $x \in X, y \in Y$.

In the KTU 2024 medical imaging context, Cycle-GAN is the **workhorse architecture for cross-modal synthesis** (e.g., synthetic CT from MRI to enable PET attenuation correction, MR-only radiotherapy planning, stain normalization in histopathology, and low-dose CT denoising).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cycle-GAN falls under the *Generative Models for Synthesis* sub-module. Students must master the **loss function formulation**, **two-generator/two-discriminator topology**, and the rationale behind **cycle consistency** (why unpaired translation is mathematically tractable).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Translator Librarians"

Imagine two librarians, **Alice** and **Bob**, who work in a university with two sections: an **English library** (Domain $X$) and a **French library** (Domain $Y$).

1. **Alice ($G$)** takes an English book and tries to *write its French translation* (synthesizes $Y$).
2. **Bob ($F$)** takes a French book and tries to *write its English translation* (synthesizes $X$).
3. There is a **strict rule**: the French translation of an English book, when re-translated back to English by Bob, must yield the **original book**. This is the *cycle consistency rule*.
4. An **examiner** ($D_X$) checks whether Bob's English output looks like a real English book (adversarial test on $X$), and another examiner ($D_Y$) judges whether Alice's French output looks like a real French book.

No **parallel dictionary** is required — Alice and Bob learn purely by *self-supervision*, comparing round-trip translations to originals. This is exactly how Cycle-GAN trains **without paired data**, which is critical in medicine where paired CT–MRI of the same patient at the same moment is rarely available.

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU 2024

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Domain $X$ | Source modality (e.g., non-contrast CT) |
| Domain $Y$ | Target modality (e.g., T1-weighted MRI) |
| Generator $G$ | Maps $X \rightarrow Y$ |
| Generator $F$ | Maps $Y \rightarrow X$ |
| Discriminator $D_X$ | Distinguishes real $X$ from $F(Y)$ |
| Discriminator $D_Y$ | Distinguishes real $Y$ from $G(X)$ |
| Cycle consistency | $F(G(x)) \approx x$ and $G(F(y)) \approx y$ |
| Unpaired training | No one-to-one correspondence required |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Medical Metric:** Cycle-GAN outputs in medical image synthesis are typically evaluated using **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio, in dB)**, **SSIM (Structural Similarity Index, range 0–1)**, and **MAE (Mean Absolute Error, in HU for CT)**. Higher PSNR and SSIM, lower MAE — indicate better translation fidelity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Topology — Why Two of Everything?

A vanilla GAN learns a single mapping with one generator and one discriminator. For *unpaired* translation, a single generator is **under-constrained** — it can map any $X$ to *any* $Y$ that fools $D_Y$, producing mode collapse. Cycle-GAN resolves this through **bijective-style coupling**: two generators and two discriminators work in tandem with a **cycle-consistency regularizer**.

## 2.2 The Three Loss Components

### 2.2.1 Adversarial Loss (LSGAN-style)

For generator $G$ and its discriminator $D_Y$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(G, D_Y, X, Y) = \mathbb{E}_{y \sim p_{\text{data}}(y)} \left[ (D_Y(y) - 1)^2 \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)} \left[ D_Y(G(x))^2 \right]
$$

And symmetrically for $F$ and $D_X$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(F, D_X, Y, X) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)} \left[ (D_X(x) - 1)^2 \right] + \mathbb{E}_{y \sim p_{\text{data}}(y)} \left[ D_X(F(y))^2 \right]
$$

> **Why Least-Squares GAN (LSGAN) loss instead of vanilla BCE?** LSGAN provides smoother gradients and mitigates vanishing-gradient problems when the discriminator confidently rejects fake samples, which is empirically critical in medical images with high dynamic range.

### 2.2.2 Cycle-Consistency Loss (L1)

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}(G, F) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)} \left[ \Vert F(G(x)) - x \Vert_1 \right] + \mathbb{E}_{y \sim p_{\text{data}}(y)} \left[ \Vert G(F(y)) - y \Vert_1 \right]
$$

This is the **mathematical heart** of Cycle-GAN. It penalizes the L1 distance between an input and its round-trip reconstruction, enforcing that translation is **information-preserving**.

### 2.2.3 Identity Loss (Optional Regularizer)

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}(G, F) = \mathbb{E}_{y \sim p_{\text{data}}(y)} \left[ \Vert G(y) - y \Vert_1 \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(x)} \left[ \Vert F(x) - x \Vert_1 \right]
$$

> **Intuition:** If you feed a target-domain image to the target-producing generator, it should output the image unchanged (preserve color/contrast/tone).

### 2.2.4 Full Objective

$$
\mathcal{L}(G, F, D_X, D_Y) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(G, D_Y) + \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(F, D_X) + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}(G, F) + \lambda_{\text{id}} \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}(G, F)
$$

The optimization is the standard adversarial min-max:

$$
G^*, F^* = \arg\min_{G,F} \max_{D_X, D_Y} \mathcal{L}(G, F, D_X, D_Y)
$$

with $\lambda = 10$ (default) and $\lambda_{\text{id}} = 0.5 \cdot \lambda = 5$ in the original paper.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Value | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}$ | $\mathbb{E}[(D(y)-1)^2] + \mathbb{E}[D(G(x))^2]$ | Adversarial loss (LSGAN) | dimensionless |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}$ | $\Vert F(G(x)) - x \Vert_1 + \Vert G(F(y)) - y \Vert_1$ | Cycle consistency (L1) | pixel intensity units |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$ | $\Vert G(y)-y \Vert_1 + \Vert F(x)-x \Vert_1$ | Identity loss | pixel intensity units |
| $\lambda$ | $10$ | Cycle weight | dimensionless |
| $\lambda_{\text{id}}$ | $0.5 \lambda$ | Identity weight | dimensionless |
| PSNR | $20 \log_{10}(\text{MAX}_I / \sqrt{\text{MSE}})$ | Fidelity metric | dB |
| SSIM | $\frac{(2\mu_x \mu_y + c_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + c_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + c_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + c_2)}$ | Structural similarity | unitless (0–1) |
| MAE | $\frac{1}{N}\sum \vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \vert$ | Mean absolute error | HU (CT) / intensity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
