> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE:** When asked for the full objective, **always include all three losses** and the $\lambda$ weighting — failing to mention $\lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}$ is a 4-mark deduction in KTU 2024 ESE valuation.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Medical Engineering

| Application | Domain $X$ | Domain $Y$ | Clinical Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| MR-based attenuation correction | MRI | Pseudo-CT | PET quantification without CT dose |
| MR-only radiotherapy planning | MRI | Pseudo-CT | Eliminates registration errors |
| Stain normalization | H&E source lab | H&E target lab | Computational pathology harmonization |
| Low-dose denoising | Low-dose CT | Normal-dose CT | Reduces patient radiation exposure |
| Cross-modality augmentation | T1 MRI | T2 / FLAIR MRI | Expands scarce labeled datasets |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cycle-Consistency Motivation

### Step 1 — The Underdetermined Translation Problem

Suppose we have a single generator $G: X \to Y$ trained only with adversarial loss. The feasible set of solutions is:

$$
\{ G : p_{G(X)} \approx p_Y \}
$$

This set is **infinite** — there are infinitely many mappings that produce a $Y$-distribution indistinguishable from real $Y$. For example, $G$ could map *every* $x$ to the same canonical $y^*$ (mode collapse) and still pass the discriminator eventually.

### Step 2 — Adding Bijective-Style Coupling

Introduce a second generator $F: Y \to X$ and require:

$$
F(G(x)) = x, \quad G(F(y)) = y \quad \forall x \in X,\; y \in Y
$$

In practice, exact equality is replaced by an L1 penalty because pixel-level exact reconstruction is generally infeasible for stochastic generators. The penalty is:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} = \mathbb{E}_{x} \Vert F(G(x)) - x \Vert_1 + \mathbb{E}_{y} \Vert G(F(y)) - y \Vert_1
$$

### Step 3 — Why L1 Instead of L2?

L1 produces **sparser residuals** (encouraging per-pixel exactness), while L2 over-penalizes large errors and under-penalizes many small ones — undesirable for preserving fine anatomical detail. In medical imaging, L1 is empirically the standard.

## 3.2 Derivation of the LSGAN Adversarial Loss

The original GAN uses the BCE loss:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}}(D) = -\mathbb{E}_y [\log D(y)] - \mathbb{E}_x [\log(1 - D(G(x)))]
$$

Mao et al. (2017) showed this saturates when $D$ confidently rejects fakes. The LSGAN replaces it with a least-squares objective. Setting $a=1, b=0$ (target labels for real/fake) and $c=1$ (generator's target):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{LSGAN}}(D) = \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_y [(D(y) - 1)^2] + \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_x [D(G(x))^2]
$$

The corresponding generator loss pulls fake samples toward the real label $1$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{LSGAN}}(G) = \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_x [(D(G(x)) - 1)^2]
$$

This is exactly what Cycle-GAN uses, dropping the constant $\tfrac{1}{2}$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Optimal Discriminator

For a fixed $G$, the optimal $D_Y^*$ is found by minimizing the adversarial loss w.r.t. $D_Y$:

$$
D_Y^*(y) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(y)}{p_{\text{data}}(y) + p_G(y)}
$$

where $p_G(y)$ is the density induced by $G(X)$. Substituting back yields the Jensen–Shannon-like divergence minimized at $p_G = p_{\text{data}}$, which is the standard GAN convergence result extended to LSGAN's $\chi^2$ divergence.

## 3.4 Combined Optimization Algorithm (Pseudocode-Equivalent Steps)

| Step | Operation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample mini-batch $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ from domain $X$ and $\{y_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ from domain $Y$ | Stochastic gradient estimation |
| 2 | Update $D_X$ to maximize $\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(F, D_X, Y, X)$ | Train discriminators first (frozen) |
| 3 | Update $D_Y$ to maximize $\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(G, D_Y, X, Y)$ | Train discriminators first (frozen) |
| 4 | Update $G$ to minimize $\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(G) + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$ | Fool discriminators + preserve information |
| 5 | Update $F$ to minimize $\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(F) + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$ | Fool discriminators + preserve information |
| 6 | Repeat for $K$ iterations until $D$ accuracy $\approx 0.5$ | Equilibrium ≈ Nash |

## 3.5 Gradient Flow — Worked Analytic Example

Let the input be a single image $x \in X$ with target $y \in Y$. Assume the generators are linear: $G(x) = W_G x$, $F(y) = W_F y$. Then:

$$
F(G(x)) = W_F W_G x
$$

The cycle loss gradient w.r.t. $W_G$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}}{\partial W_G} = \text{sign}(W_F W_G x - x) \cdot W_F^T
$$

The adversarial loss pushes $W_G x$ toward the $Y$ manifold, while the cycle loss gradient (above) pulls $W_F W_G x$ back toward $x$. At equilibrium, $W_F W_G \approx I$ (identity) and $W_G x \approx y$, giving $W_F y \approx x$ — the desired bijection. This is the **information-preservation contract** in analytic form.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Cycle-GAN Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Domain X Sample: x] --> G1[Generator G]
    B[Domain Y Sample: y] --> F1[Generator F]
    G1 -->|G of x| YF[Synthesized y hat]
    YF --> F1
    F1 -->|F of G of x| XR[Reconstructed x hat]
    B --> F1
    F1 -->|F of y| XF[Synthesized x hat]
    XF --> G1
    G1 -->|G of F of y| YR[Reconstructed y hat]
    YF --> DY[Discriminator D of Y]
    B --> DY
    XF --> DX[Discriminator D of X]
    A --> DX
    DY -->|Adversarial Loss L GAN| LOSS[Combined Loss]
    DX -->|Adversarial Loss L GAN| LOSS
    XR -->|Cycle Loss L cyc| LOSS
    YR -->|Cycle Loss L cyc| LOSS
    LOSS --> OPT[Optimizer Adam lr 2e-4 beta1 0.5]
```

## 4.2 Generator Internal Architecture (ResNet-9 Blocks)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INP[Input Image 1ch or 3ch 256x256] --> C1[Conv7x64 stride 1 pad 3 Instance Norm ReLU]
    C1 --> C2[Conv3x128 stride 2 pad 1 Instance Norm ReLU]
    C2 --> C3[Conv3x256 stride 2 pad 1 Instance Norm ReLU]
    C3 --> R1[ResBlock 256 channels]
    R1 --> R2[ResBlock 256]
    R2 --> R3[ResBlock 256]
    R3 --> R4[ResBlock 256]
    R4 --> R5[ResBlock 256]
    R5 --> R6[ResBlock 256]
    R6 --> R7[ResBlock 256]
    R7 --> R8[ResBlock 256]
    R8 --> R9[ResBlock 256]
    R9 --> DT1[DeConv 256 to 128 stride 2 Instance Norm ReLU]
    DT1 --> DT2[DeConv 128 to 64 stride 2 Instance Norm ReLU]
    DT2 --> CO[Conv 7x3 stride 1 pad 3 Tanh activation]
    CO --> OUTP[Synthesized Output Image]
```

## 4.3 PatchGAN Discriminator (70 × 70 receptive field)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PIN[Input Image 256x256] --> PC1[Conv4x64 stride 2 Instance Norm LeakyReLU 0.2]
    PC1 --> PC2[Conv4x128 stride 2 Instance Norm LeakyReLU 0.2]
    PC2 --> PC3[Conv4x256 stride 2 Instance Norm LeakyReLU 0.2]
    PC3 --> PC4[Conv4x512 stride 1 Instance Norm LeakyReLU 0.2]
    PC4 --> PC5[Conv4x1 stride 1 no norm]
    PC5 --> POUT[Patch Map 30x30 Real or Fake Probability per patch]
```

## 4.4 Full Training-Pipeline Block Diagram for Medical Image Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DATA[Data Acquisition]
        DX1[CT Scanner Patient 1 to N unpaired]
        DX2[MRI Scanner Patient 1 to M unpaired]
    end
    subgraph PREP[Preprocessing]
        PR1[Resample to 1x1x1 mm cubed]
        PR2[Intensity Normalization HU or Z score]
        PR3[Random Crop 256x256 Axial Slice]
    end
    subgraph CYCLE[Cycle GAN Training]
        G1A[Generator G CT to MRI]
        G1B[Generator F MRI to CT]
        D1A[Discriminator D of MRI]
        D1B[Discriminator D of CT]
    end
    subgraph EVAL[Validation Module]
        EV1[PSNR in dB]
        EV2[SSIM index]
        EV3[MAE in HU]
    end
    DX1 --> PR1
    DX2 --> PR1
    PR1 --> PR2 --> PR3
    PR3 --> G1A
    PR3 --> G1B
    G1A --> D1A
    G1B --> D1B
    G1A --> EV1
    G1A --> EV2
    G1A --> EV3
    EV1 --> OUT[Deployment Synthetic MRI in PACS]
    EV2 --> OUT
    EV3 --> OUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`
**Define Cycle-GAN. State the two key losses that enable unpaired image-to-image translation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cycle-GAN is a generative adversarial network architecture (1 mark) for unpaired image-to-image translation between two domains $X$ and $Y$ (1 mark). The two key losses are: (i) **Adversarial Loss** — forces generated images to be indistinguishable from real images in the target domain, and (ii) **Cycle-Consistency Loss** — penalizes the difference between an input image and its reconstruction after a forward–backward round-trip translation, ensuring $F(G(x)) \approx x$ (1 mark).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Supplementary]`
**What is the role of the identity loss in Cycle-GAN? In which medical-imaging scenario is it most beneficial?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The identity loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}} = \mathbb{E}_y \Vert G(y) - y \Vert_1 + \mathbb{E}_x \Vert F(x) - x \Vert_1$ (1.5 marks) encourages a generator to behave as an identity function when given an image already in its target domain, thereby preserving color/contrast/tone (1 mark). It is most beneficial in **stain normalization** and **cross-scanner harmonization**, where the generator should not distort a target-style image (0.5 mark).

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the **full objective function** of Cycle-GAN. Clearly define the two generators, two discriminators, the three loss components, and the weighting hyperparameters. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain with a medical-imaging example **why cycle consistency is necessary** when a single-generator GAN would fail. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Let domains be $X$ (source) and $Y$ (target). Generators $G: X \to Y$ and $F: Y \to X$. Discriminators $D_Y$ and $D_X$ judge authenticity of images in their respective domains (1 mark).

**Adversarial Loss for $G$ and $D_Y$** (LSGAN form):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(G, D_Y) = \mathbb{E}_{y} \left[ (D_Y(y) - 1)^2 \right] + \mathbb{E}_{x} \left[ D_Y(G(x))^2 \right]
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Adversarial Loss for $F$ and $D_X$** (symmetric):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(F, D_X) = \mathbb{E}_{x} \left[ (D_X(x) - 1)^2 \right] + \mathbb{E}_{y} \left[ D_X(F(y))^2 \right]
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Cycle-Consistency Loss** (L1):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}(G, F) = \mathbb{E}_x \Vert F(G(x)) - x \Vert_1 + \mathbb{E}_y \Vert G(F(y)) - y \Vert_1
$$

(1.5 marks)

**Identity Loss** (optional but credited):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}(G, F) = \mathbb{E}_y \Vert G(y) - y \Vert_1 + \mathbb{E}_x \Vert F(x) - x \Vert_1
$$

(0.5 mark)

**Full Objective** with weights $\lambda$ (cycle) and $\lambda_{\text{id}}$ (identity):

$$
\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(G, D_Y) + \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(F, D_X) + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}} \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}
$$

(1 mark)

Optimized as $G^*, F^* = \arg\min_{G,F} \max_{D_X, D_Y} \mathcal{L}$. Default $\lambda = 10$.

---

**(b) Medical-Imaging Failure Mode (7 marks):**

A single generator $G$ trained only with adversarial loss on domain $Y$ can produce $G(x)$ that is statistically indistinguishable from real $Y$ **without preserving any anatomical correspondence** to $x$ (1 mark).

**Concrete example — CT → MRI synthesis for MR-only radiotherapy planning (2 marks):**
Suppose the generator maps *every* input CT slice to the *mean* T1-weighted MRI of the training set. The discriminator accepts it because the marginal distribution matches, but per-patient anatomy is destroyed — the resulting "MRI" has no tumor, no organ boundaries, no useful structure for the radiation oncologist. This is **mode collapse** (1 mark).

**Why cycle consistency rescues this (2 marks):**
By demanding $F(G(x)) \approx x$, the model is forced to encode *patient-specific* anatomical information into $G(x)$ — otherwise reconstruction back to the original CT is impossible. Cycle consistency acts as a **perceptual identity contract** (1 mark).

**Clinical consequence:** Without cycle consistency, MR-only treatment plans would have wrong tumor contours → radiation miss → treatment failure. Cycle-GAN's cycle loss makes the synthesized MRI a *bijection-faithful* proxy (1 mark — wrap-up).

---

### Question B — Alternative Internal Choice (14 Marks)

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Cycle-GAN, Pix2Pix, and UNIT** for medical image-to-image translation. Tabulate differences in supervision, loss, and clinical use. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **failure modes of Cycle-GAN** in medical image synthesis (hallucination, mode collapse, intensity shift) and how they impact diagnosis. Suggest one mitigation for each. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution (Outline)

**(a)** Comparison table (7 marks — distribute ~1.2 marks per row × 5 substantive rows, with intro 1 mark):

| Feature | Pix2Pix | Cycle-GAN | UNIT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Supervision | Paired (pixel-wise) | Unpaired | Unpaired |
| Loss | L1 + Adversarial | Adv + Cycle + Identity | Adv + Cycle + VAE |
| Cycle constraint | Not needed | Yes | Yes (latent space) |
| Use case | Same-patient CT↔MRI | Cross-patient synthesis | Multi-modal harmonization |
| Failure on small data | Low (with paired) | Medium (needs volume) | High (VAE unstable) |

(1 mark) Verdict: Pix2Pix preferred when paired data is available; otherwise Cycle-GAN is the KTU-recommended default.

**(b) Failure modes (7 marks):**
- **Hallucination** — Cycle-GAN can invent tumors/vessels absent in source. Mitigation: add a **structure-consistency loss** using segmentation masks as auxiliary supervision (2 marks).
- **Mode collapse** — Generator outputs the same "average" image. Mitigation: use **minibatch discrimination** in $D$ or reduce learning rate (2 marks).
- **Intensity shift** — HU values drift from real CT distribution. Mitigation: add a **pixel-domain L1 loss between $G(x)$ and a small paired validation set** (2 marks).
- Wrap-up: 1 mark on clinical impact summary (e.g., pathology over-call, treatment planning errors).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Omitting the $\lambda$ weight in the full objective** → −2 marks. Always write $\lambda \cdot \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}$.
> 2. **Confusing cycle consistency with cycle loss** — consistency is the *concept*, loss is the *math expression*; examiners want both.
> 3. **Forgetting that Cycle-GAN is *unpaired*** — confusing it with Pix2Pix costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Writing generators as $D_G$** — common typo, treat as wrong notation.
> 5. **Skipping the discriminator's role** in the objective — must include $\max_{D_X, D_Y}$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Cycle-GAN performs **unpaired** cross-domain image translation using **two generators** ($G$, $F$) and **two discriminators** ($D_X$, $D_Y$).
- The **three loss components** are: adversarial (LSGAN), cycle-consistency (L1), and identity (L1, optional).
- The **full objective** is $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}^G + \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}^F + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$ with default $\lambda = 10$.
- Cycle consistency enforces $F(G(x)) \approx x$ and $G(F(y)) \approx y$, preventing **mode collapse** and preserving **anatomical correspondence**.
- The **generator** is a ResNet-9-block encoder–decoder; the **discriminator** is a 70×70 PatchGAN.
- **Medical applications**: CT↔MRI synthesis, MR-only radiotherapy, stain normalization, low-dose CT denoising, modality augmentation.
- **Evaluation metrics** in clinical practice: **PSNR (dB) ↑**, **SSIM (0–1) ↑**, **MAE (HU) ↓**.
- **Failure modes**: hallucination, mode collapse, intensity shift — each requires a targeted mitigation.
- **Vs. Pix2Pix**: Cycle-GAN is unpaired; Pix2Pix requires pixel-aligned training pairs.
- **Identity loss** is most valuable in **stain normalization** and tone-preservation tasks.
- **Discriminator update rule** is *2:1* or *1:1* with generator, using Adam ($\beta_1 = 0.5$, $\text{lr} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometry-Consistent GAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT865 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Geometry-Consistent GAN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Geometry-Consistent Generative Adversarial Network (GC-GAN)** is a specialized deep generative architecture designed for **unpaired cross-modality medical image translation** (e.g., CT $\rightarrow$ MRI, MR $\rightarrow$ CT) in which the translated image is enforced to share the **same underlying anatomical geometry** as the source image. Geometry is operationalized through **semantic segmentation masks** that act as a privileged structural prior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Generative Models in Medical Imaging):**
> Geometry-Consistent GANs extend the standard **CycleGAN** framework by replacing the strict cycle-reconstruction constraint with a **soft, mask-guided geometry consistency loss**, allowing realistic one-to-many mappings while preventing hallucinated anatomical structures.

Formally, given source domain $X$ (with corresponding segmentation masks $S_X$) and target domain $Y$, a GC-GAN learns two mappings:

$$G_{X \rightarrow Y}: (x, s_x) \mapsto \hat{y}, \qquad G_{Y \rightarrow X}: (y, s_y) \mapsto \hat{x}$$

such that the geometry of $\hat{y}$ (and $\hat{x}$) is **spatially aligned** with that of $x$ (and $y$, respectively).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sketch Artist Who Must Respect the Blueprint"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):**
> Imagine a **forensic sketch artist** who is asked to render a photograph of a suspect in **two different lighting conditions** — daylight and infrared. A naive artist might redraw the suspect from scratch, potentially changing the face, the hair, or the position of scars. A **geometry-consistent artist**, however, is given the **same anatomical blueprint (segmentation mask)** for both renderings. The face shape, the eyes, the jawline, and the moles **must remain in the exact same spatial locations**; only the **appearance (intensity, contrast, texture)** changes. GC-GANs do exactly this: the **mask is the blueprint**; the GAN only changes the **appearance (modality)**.

### 1.3 Why Geometry Consistency Matters in Medical Imaging

- **Cycle-consistency alone is too rigid** — forces a bijective mapping, which is medically unrealistic (one CT slice can map to many plausible MR appearances).
- **Anatomical hallucination is dangerous** — a generator that "invents" a tumor or erases a ventricle is clinically catastrophic.
- **Modality-invariant geometry is a valid prior** — bones, organs, vessels occupy the **same physical coordinates** in CT and MR.

| Property | CycleGAN | Geometry-Consistent GAN |
|---|---|---|
| Cycle constraint | **Hard (pixel/feature)** | **Soft (mask-guided)** |
| One-to-many mapping | ✗ | ✓ |
| Requires segmentation masks | ✗ | ✓ |
| Clinical safety | Moderate | **High** |
| Hallucination risk | Higher | **Lower** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline — Six Logic Steps

1. **Dual-Domain Input Stream** — The model ingests two modalities (e.g., $X$ = CT, $Y$ = MR) **plus** their pre-aligned segmentation masks ($S_X$, $S_Y$).
2. **Generator Forward Pass** — The generator $G_{X \rightarrow Y}$ receives a **concatenated tensor** $\left[x \, \vert\, s_x\right]$ and produces the translated image $\hat{y}$.
3. **Geometry-Consistency Projection** — A differentiable segmentation network $F$ is applied to $\hat{y}$ to predict $\hat{s}_y$. The loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$ penalizes the divergence between $\hat{s}_y$ and $s_x$.
4. **Adversarial Discrimination** — A domain discriminator $D_Y$ judges whether $\hat{y}$ is a real MR image or a synthesized one (standard GAN objective).
5. **Cycle + Geometry Joint Objective** — The total loss combines adversarial, cycle, identity, and geometry terms with weighting coefficients.
6. **Symmetric Reverse Path** — The mirror direction $G_{Y \rightarrow X}$ is trained simultaneously, enforcing consistency in both directions.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1** — masks inject **anatomical prior knowledge** without requiring paired data.
- **Step 3** — the predicted mask on the generated image is the **geometric fingerprint** that must match the source.
- **Step 5** — the joint objective prevents mode collapse while keeping anatomy intact.
- **Step 6** — symmetric training ensures the generator is **bidirectionally consistent**, important for downstream registration and fusion tasks.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}}^{Y}$ | Adversarial loss (target domain) | $\mathbb{E}_{y \sim Y} \left[\log D_Y(y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim X} \left[\log(1 - D_Y(G_{X \rightarrow Y}(x, s_x)))\right]$ |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}$ | Cycle-consistency loss | $\Vert G_{Y \rightarrow X}(G_{X \rightarrow Y}(x, s_x), s_x) - x \Vert_1$ |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$ | Identity loss | $\Vert G_{X \rightarrow Y}(y, s_y) - y \Vert_1$ |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$ | Geometry-consistency loss | $\Vert F(G_{X \rightarrow Y}(x, s_x)) - s_x \Vert_1$ |
| $\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}}$ | Composite objective | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}} + \lambda_{\text{cyc}}\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}}\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}} + \lambda_{\text{geo}}\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$ |
| $\lambda_{\text{cyc}}$ | Cycle weight (KTU typical) | **10** |
| $\lambda_{\text{id}}$ | Identity weight (KTU typical) | **0.5** |
| $\lambda_{\text{geo}}$ | Geometry weight (KTU typical) | **1–5** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Reminder:** Throughout KTU boards, $\Vert \cdot \Vert_1$ denotes the **L1 (mean absolute error) norm**, the preferred pixel-wise loss in medical image synthesis.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radiation-free MR-only radiotherapy planning** — synthesize CT from MR for dose calculation.
- **Modality imputation** — recover missing MRI sequences (e.g., T2 from T1) for patients with incomplete scans.
- **Data augmentation** for downstream **segmentation networks** when one modality is scarce.
- **Cross-modal registration preprocessing** — geometry-consistent pairs improve rigid/deformable registration accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Geometry-Consistency Loss

Let $F: \mathbb{R}^{H \times W} \rightarrow [0,1]^{C \times H \times W}$ be a pre-trained, **frozen** segmentation network with $C$ anatomical classes. Given a generated image $\hat{y} = G_{X \rightarrow Y}(x, s_x)$, the predicted soft mask is:

$$F(\hat{y}) = \text{softmax}\left(\text{SegNet}(\hat{y})\right) \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times H \times W}$$

The geometry-consistency loss enforces that $F(\hat{y})$ reproduces the source mask $s_x$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}} = \frac{1}{C \cdot H \cdot W} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \sum_{i=1}^{H} \sum_{j=1}^{W} \left\vert F(\hat{y})_{c,i,j} - s_{x,c,i,j} \right\vert$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}} &= \frac{1}{CHW} \sum_{c,i,j} \left\vert F(\hat{y})_{c,i,j} - s_{x,c,i,j} \right\vert \\[4pt]
&= \mathbb{E}_{c,i,j}\left[\left\vert F(\hat{y})_{c,i,j} - s_{x,c,i,j} \right\vert\right] \\[4pt]
&\approx \mathbb{E}_{x \sim X}\left[\left\Vert F(G_{X \rightarrow Y}(x, s_x)) - s_x \right\Vert_1\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Minimizing $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$ pushes the generator to produce an image $\hat{y}$ whose **predicted anatomical layout** matches the source mask $s_x$ — i.e., the right structures appear in the right places.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Full Composite Objective

The complete GC-GAN objective for one direction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{GC-GAN}} &= \mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}}^{X} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}}^{Y} \\[4pt]
&\quad + \lambda_{\text{cyc}}\left(\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}^{X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow X} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}^{Y \rightarrow X \rightarrow Y}\right) \\[4pt]
&\quad + \lambda_{\text{id}}\left(\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}^{X} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}^{Y}\right) \\[4pt]
&\quad + \lambda_{\text{geo}}\left(\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}^{X \rightarrow Y} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}^{Y \rightarrow X}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting each term explicitly:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}}^{Y} &= \mathbb{E}_{y}\left[\log D_Y(y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x}\left[\log(1 - D_Y(\hat{y}))\right] \\[4pt]
\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}^{X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow X} &= \mathbb{E}_{x}\left[\Vert G_{Y \rightarrow X}(\hat{y}, s_x) - x \Vert_1\right] \\[4pt]
\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}^{X \rightarrow Y} &= \mathbb{E}_{x}\left[\Vert F(\hat{y}) - s_x \Vert_1\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The geometry term is the **only term that depends on the segmentation network** $F$. This is what differentiates a GC-GAN from a vanilla CycleGAN. If a student writes the CycleGAN objective and forgets $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$, they are describing the **wrong architecture**.

### 3.3 Loss-Weight Sensitivity Analysis (Analytical Matrix)

| $\lambda_{\text{geo}}$ Value | Effect on Synthesis |
|---|---|
| $\lambda_{\text{geo}} = 0$ | Reduces to **CycleGAN** — no explicit geometry constraint |
| $\lambda_{\text{geo}} = 1$ | Mild geometry guidance — softer translations |
| $\lambda_{\text{geo}} = 5$ | **Recommended range** — strong anatomical fidelity |
| $\lambda_{\text{geo}} \to \infty$ | Generator collapses to identity (no modality change) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Begin with $\lambda_{\text{geo}} = 1$ and increase if mask-Dice on synthesized images $< 0.85$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of GC-GAN

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeI["CT IMAGE X"] --> nodeJ["CONCAT WITH MASK SX"]
    nodeK["SEG MASK SX"] --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeA["GENERATOR G_XtoY"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["SYNTHESIZED MR Y_HAT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["FROZEN SEG NET F"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PREDICTED MASK S_YHAT"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["GEOMETRY LOSS L_GEO"]
    nodeK --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF["DISCRIMINATOR D_Y"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["ADVERSARIAL LOSS L_ADV"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["CYCLE PATH G_YtoX"]
    nodeH --> nodeI2["RECONSTRUCTED CT X_HAT"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ2["CYCLE LOSS L_CYC"]
    nodeI2 --> nodeJ2
```

### 4.2 Training-Time Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1["REAL CT X"] --> nodeM1["CONCAT SX"]
    nodeS2["REAL MR Y"] --> nodeM2["CONCAT SY"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeG1["G_XtoY"]
    nodeM2 --> nodeG2["G_YtoX"]
    nodeG1 --> nodeR1["FAKE MR Y_HAT"]
    nodeG2 --> nodeR2["FAKE CT X_HAT"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeF1["FROZEN SEG F"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeF2["FROZEN SEG F"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeL1["L_GEO_XtoY"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeL2["L_GEO_YtoX"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeD1["D_Y"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeD2["D_X"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeC1["CYCLE G_YtoX"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeC2["CYCLE G_XtoY"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeCL["L_CYC"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeCL
```

### 4.3 Geometry vs. Cycle Consistency — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeZ["CYCLEGAN IDEA"] --> nodeW["BIJECTIVE PIXEL MAPPING"]
    nodeV["GC-GAN IDEA"] --> nodeW2["SOFT MASK GUIDED MAPPING"]
    nodeW --> nodeX["RISK: ANATOMICAL HALLUCINATION"]
    nodeW2 --> nodeY["SAFETY: ANATOMY PRESERVED"]
    nodeX --> nodeP["BAD CLINICAL OUTCOME"]
    nodeY --> nodeQ["GOOD CLINICAL OUTCOME"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** Always label the **four loss pathways** (adversarial, cycle, identity, geometry) in your exam diagrams. Examiners specifically award marks for **complete loss-flow drawings**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
> *Define a Geometry-Consistent GAN. How does it differ from a vanilla CycleGAN in unpaired medical image translation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Geometry-Consistent GAN (GC-GAN) is an unpaired image-to-image translation network that uses **semantic segmentation masks as an anatomical prior** to enforce that the **spatial layout of organs/structures is preserved** between the source and translated image.
- **CycleGAN** enforces pixel/feature-level cycle-reconstruction — strict bijective mapping, prone to anatomical hallucination.
- **GC-GAN** enforces a **soft, mask-based geometry consistency loss** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}} = \Vert F(\hat{y}) - s_x \Vert_1$, allowing one-to-many modality translations while keeping anatomy intact.
- Key distinction: GC-GAN **requires pre-aligned segmentation masks**; CycleGAN does not. *(1 mark per bullet.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Equivalent]**
> *List and briefly explain any THREE loss components used in training a Geometry-Consistent GAN.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Adversarial Loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}}$** — pushes the generator to produce realistic target-domain images via a discriminator.
2. **Cycle-Consistency Loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}$** — ensures $G_{Y \rightarrow X}(G_{X \rightarrow Y}(x)) \approx x$, preserving background and global structure.
3. **Geometry-Consistency Loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$** — enforces the generated image's predicted mask matches the source mask, preserving anatomical geometry.

*(Identity loss may also be cited as a valid third option.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
>
> *(a)* Derive the **complete composite loss function** of a Geometry-Consistent GAN used for unpaired CT $\rightarrow$ MR translation. Clearly state each term, its mathematical form, and the role of its weighting coefficient. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* With the aid of a **labelled block diagram**, explain the **forward pass** of a GC-GAN during training. Highlight the role of the **frozen segmentation network** $F$ and discuss what happens if $F$ is **not frozen**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

The total GC-GAN objective combines four families of loss terms, summed over both translation directions $X \rightarrow Y$ and $Y \rightarrow X$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} &= \mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}}^{X} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}}^{Y} \\[4pt]
&\quad + \lambda_{\text{cyc}}\left(\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}^{X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow X} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}^{Y \rightarrow X \rightarrow Y}\right) \\[4pt]
&\quad + \lambda_{\text{id}}\left(\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}^{X} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}^{Y}\right) \\[4pt]
&\quad + \lambda_{\text{geo}}\left(\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}^{X \rightarrow Y} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}^{Y \rightarrow X}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Term-by-term justification (1 mark each, plus 3 for assembly):**

1. **Adversarial loss** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}}^{Y} = \mathbb{E}_y[\log D_Y(y)] + \mathbb{E}_x[\log(1 - D_Y(G_{X \rightarrow Y}(x, s_x)))]$ — realism.
2. **Cycle loss** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}^{X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow X} = \mathbb{E}_x[\Vert G_{Y \rightarrow X}(G_{X \rightarrow Y}(x, s_x), s_x) - x \Vert_1$ — bidirectional reconstruction.
3. **Identity loss** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}^{Y} = \mathbb{E}_y[\Vert G_{X \rightarrow Y}(y, s_y) - y \Vert_1$ — color/tonal preservation.
4. **Geometry loss** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}^{X \rightarrow Y} = \mathbb{E}_x[\Vert F(G_{X \rightarrow Y}(x, s_x)) - s_x \Vert_1$ — anatomical consistency.
5. **Weighting coefficients** $\lambda_{\text{cyc}} = 10$, $\lambda_{\text{id}} = 0.5$, $\lambda_{\text{geo}} \in [1, 5]$ — balance relative contributions.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Block diagram (text representation for answer sheet):**

> Input $X$ $\rightarrow$ Concat with $S_X$ $\rightarrow$ $G_{X \rightarrow Y}$ $\rightarrow$ $\hat{Y}$ $\rightarrow$ (i) Frozen $F$ $\rightarrow$ predicted $\hat{S}_Y$ $\rightarrow$ $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$ against $S_X$; (ii) $D_Y$ $\rightarrow$ $\mathcal{L}_{\text{adv}}$; (iii) $G_{Y \rightarrow X}$ cycle $\rightarrow$ $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}$.

**Role of Frozen $F$ (3 marks):**
- $F$ acts as a **fixed anatomical judge** — provides a stable gradient signal regarding geometry.
- Only $G$ and $D$ are updated; $F$'s weights remain constant.
- This decoupling prevents $F$ from "cheating" by learning to match the generator's output.

**If $F$ is NOT frozen (2 marks):**
- $F$ would co-adapt to $G$'s outputs, producing **deceptively low** $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$.
- The geometry constraint would become **meaningless** — the model would trivially satisfy it by training $F$ to predict anything $G$ produces.
- Net result: the architecture degrades to a **vanilla CycleGAN** with an extra (useless) auxiliary network.

**Quality-of-explanation marks (2):** Labelled diagram +1; clear mention of $F$ as "frozen segmentation oracle" +1.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
>
> *(a)* Explain the **motivation** for introducing geometry consistency in medical image synthesis. Why is cycle-consistency alone insufficient? Give **two clinical scenarios** where geometry inconsistency would be hazardous. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* Compare and contrast **CycleGAN, pix2pix, and GC-GAN** in a tabular form covering: data pairing requirement, geometry preservation, loss function, typical application, and hallucination risk. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Motivation (3 marks):**
- Different modalities (CT, MR, X-ray, PET) image the **same anatomy** but differ in appearance.
- Translation between modalities should change **appearance, not anatomy**.
- Pure pixel-level cycle-consistency assumes **one-to-one** mapping — medically invalid because a CT slice can plausibly map to many MR intensities for the same tissue.

**Why cycle-consistency alone is insufficient (2 marks):**
- Cycle-consistency forces the generator to be an approximate involution — too rigid.
- It does not **explicitly constrain anatomy**; structural errors propagate silently.
- It cannot leverage **available anatomical priors** (segmentation masks).

**Two clinical scenarios where geometry inconsistency is hazardous (2 marks):**
1. **MR-based radiotherapy planning** — synthesized CT with hallucinated bone or organ boundaries leads to **incorrect radiation dose calculation** and potential patient harm.
2. **Cross-modal tumor segmentation** — translating MR $\rightarrow$ CT with a "moved" tumor confuses downstream **lesion tracking and recurrence assessment**.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

| Criterion | **pix2pix** | **CycleGAN** | **GC-GAN** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data pairing | **Paired** (required) | **Unpaired** | **Unpaired** |
| Geometry preservation | Indirect (via pixel loss) | Implicit (via cycle) | **Explicit (via $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$)** |
| Core loss | L1 + Adversarial | Adv + Cycle + Identity | Adv + Cycle + Identity + **Geometry** |
| Requires masks | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| One-to-many mapping | ✗ | Limited | ✓ |
| Hallucination risk | Low (paired) | **Moderate–High** | **Low** |
| Typical application | Low-dose denoising | Style transfer | **Modality translation with anatomical safety** |
| Clinical suitability | Limited (pairing rare) | Moderate | **High** |

*(Award 1 mark per correctly addressed row; 1 mark for overall comparison conclusion.)*

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "frozen" status of $F$** — many students write $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$ but omit that $F$ is not trained. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Conflating CycleGAN and GC-GAN objectives** — writing a CycleGAN loss and labelling it GC-GAN. Always include the $\mathcal{L}_{\text{geo}}$ term explicitly. **(-3 marks)**
> 3. **Wrong loss weight defaults** — KTU expects $\lambda_{\text{cyc}} = 10$, not $1$. Memorize the canonical values. **(-1 mark)**
> 4. **Omitting the mask concat in the generator input** — the generator receives $[x \vert s_x]$, not just $x$. **(-1 mark)**
> 5. **Writing the discriminator loss in the wrong direction** — remember $\log(1 - D(\cdot))$ is the generator's minimization, while the discriminator minimizes $-\log D(\cdot)$. **(-2 marks)**
> 6. **Ignoring the symmetric $Y \rightarrow X$ direction** — the full loss must sum over **both** directions. **(-2 marks)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GC-GAN = CycleGAN + Geometry-Consistency Loss** (the only essential upgrade).
- **Segmentation masks are the anatomical blueprint**; the generator must respect them.
- **Segmentation network $F$ must be FROZEN** during training.
- **Generator input is $[x \vert s_x]$** — concatenation, not addition.
- **Total loss** = Adversarial + Cycle + Identity + Geometry, with weights $10, 0.5, 1{-}5$ respectively.
- **One-to-many mapping is allowed** — geometry constrains *where*, not *how it looks*.
- **Clinical use cases**: MR-only radiotherapy planning, modality imputation, cross-modal registration.
- **Hyperparameter defaults to memorize**: $\lambda_{\text{cyc}} = 10$, $\lambda_{\text{id}} = 0.5$, $\lambda_{\text{geo}} = 1{-}5$.
- **Evaluation metrics**: MAE, PSNR, SSIM (image fidelity) **+** Dice score on predicted mask (geometric fidelity).
- **Failure mode if $\lambda_{\text{geo}}$ is too high**: identity mapping (no modality change).
- **Failure mode if $\lambda_{\text{geo}} = 0$**: degrades to vanilla CycleGAN.
- **KTU favorite question pattern**: "Derive the total loss" + "Explain role of frozen $F$".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medical Applications of Image-to-Image Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT865 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Medical Applications of Image-to-Image Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Image-to-Image Translation (I2I)** in medical imaging is a class of deep generative learning problems where a model learns a parametric mapping function $\mathcal{G}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that transforms an input medical image from a source domain $\mathcal{X}$ (e.g., a T1-weighted MRI scan) into a corresponding output image in a target domain $\mathcal{Y}$ (e.g., a T2-weighted MRI scan or a CT scan), while preserving anatomical fidelity, structural correspondence, and clinically relevant diagnostic information.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBMT865), this topic is grounded in the broader theme of **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)** and **conditional image synthesis**, where the translation is guided by paired, unpaired, or partially paired training data. The medical applications emphasize **cross-modality synthesis**, **data augmentation**, **domain adaptation**, and **tumor/lesion synthesis** for downstream clinical tasks.

Mathematically, the translation objective is expressed as:

$$\hat{y} = \mathcal{G}_{\theta}(x), \quad x \in \mathcal{X}, \hat{y} \in \hat{\mathcal{Y}}$$

where $\theta$ represents the learnable parameters of the generator network, and the goal is to minimize a domain-specific loss:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} \; \mathcal{L}_{\text{I2I}}\big(\mathcal{G}_{\theta}(x), y\big)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Image-to-Image Translation is the cornerstone of **synthetic medical data generation**. The standard metrics a student must know are **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio) ≥ 28 dB**, **SSIM (Structural Similarity Index) ≥ 0.85**, and **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance) ≤ 50** for clinically acceptable synthesis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multilingual medical translator sitting between two radiologists** — one who only speaks the language of MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) and another who only speaks the language of CT (computed tomography). Both are looking at the exact same patient, but they use entirely different "vocabularies" (intensity scales, tissue contrast mechanisms, and noise profiles) to describe the anatomy.

A **medical image-to-image translation model** acts as this universal translator. You feed it an MRI of a patient's brain, and it "speaks out" a corresponding CT scan, preserving the anatomical geometry, the tumor shape, and the bone structures — even though the original patient was never scanned with a CT machine.

A simpler real-world analogy: think of it like a **photo filter app** on a smartphone. The app takes your daylight photo and instantly converts it to a night-vision image. The trees, people, and buildings remain in the exact same positions (anatomical consistency), but the *appearance* has changed completely (modality shift). Medical I2I works the same way, except the "filters" are learned from thousands of patient scans rather than programmed by hand.

**Key Insight:** The translation is **not** about changing anatomy — it is about changing the *imaging physics* that produces the visual representation of the anatomy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this critical in medicine?**
> 1. **MRI is slow and expensive** — synthesizing CT from MRI avoids patient radiation exposure.
> 2. **CT has poor soft-tissue contrast** — synthesizing MRI from CT avoids long scan times.
> 3. **Rare diseases have scarce data** — synthetic tumor generation augments training datasets.
> 4. **Multi-modal fusion** enables combined diagnostic insights from a single scan.

## 1.3 Classification of I2I Paradigms

| Paradigm | Data Requirement | Core Idea | Medical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Paired (Supervised)** | Perfectly aligned $(x_i, y_i)$ pairs | pix2pix, U-Net + cGAN | CT ↔ MRI translation |
| **Unpaired** | Two unrelated image sets $\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y}$ | CycleGAN, UNIT | Domain adaptation across hospitals |
| **One-to-Many** | One input → multiple plausible outputs | BicycleGAN, MUNIT | Tumor variability synthesis |
| **Many-to-One** | Multiple sources → unified target | StarGAN-v2 | Harmonizing multi-scanner data |
| **Diffusion-Based** | Iterative denoising | DDPM, Stable Diffusion variants | High-fidelity synthesis |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture: The pix2pix Framework (Foundational)

The **pix2pix** model (Isola et al., 2017) is the canonical supervised baseline for medical I2I. It combines a **U-Net generator** with a **PatchGAN discriminator** under a conditional GAN (cGAN) objective.

### 2.1.1 Generator $\mathcal{G}$: U-Net Backbone

The U-Net encoder–decoder preserves spatial information through **skip connections** between mirror-symmetric layers. For a medical input $x \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$:

1. **Encoding path**: successive Conv → BatchNorm → LeakyReLU layers downsample the image, capturing hierarchical features at scales $\frac{H}{2}, \frac{H}{4}, \frac{H}{8}, \ldots, \frac{H}{2^k}$.
2. **Bottleneck**: the deepest feature map represents the most abstract semantic representation.
3. **Decoding path**: successive Transposed-Conv → BatchNorm → ReLU layers upsample the feature map.
4. **Skip connections**: at each decoding level $k$, the encoder feature map $\mathcal{E}_k$ is concatenated with the decoder feature map $\mathcal{D}_k$:

$$\mathcal{D}_k = \text{UpConv}\big(\mathcal{D}_{k+1}\big) \oplus \mathcal{E}_k$$

where $\oplus$ denotes channel-wise concatenation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why skip connections in medical I2I?** Anatomical structures (organ boundaries, tumor edges) are *fine-grained, high-frequency details*. Without skip connections, the encoder–decoder would lose these — producing blurry, anatomically inconsistent outputs. Skip connections are the *single most important architectural choice* for preserving anatomy.

### 2.1.2 Discriminator $\mathcal{D}$: PatchGAN

Unlike classical GANs that output a single real/fake scalar, **PatchGAN** classifies every $N \times N$ patch of the image as real or fake. The final output is a feature map of realism scores:

$$D(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^{\frac{H}{N} \times \frac{W}{N}}$$

This forces the generator to match high-frequency local structure — which is essential in medical imaging where texture, edges, and tissue interfaces carry diagnostic value.

### 2.1.3 The pix2pix Loss Function

The total objective combines a conditional adversarial loss with an $L_1$ reconstruction loss:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{pix2pix}}(\mathcal{G}, \mathcal{D}) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{x,y}\left[\log \mathcal{D}(x, y)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{x}\left[\log\big(1 - \mathcal{D}(x, \mathcal{G}(x))\big)\right]}_{\text{Adversarial Loss } \mathcal{L}_{\text{cGAN}}} + \lambda \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{x,y}\left[\|y - \mathcal{G}(x)\|_1\right]}_{\text{Reconstruction Loss } \mathcal{L}_{L_1}}$$

- The **adversarial term** pushes $\mathcal{G}$ to produce realistic-looking images in the target modality.
- The **$L_1$ term** enforces pixel-wise fidelity to the ground truth (in paired settings).
- The hyperparameter $\lambda$ (typically $\lambda = 100$) balances realism vs. fidelity.

## 2.2 Unpaired Translation: CycleGAN for Medical Domain Adaptation

When paired $(x, y)$ data is unavailable (e.g., MRI from Scanner A and CT from Scanner B on *different patients*), we use **CycleGAN** (Zhu et al., 2017). It learns two mappings:

$$\mathcal{G}_{XY}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y} \quad \text{and} \quad \mathcal{G}_{YX}: \mathcal{Y} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$$

The **cycle-consistency loss** enforces that translating $x \to y$ and then $y \to x$ should return the original:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}, \mathcal{G}_{YX}) = \mathbb{E}_{x}\big[\|\mathcal{G}_{YX}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}(x)) - x\|_1\big] + \mathbb{E}_{y}\big[\|\mathcal{G}_{XY}(\mathcal{G}_{YX}(y)) - y\|_1\big]$$

The total CycleGAN objective is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CycleGAN}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}, \mathcal{D}_Y) + \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(\mathcal{G}_{YX}, \mathcal{D}_X) + \lambda_{\text{cyc}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$$

where $\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$ is the **identity loss** that preserves color/intensity composition when the input is already in the target domain.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in Part A, Part B, and university exam questions.**

| # | Formula | Name | Purpose | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{L^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ | Peak SNR | Pixel fidelity | $L$ = max intensity (e.g., 255); dB |
| 2 | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error | Pixel error | $(y_i - \hat{y}_i)$ in intensity |
| 3 | $\text{SSIM}(y, \hat{y}) = \dfrac{(2\mu_y \mu_{\hat{y}} + c_1)(2\sigma_{y\hat{y}} + c_2)}{(\mu_y^2 + \mu_{\hat{y}}^2 + c_1)(\sigma_y^2 + \sigma_{\hat{y}}^2 + c_2)}$ | Structural Similarity | Structural fidelity | $c_1 = (0.01 L)^2$, $c_2 = (0.03 L)^2$ |
| 4 | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Mean Absolute Error | Robust pixel error | — |
| 5 | $\mathcal{L}_{L_1} = \|y - \mathcal{G}(x)\|_1$ | L1 Reconstruction | Preserves sharpness | Used in pix2pix |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} = \|\mathcal{G}_{YX}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}(x)) - x\|_1$ | Cycle Consistency | Enforces invertibility | Used in CycleGAN |
| 7 | $\text{FID} = \|\mu_r - \mu_g\|^2 + \text{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | Fréchet Inception Distance | Distribution-level realism | Lower is better |
| 8 | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{percep}} = \sum_{l}\|\phi_l(y) - \phi_l(\mathcal{G}(x))\|_1$ | Perceptual Loss | High-level feature match | $\phi_l$ = VGG layer $l$ |
| 9 | $\text{NMSE} = \dfrac{\|y - \hat{y}\|_2^2}{\|y\|_2^2}$ | Normalized MSE | Scale-invariant fidelity | Used in MRI synthesis |
| 10 | $\text{HU}_{\text{MAE}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert \text{HU}_i^{\text{GT}} - \text{HU}_i^{\text{syn}} \vert$ | HU Error (CT) | CT intensity fidelity | Hounsfield Units; target ≤ 50 HU |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Radiation-Free CT Synthesis from MRI** — Used in MRI-only radiotherapy planning; eliminates the need for a separate planning CT, reducing patient radiation dose by **~3–5 mSv per course**.
2. **Cross-Scanner Harmonization** — Pooling data from GE, Siemens, and Philips scanners for AI training, despite vendor-specific intensity variations.
3. **Rare Disease Augmentation** — Synthesizing hundreds of pseudo-scans of pediatric brain tumors where real cases are limited.
4. **MR Motion Correction** — Synthesizing a "clean" MRI from a motion-corrupted acquisition.
5. **Modality-Invariant Segmentation Pre-training** — Training a tumor segmentation model on synthetic multi-modal data so it generalizes to unseen modalities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $L_1$ (not $L_2$) is Used in pix2pix

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let the ground truth target image be $y \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W}$ and the generated image be $\hat{y} = \mathcal{G}(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W}$. We wish to choose between $L_1$ and $L_2$ reconstruction losses.

### 3.1.2 L2 Loss Analysis

The pixel-wise squared error is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{L_2}(y, \hat{y}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

Taking the gradient with respect to $\hat{y}_i$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{L_2}}{\partial \hat{y}_i} = -\frac{2}{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)$$

The gradient magnitude grows **linearly with the error**. For a large pixel error $\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$, the gradient is large, causing the optimizer to **over-penalize outliers**.

In medical imaging, anatomical boundaries (e.g., tumor edges) are sparse but high-frequency. A few misclassified boundary pixels under $L_2$ dominate the loss, forcing the generator to **over-smooth** — the canonical "blurry output" problem.

### 3.1.3 L1 Loss Analysis

The pixel-wise absolute error is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{L_1}(y, \hat{y}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

Taking the gradient (with subgradient for the absolute value):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{L_1}}{\partial \hat{y}_i} = -\frac{1}{N}\text{sign}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)$$

The gradient is a **constant-magnitude** $\pm \frac{1}{N}$ vector, **independent of the error magnitude**. This means:

- Small errors (high-frequency boundary pixels) and large errors (low-frequency smooth regions) receive **equal gradient pressure**.
- The generator is not biased toward either over-smoothing or over-sharpening.

### 3.1.4 Conclusion

Since medical images contain *both* smooth tissue regions *and* sharp anatomical boundaries, $L_1$ is the empirically and analytically superior choice for medical I2I:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}_{L_1} \text{ is preferred over } \mathcal{L}_{L_2} \text{ for medical I2I to avoid over-smoothing}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Cycle Consistency in CycleGAN

### 3.2.1 Setup

We have two unpaired image sets $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_M\}$ and $\mathcal{Y} = \{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_N\}$ from two medical modalities (e.g., MRI and CT). We seek mappings $\mathcal{G}_{XY}$ and $\mathcal{G}_{YX}$ such that:

$$\mathcal{G}_{XY}: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}, \quad \mathcal{G}_{YX}: \mathcal{Y} \to \mathcal{X}$$

### 3.2.2 The Uniqueness Problem

Without any constraint, an infinite family of mappings $F: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ can match the marginal distributions $p(\mathcal{Y})$. For example, $F$ could be a constant map $F(x) = y_1$ for all $x$ — the discriminator would still see a plausible CT distribution.

### 3.2.3 Cycle-Consistency as an Inverse Constraint

Cycle consistency is the assumption:

$$\mathcal{G}_{YX}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}(x)) \approx x, \quad \forall x \in \mathcal{X}$$

This means $\mathcal{G}_{XY}$ must be *approximately invertible*. Algebraically, the cycle can be expressed as:

$$\mathcal{G}_{YX} \circ \mathcal{G}_{XY} \approx \text{Id}_{\mathcal{X}}$$

$$\mathcal{G}_{XY} \circ \mathcal{G}_{YX} \approx \text{Id}_{\mathcal{Y}}$$

### 3.2.4 The Cycle Loss Formulation

Encoding this as an $L_1$ penalty for both directions:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}, \mathcal{G}_{YX}) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}(\mathcal{X})}\big[\| \mathcal{G}_{YX}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}(x)) - x \|_1 \big] + \mathbb{E}_{y \sim p_{\text{data}}(\mathcal{Y})}\big[\| \mathcal{G}_{XY}(\mathcal{G}_{YX}(y)) - y \|_1 \big]$$

The first term penalizes the forward-backward cycle, and the second penalizes the backward-forward cycle.

### 3.2.5 Why This Works in Medical Imaging

In CT↔MRI translation, the *anatomy* (skull shape, tumor location, organ geometry) is preserved across modalities. The cycle loss forces $\mathcal{G}_{XY}$ and $\mathcal{G}_{YX}$ to encode and decode the anatomical skeleton, while the adversarial losses handle the modality-specific intensity mapping.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Medical I2I Method Comparison

| Method | Modality Pair | Paired? | Loss Functions | Key Advantage | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **pix2pix** | T1 MRI → T2 MRI | Yes | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cGAN}} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{L_1}$ | High pixel fidelity | Requires perfect registration |
| **CycleGAN** | CT → MRI (unpaired) | No | $2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}} + \lambda_{\text{cyc}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$ | No paired data | Mode collapse risk |
| **UNIT** | Multi-scanner | No | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{VAE}}$ | Shared latent space | Complex training |
| **MUNIT** | One MRI → many CTs | No | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{style}}$ | Multi-modal output | Style ambiguity |
| **BicycleGAN** | Low-dose CT → normal CT | Yes | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cGAN}} + \mathcal{L}_{L_1} + \mathcal{L}_{\text{KL}}$ | Stochastic output | Slower inference |
| **DDPM-based** | MRI → CT (diffusion) | Optional | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE on noise}}$ | High realism | Slow sampling (~50–1000 steps) |
| **nnU-Net (synthesis-free baseline)** | — | — | Dice + CE | No synthesis needed | Limited modality generalization |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: PSNR & SSIM Calculation

**Given**:
- Ground truth CT patch $y = [100, 120, 150, 80, 200]$ (in Hounsfield Units, scaled to $[0, 255]$)
- Synthesized CT patch $\hat{y} = [105, 115, 148, 82, 195]$
- Image size $N = 5$, $L = 255$

**Step 1: Compute MSE**

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

Element-wise errors: $5, 5, 2, 2, 5$. Squared: $25, 25, 4, 4, 25$. Sum: $83$.

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{83}{5} = 16.6$$

**Step 2: Compute PSNR**

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^2}{16.6}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(3917.77) = 10 \times 3.593 = 35.93 \text{ dB}$$

Since PSNR > 30 dB, the synthesis is of **clinically acceptable pixel fidelity**.

**Step 3: Compute SSIM** (using $\mu_y = 130$, $\mu_{\hat{y}} = 129$, $\sigma_y^2 = 1880$, $\sigma_{\hat{y}}^2 = 1700.4$, $\sigma_{y\hat{y}} = 1746$, $c_1 = 6.5025$, $c_2 = 58.5225$):

$$\text{SSIM} = \frac{(2 \cdot 130 \cdot 129 + 6.5025)(2 \cdot 1746 + 58.5225)}{(130^2 + 129^2 + 6.5025)(1880 + 1700.4 + 58.5225)}$$

$$\text{SSIM} = \frac{(33540 + 6.5025)(3492 + 58.5225)}{(16900 + 16641 + 6.5025)(3638.9225)}$$

$$\text{SSIM} = \frac{(33546.5025)(3550.5225)}{(33547.5025)(3638.9225)} = \frac{1.1914 \times 10^8}{1.2208 \times 10^8} \approx 0.976$$

Since SSIM > 0.9, the **structural fidelity is excellent** — the synthesized patch is a faithful representation of the ground truth.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 pix2pix Architecture for CT ↔ MRI Translation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input MRI Slice X] --> B[U-Net Encoder E1]
    B --> C[Encoder E2 Down Sample]
    C --> D[Encoder E3 Down Sample]
    D --> E[Encoder E4 Bottleneck]
    E --> F[Decoder D3 Up Sample]
    F --> G[Decoder D2 Up Sample]
    G --> H[Decoder D1 Up Sample]
    D3SK[Skip E3] --> F
    D2SK[Skip E2] --> G
    D1SK[Skip E1] --> H
    H --> I[Output Synthetic CT Y]
    I --> J[PatchGAN Discriminator D]
    A --> J
    J --> K[Realism Score Map NxN]
    I --> L[L1 Loss vs Ground Truth CT]
    K --> M[Adversarial Loss]
    L --> N[Total Generator Loss]
    M --> N
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **skip connections** (D1SK, D2SK, D3SK) preserve anatomical detail. The **PatchGAN** judges realism locally. The **$L_1$ loss** enforces pixel-wise fidelity. This is the **canonical medical I2I pipeline**.

## 4.2 CycleGAN Architecture for Unpaired Cross-Modality Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Domain_MRI
        X[MRI Image X]
    end
    subgraph Domain_CT
        Y[CT Image Y]
    end
    X --> GXY[Generator GXY MRI to CT]
    GXY --> YFAKE[Synthetic CT Y']
    Y --> GYX[Generator GYX CT to MRI]
    GYX --> XFAKE[Synthetic MRI X']
    YFAKE --> GYX
    GYX --> XBACK[Reconstructed MRI X Back]
    XFAKE --> GXY
    GXY --> YBACK[Reconstructed CT Y Back]
    YFAKE --> DY[Discriminator DY for CT]
    XFAKE --> DX[Discriminator DX for MRI]
    X --> DX
    Y --> DY
    XBACK --> L1A[Forward Cycle Loss]
    YBACK --> L1B[Backward Cycle Loss]
    DY --> LGAN1[Adversarial Loss GAN1]
    DX --> LGAN2[Adversarial Loss GAN2]
    L1A --> LTOT[Total Loss]
    L1B --> LTOT
    LGAN1 --> LTOT
    LGAN2 --> LTOT
```

## 4.3 Medical Application Block Diagram: End-to-End Clinical Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Patient Receives MRI Scan Only] --> B[Preprocess Slice Resize 256x256 Normalize Intensity]
    B --> C[Trained pix2pix Generator]
    C --> D[Synthetic CT Image]
    D --> E[Dose Calculation Engine]
    E --> F[Radiotherapy Treatment Plan]
    F --> G[Plan Verification by Oncologist]
    G --> H[Approved for Treatment]
    H --> I[No Radiation from Planning CT]
    I --> J[Patient Safety Improved]
    D --> K[Archive in PACS System]
    K --> L[Future AI Training Data Augmentation]
```

## 4.4 Tumor Synthesis Pipeline for Data Augmentation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Real Brain MRI with Tumor] --> B[Segment Tumor Mask using U-Net]
    B --> C[Extract Tumor Shape and Location]
    C --> D[Random Affine Transform Rotation Scaling]
    D --> E[Insert Synthetic Tumor into Healthy MRI]
    E --> F[Style Transfer Network CycleGAN]
    F --> G[Generate Diverse Synthetic Tumor MRIs]
    G --> H[Augment Original Dataset 10x to 100x]
    H --> I[Train Downstream Classifier]
    I --> J[Improved Tumor Detection Accuracy]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define image-to-image translation in the context of medical imaging. Differentiate between paired and unpaired translation paradigms with one medical example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Image-to-image translation (I2I) is a deep learning task that learns a parametric mapping $\mathcal{G}_{\theta}: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ between two medical image domains, transforming an input image from one modality (e.g., MRI) into a corresponding output in another modality (e.g., CT) while preserving anatomical structure.

**Paired Translation** requires perfectly aligned source-target image pairs $(x_i, y_i)$, typically obtained from the same patient scanned in both modalities. **Example:** pix2pix-based T1-weighted MRI to T2-weighted MRI translation using registered slices.

**Unpaired Translation** does not require direct correspondence; it uses two unrelated image sets and learns the mapping through cycle consistency. **Example:** CycleGAN-based cross-scanner harmonization of MRIs from GE and Siemens scanners without requiring the same patients on both.

*(1 mark each for definition, paired explanation with example, unpaired explanation with example = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: Metric and Loss `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is the $L_1$ reconstruction loss preferred over the $L_2$ (MSE) loss in medical image-to-image translation? Mention one evaluation metric used to assess the generated images.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The $L_1$ loss $\mathcal{L}_{L_1} = \|y - \mathcal{G}(x)\|_1$ produces a constant-magnitude gradient via the sign function, making it **equally sensitive to all error magnitudes**. In contrast, the $L_2$ loss produces gradients proportional to the error magnitude, which causes the generator to **over-smooth sharp anatomical boundaries** (e.g., tumor edges) by disproportionately penalizing large errors in smooth regions. Since medical images contain *both* smooth tissues and sharp boundaries, $L_1$ yields sharper, more diagnostically faithful synthesis.

A standard evaluation metric is the **Structural Similarity Index (SSIM)**, which captures luminance, contrast, and structural fidelity between the synthesized and ground truth images, with values closer to **1.0** indicating superior quality.

*(2 marks for $L_1$ vs $L_2$ reasoning, 1 mark for metric = 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Comprehensive I2I for Cross-Modality Synthesis `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture and loss function of the **pix2pix** model in detail. Show how it is applied to the medical problem of CT synthesis from MRI.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **CycleGAN** framework. Derive the cycle-consistency loss and explain its necessity in unpaired medical I2I with a real clinical example.

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Architecture:** pix2pix consists of two networks: a **U-Net generator** $\mathcal{G}$ and a **PatchGAN discriminator** $\mathcal{D}$.

The **U-Net generator** has an encoder–decoder structure. The encoder path downsamples the input MRI through successive convolutional layers, halving spatial resolution at each level. The decoder path upsamples through transposed convolutions. Crucially, **skip connections** concatenate encoder feature maps with corresponding decoder feature maps at mirror-symmetric depths, preserving high-frequency anatomical details like tumor boundaries and organ edges.

The **PatchGAN discriminator** outputs a realism score for every $70 \times 70$ patch of the image, rather than a single global score. This forces the generator to match local texture statistics — essential for medical imaging where fine texture carries diagnostic value.

**Loss Function:** The total objective is:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{pix2pix}}(\mathcal{G}, \mathcal{D}) = \mathbb{E}_{x,y}\big[\log \mathcal{D}(x, y)\big] + \mathbb{E}_{x}\big[\log(1 - \mathcal{D}(x, \mathcal{G}(x)))\big] + \lambda \mathbb{E}_{x,y}\big[\|y - \mathcal{G}(x)\|_1\big]$$

- The first two terms are the **conditional adversarial loss**, pushing the generator to produce realistic CT-style images.
- The third term is the **$L_1$ reconstruction loss**, enforcing pixel-wise fidelity to the ground truth CT.
- $\lambda = 100$ is the standard weighting.

**Application to CT Synthesis from MRI:** Given a paired dataset of $(x_i, y_i)$ where $x_i$ is an MRI slice and $y_i$ is the corresponding registered CT slice of the same patient, pix2pix learns $\mathcal{G}_{\theta}$ that maps MRI intensities to CT Hounsfield Units. After training, the generator can synthesize a CT from a new patient's MRI, enabling **MRI-only radiotherapy planning** without subjecting the patient to a planning CT scan.

*(Distribution: 3 marks architecture, 3 marks loss equation with explanation, 1 mark clinical application = 7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Framework:** CycleGAN learns two generators $\mathcal{G}_{XY}: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ and $\mathcal{G}_{YX}: \mathcal{Y} \to \mathcal{X}$, and two discriminators $\mathcal{D}_X$ and $\mathcal{D}_Y$, one per domain.

**Derivation of Cycle-Consistency Loss:**

Without any constraint, infinitely many mappings could fool the discriminators. To enforce *invertibility*, we require:

$$\mathcal{G}_{YX}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}(x)) \approx x, \quad \forall x \in \mathcal{X}$$

$$\mathcal{G}_{XY}(\mathcal{G}_{YX}(y)) \approx y, \quad \forall y \in \mathcal{Y}$$

Encoding this as an $L_1$ penalty:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}, \mathcal{G}_{YX}) = \mathbb{E}_{x}\big[\|\mathcal{G}_{YX}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}(x)) - x\|_1\big] + \mathbb{E}_{y}\big[\|\mathcal{G}_{XY}(\mathcal{G}_{YX}(y)) - y\|_1\big]$$

The total CycleGAN objective combines adversarial losses on both domains with the cycle loss:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(\mathcal{G}_{XY}, \mathcal{D}_Y) + \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}}(\mathcal{G}_{YX}, \mathcal{D}_X) + \lambda_{\text{cyc}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$$

**Why It is Necessary:** In many clinical scenarios, paired data is impossible. For example, in **cross-scanner harmonization**, a hospital may have 10,000 MRIs from a Siemens scanner and 8,000 MRIs from a GE scanner, but only 50 patients scanned on both. CycleGAN uses the unpaired datasets to learn a domain mapping that harmonizes intensity distributions, allowing AI models trained on combined data to perform consistently.

**Clinical Example:** Harmonizing brain MRIs from multiple hospitals for a multi-center Alzheimer's detection study, where each scanner has unique intensity bias.

*(Distribution: 2 marks framework, 2 marks loss derivation, 2 marks necessity, 1 mark clinical example = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B: Loss Functions, Metrics, and Clinical Application `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the PSNR and SSIM formulas used to evaluate medical I2I synthesis. Explain the meaning of each term in the SSIM equation.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the role of image-to-image translation in **data augmentation for rare disease detection**. Describe the tumor synthesis pipeline with a suitable architecture.

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**PSNR Derivation:**

The Mean Squared Error between ground truth $y$ and synthesized $\hat{y}$ of size $N$ pixels is:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

PSNR measures the ratio of peak signal power $L^2$ (where $L$ is the maximum intensity, e.g., 255 for 8-bit or 4095 for 12-bit DICOM) to the noise power MSE:

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{L^2}{\text{MSE}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Interpretation:** PSNR > 30 dB indicates clinically acceptable pixel fidelity.

**SSIM Derivation:**

SSIM compares three components — luminance, contrast, and structure — between two local image windows:

$$\text{SSIM}(y, \hat{y}) = \frac{(2\mu_y \mu_{\hat{y}} + c_1)(2\sigma_{y\hat{y}} + c_2)}{(\mu_y^2 + \mu_{\hat{y}}^2 + c_1)(\sigma_y^2 + \sigma_{\hat{y}}^2 + c_2)}$$

**Meaning of Each Term:**
- $\mu_y, \mu_{\hat{y}}$: mean intensities of the two windows (luminance comparison)
- $\sigma_y^2, \sigma_{\hat{y}}^2$: variances of the two windows (contrast comparison)
- $\sigma_{y\hat{y}}$: covariance between the two windows (structural comparison)
- $c_1 = (0.01 L)^2$, $c_2 = (0.03 L)^2$: stabilization constants to prevent division by zero

SSIM ranges in $[-1, 1]$, with $1$ indicating perfect structural match. SSIM > 0.85 is the clinical benchmark for medical I2I.

*(Distribution: 2 marks PSNR, 3 marks SSIM derivation, 2 marks term explanation = 7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Role in Data Augmentation:** Rare diseases (e.g., pediatric medulloblastoma, atypical meningiomas) have very few real imaging samples — often fewer than 50 cases per institution. Deep learning models trained on such small datasets severely overfit and fail to generalize. I2I translation enables **synthetic data augmentation** by generating plausible variations of the limited real cases, expanding the training set 10× to 100×.

**Tumor Synthesis Pipeline:**

1. **Source Mask Extraction:** A pre-trained U-Net segments the tumor from the available real MRI scans, producing binary masks.
2. **Mask Augmentation:** Apply random affine transformations (rotation by $\theta \in [-30°, 30°]$, scaling $s \in [0.8, 1.2]$, translation $t \in [-20, 20]$ pixels) to create anatomically diverse tumor placements.
3. **Style-Content Disentanglement (MUNIT/BicycleGAN):** A multi-modal generator disentangles the tumor's *content* (shape, location) from its *style* (intensity, texture, surrounding edema), allowing the same tumor shape to be rendered in many intensity variants.
4. **Realistic Blending:** The synthesized tumor is blended into a healthy MRI using Poisson image editing or a learned fusion network, ensuring the boundary gradients are smooth and consistent with surrounding tissue.
5. **Quality Control:** A downstream classifier (trained on real data) is evaluated on the synthetic data; if accuracy drops significantly, the synthesis is rejected.

This pipeline is used in **BRATS-style brain tumor challenges** to augment training data and improve segmentation Dice scores by **3–7%**.

*(Distribution: 2 marks augmentation rationale, 4 marks pipeline steps, 1 mark outcome metric = 7 marks)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Medical I2I Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $L_1$ and $L_2$ reasoning** — Students often write "$L_2$ is better because it punishes large errors more" without realizing this is *exactly why* it fails in medical imaging (over-smoothing). Always explain the constant-gradient property of $L_1$.
> 2. **Forgetting clinical context** — The KTU examiner awards marks for **clinical relevance** (radiation reduction, rare disease data, multi-center harmonization). Pure technical answers without clinical anchoring lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the discriminator in pix2pix** — Many students describe only the U-Net generator and forget PatchGAN. The discriminator is *essential* — it forces realistic texture synthesis.
> 4. **Writing $L_1 = |y - \hat{y}|$** without specifying it is summed over all $N$ pixels and normalized — vague notation loses credit.
> 5. **Mixing up PSNR thresholds** — PSNR > 30 dB is acceptable, > 35 dB is good, > 40 dB is excellent. Be specific.
> 6. **Omitting the identity loss in CycleGAN** — The $\mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$ term is a sub-part of the total CycleGAN objective; omitting it loses marks on derivation questions.
> 7. **Forgetting skip connections' role** — In U-Net questions, always state: "skip connections preserve high-frequency anatomical detail that would otherwise be lost in the bottleneck."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Medical Image-to-Image Translation**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ I2I is a generative mapping $\mathcal{G}_{\theta}: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ between medical image domains.
- ✅ **Paired I2I** (pix2pix) requires registered $(x, y)$ image pairs.
- ✅ **Unpaired I2I** (CycleGAN) uses cycle consistency to learn mappings without correspondence.
- ✅ **Skip connections** in U-Net preserve high-frequency anatomical detail — *critical for medical I2I*.

### Loss Functions
- ✅ **pix2pix loss** = $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cGAN}} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{L_1}$ with $\lambda = 100$.
- ✅ **CycleGAN loss** = $2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{GAN}} + \lambda_{\text{cyc}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} + \lambda_{\text{id}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{id}}$.
- ✅ **$L_1$ preferred over $L_2$** — constant gradient avoids over-smoothing of boundaries.

### Evaluation Metrics
- ✅ **PSNR > 30 dB** = clinically acceptable pixel fidelity.
- ✅ **SSIM > 0.85** = structurally faithful synthesis.
- ✅ **FID < 50** = realistic distribution-level synthesis.
- ✅ **MAE in HU < 50** = acceptable CT Hounsfield Unit error.

### Medical Applications
- ✅ **CT ↔ MRI synthesis** for radiation-free radiotherapy planning.
- ✅ **Cross-scanner harmonization** for multi-center AI studies.
- ✅ **Rare disease data augmentation** via tumor synthesis.
- ✅ **Domain adaptation** for unseen hospital data.

### Key Architectural Components
- ✅ **U-Net generator** = encoder–decoder + skip connections.
- ✅ **PatchGAN discriminator** = local $N \times N$ realism scoring.
- ✅ **Cycle consistency** = invertibility constraint for unpaired translation.
- ✅ **Identity loss** = color/intensity preservation.

### Key Takeaway
> **"Medical Image-to-Image Translation is the bridge that connects imaging modalities, scanners, and data scarcity — enabling AI to learn from data that does not yet exist, while preserving the patient's anatomy pixel-by-pixel."**
